text: **The Xuan Guai Lu by Niu Sengru of the Tang Dynasty** Volume One ○ Du Zichun Du Zichun was a man from the Zhou and Sui dynasties. In his youth, he was dissolute and neglected his family affairs, yet he had a relaxed and unrestrained temperament, enjoying alcohol and wandering in morally questionable ways. His assets were depleted, and he turned to relatives and friends for help, but they all abandoned him due to his lack of diligence. In the winter, he walked through Chang'an in tattered clothes, empty-handed, and without food, wandering aimlessly and unsure of where to go. At the West Gate of the East Market, his face bore the visible signs of extreme hunger and cold, and he looked up to the sky and sighed deeply. An old man came forward with a walking stick and asked, "Why are you sighing, sir?" Du Zichun explained his situation and expressed his frustration at the indifference of his relatives. His feelings of indignation were evident on his face. The old man asked, "How much would be enough for a comfortable life?" Du Zichun replied, "Thirty to fifty thousand coins would be sufficient." The old man said, "Not enough, tell me more." "One hundred thousand," Du Zichun said. "Not enough," the old man replied. "One million," Du Zichun said. "Not enough," the old man said. "Three million," Du Zichun said. "That will do," the old man replied. He then took out a string of coins from his sleeve and said, "Here is some money for tonight. Meet me at the Persian inn in the West Market tomorrow at noon. Be careful not to be late." When the time came, Du Zichun went to the inn, and the old man indeed gave him three million coins without revealing his name and then left.

After becoming wealthy, Zi Chun's desire to indulge in extravagance flared up once more. He believed he would never again be a wanderer, and so he rode in luxurious carriages and wore fine clothes, gathering with drinking companions and hiring musicians and dancers from brothels. He no longer cared about making a living. Within one or two years, his wealth was gradually spent. His clothes and vehicles, once luxurious, became cheap, and he went from riding horses to riding donkeys and then to walking, suddenly returning to his original state. Soon after, he was at a loss again and lamented by the city gate. As soon as he spoke, an old man appeared, grasped his hand and said, "You are back to this, how extraordinary! I will help you again, but how many strings of coins are enough?" Zi Chun was ashamed and could not respond, and the old man pressed him further, to which Zi Chun could only apologize in embarrassment. The old man said, "Come to the same place tomorrow at noon." Reluctantly, Zi Chun went, and received one million coins. At first, he was determined to use the money to make a living, thinking that figures like Shi Chong and Yi Dun were insignificant. However, as soon as the money was in his hands, his heart turned again, and his desire for pleasure returned to its former state. Within three or four years, he was poorer than before.

He encountered the old man again at the same place, and Zi Chun could not bear his shame, covering his face and running away. The old man pulled his sleeve to stop him, saying, "Alas! It was a poor plan." Then he gave him three million, saying, "If this does not heal, then you will be poor beyond help." Zi Chun said, "I am a wandering, disreputable person, and my life's resources are exhausted. Neither relatives nor wealthy families care for me. Only this old man has given me three times. How can I repay such kindness?" He then told the old man, "With this, I can establish myself in the world, provide for widows and orphans, and restore my reputation in the teachings. Grateful for the old man's deep kindness, after I have established myself, I will follow your commands." The old man said, "It is my heart. When you have finished managing your affairs, next year during the Zhongyuan Festival, meet me under the two cypress trees of Laojun." Zi Chun, knowing that many widows and orphans lived in Huainan, transferred his resources to Yangzhou, bought a hundred acres of good land, built a grand mansion in the city, and set up over a hundred houses on main roads. He summoned all the widows and orphans and divided them among the houses, married off his nephews and nieces, arranged the burial of the deceased, and treated those who were kind to him with warmth and those who were hostile with forgiveness. After completing all these tasks, he went to meet the old man as agreed.

The old man was chanting in the shade of two cypress trees, and then he ascended with him to the Yuntai Peak of Mount Hua. After traveling more than forty miles, they came upon a dwelling, the rooms and houses of which were clean and tidy, not inhabited by ordinary people. Colorful clouds covered from afar, and lunatic phoenixes and cranes were flying. Above was a main hall, and in the middle was a furnace, nine feet high, emitting purple flames that shone brightly, illuminating the windows. Nine jade maidens stood around the furnace, and the green dragon and white tiger stood respectively in front and behind. By then, it was evening, and the old man no longer wore secular clothing; instead, he was a scholar with a yellow crown and a red cape. He held three white stones and a cup of wine and gave them to Zichun, instructing him to eat them quickly. He then took a tiger skin and laid it on the west wall inside, facing east, and warned, "Be cautious not to speak. Even revered deities, evil spirits, yakshas, fierce beasts, and hells, and even if your relatives are bound by them, all kinds of suffering are not real. Just remain still and silent. Be at ease and do not fear; ultimately there will be no suffering. Concentrate on what I have said." After speaking, he left. Zichun looked at the courtyard and saw only a large jar, full of water.

The Taoist had just departed when flags, spears, and armor, a thousand chariots and ten thousand horses, filled the ravines and valleys, advancing with shouts and commands that shook the heavens. A man called himself the Great General, over ten feet tall, with both men and horses clad in golden armor, shining brightly. Hundreds of personal guards drew their swords and stringed their bows, entering the hall directly and shouting, "Who are you, and dare you not avoid the Great General!" The attendants drew their swords and advanced, forcing the question of names and what the individual was doing, but all answered not. The questioner grew furious, urging execution and shooting arrows, the sound like thunder, yet still no response. The general left in anger. Soon, thousands of tigers, venomous dragons, lion-like beasts, lions, and vipers roared, snarling, and clawing, rushing forward, wishing to seize and devour, or leaping over them. Zi Chun remained unperturbed. After a while, they dispersed. Then, heavy rain poured down, thunder and lightning darkened the sky, fire wheels moved around them, and lightning flashed before and behind them, blinding their eyes. In an instant, the water in the courtyard was over ten feet deep, flowing lightning and roaring thunder, the force like mountains and rivers being torn apart, unstoppable. In an instant, the waves reached under their feet. Zi Chun sat calmly without looking up. After a short while, it dispersed.

The general returned, leading a bull-headed jailer with an appearance so bizarre and supernatural that it seemed like a ghost. He placed a large cauldron of boiling water in front of Zi Chun, surrounded by long spears and forks. He commanded, "If you reveal your name, you will be set free. If you refuse, we will impale you and cast you into the cauldron." Zi Chun remained silent. The general then dragged Zi Chun's wife down the steps and pointed at her, saying, "Speak your name to save yourself." Still, she did not respond. The general ordered whips and beatings, causing blood to flow, or shooting and chopping, or boiling and burning, all forms of torture that were unbearable. The wife wept and cried, saying, "Truly, I am unworthy and clumsy, a disgrace to a gentleman. Fortunately, I have served you for many years. Now I am held by a powerful spirit, unable to bear the suffering. I dare not hope that you will crawl and beg, but I hope for a word from you to save my life. Who among people has no feelings? You are willing to spare just one word." She wept tears in the courtyard, cursing and scolding, but Zi Chun remained unmoved. The general said, "I cannot harm your wife?" and ordered to fetch a millstone, cutting her from the feet upwards. She cried more desperately. The general said, "This demon's magic is complete. Cannot allow him to stay in the world for long," and ordered the attendants to behead him.

After the execution, the soul was led to the King of Hell, who asked, "Is this the evil person from Yuntai Peak?" The soul was then sent to prison, where it endured all manner of tortures: molten copper, iron rods, pestles and mortars, fire pits, boiling cauldrons, knife mountains, and sword forests. Despite these torments, the soul remembered the words of the Taoist and found them somewhat bearable, never groaning even once. When the jailer reported that the soul had finished its punishment, the King of Hell said, "This person is evil and treacherous; it is not fitting for them to be reborn as a man. Let them be reborn as a woman." The soul was reborn into the family of Wang Qin, the county magistrate of Shanfu, Song Prefecture. She was born with many illnesses, enduring the pain of acupuncture and medicine without respite. She also fell into fires and fell off beds, suffering greatly but never crying out. As she grew up, she was beautiful beyond compare, yet she remained mute. Her family called her a deaf-mute girl, and relatives and friends mocked and teased her in countless ways, but she could not respond.

A Fellow Village Scholar Named Lu Gui There was a scholar named Lu Gui from the same village who, upon hearing of her beauty and virtue, admired her greatly and sought her hand through a matchmaker. Her family declined because she was mute. Lu said, "If she is a good wife, why does it matter if she cannot speak? It is also sufficient to warn against the gossipy women." Thus, he accepted her. Lu prepared the necessary rituals and married her. After several years, they were deeply affectionate, and she gave birth to a son who was only two years old but incredibly intelligent. When Lu held the child and spoke to him, the child did not respond. Despite many attempts to engage him, the child remained silent. Enraged, Lu said, "In the past, the wife of Duke Jia despised her husband because he lacked wit. However, when she saw him shoot pheasants, she still let go of her resentment. Now, I am inferior to Duke Jia, and my literary and artistic talents are not just for shooting pheasants, yet he remains silent. A man who is despised by his wife, what use is his son?" He then grabbed the child's feet and struck his head against a rock, killing him instantly. Blood splattered several steps. Zi Chun, filled with love for the child, forgot his vow and unconsciously exclaimed, "Ah!"

Ah! The sound had not yet died down when the person sat in the same place, and the Taoist priest was also in front of him. It was the fifth watch of the night. The purple flame shot through the house and into the sky, and a fire broke out in all four quarters, burning down all the houses and rooms. The Taoist priest sighed and said, "You scholar have caused me so much trouble!" He then pulled the person's hair and threw him into a water jar. Soon the fire was extinguished. The Taoist priest stepped forward and said, "Come out. Your heart, free from joy, anger, sorrow, fear, and desire, can all be forgotten. The only thing that has not been achieved is love. If you had not made that 'Ah!' sound, my medicine would have been successful, and you would have become an immortal. Alas, it is difficult to find someone with the talent to become an immortal! My medicine can be refined again, and your body is still part of the world. Be diligent!" He pointed to the road to send the person back. Zi Chun forced his way up to look, and the furnace was already ruined, with an iron pillar as thick as an arm and several feet long inside. The Taoist priest took off his clothes and used a knife to shave it. Zi Chun returned home, feeling ashamed for breaking his vow, and tried to make amends for his mistake. He walked to Yun Tai Peak, where there were no human tracks, and returned home in regret.

Pei Chen, Wang Jingbo, and Liang Fang were friends who sought to transcend conventional social and moral norms. During the era of Emperor Yang of Sui, they entered the White Deer Mountain together to practice Daoism, believing that gold and silver could be transmuted, the elixir of immortality could be attained, and that spiritual transformation and ascension to heaven were possible through diligent study. They worked tirelessly, their hands and feet covered in calluses, for more than a decade. Soon, Liang Fang died, and Wang Jingbo said to Pei Chen, "I have forsaken my country and family, cut myself off from music, grown tired of rich food, abandoned beautiful colors, left grand houses for a humble hut, and valued solitude over pleasure. Is it not to hope to ride clouds and ride on cranes, to play in the mythical Isles of the Immortals? Even if I do not achieve immortality, I still hope for long life, to live as long as the heavens and earth. Now, the sea of immortality is boundless, and I have not yet attained eternal life. My hard work in the mountains has not saved me from death. What I enjoy is to descend from the mountains, ride in fine vehicles and wear light clothing, listen to songs and admire beautiful women, travel to Luoyang, and only then seek to achieve something, to establish accomplishments and leave a legacy, to honor the human world. Even if I cannot rest in the Three Mountains, drink from the Jade Pool, ride dragons and wear clouds, sing with phoenixes and fly with cranes, and be companions with immortals, I can still wear gold and purple, have my image inscribed on the Hall of Heroic Deeds, and mingle with the ranks of high officials. What do you think? Why don't you return? Don't die in vain in the mountains." Pei Chen replied, "I am now awake from my dream; I will not be disheartened again." Wang Jingbo then returned, but Pei Chen could not persuade him to stay. At the beginning of the Zhenguan era of the Tang dynasty, Wang Jingbo was appointed as a Left Guard Cavalry Officer based on his previous credentials, and the general Zhao Fei married him to his daughter. Over the next few years, Wang Jingbo was promoted to a judge in the Supreme Court, wore red robes, and was sent as an envoy to Huainan, where he sailed past Gaoyou.

The envoy's journey was marked by his commanding presence, with shouts and commands like a gust of wind, causing boats to dare not move. It was slightly raining at the time. Suddenly, a fishing boat passed through, with an elderly man inside, dressed in a straw cloak and hat, rowing his boat away swiftly, like the wind. Jingbo thought he was the envoy, with his authority resonating far and wide, and wondered how this fisherman dared to pass him by. He looked at him, and it was Chen. He immediately ordered to follow him, and then asked him to tie his boat, inviting him to sit inside. He grasped his hand and comforted him, saying, "Brother, you have been living deep in the mountains for a long time, abandoning officialdom without achieving anything, reaching this extreme. The wind cannot be tied, the shadow cannot be caught. Ancient people, tired of long nights, still held candles to play. How much more so for young people to throw away their daytime?" Jingbo has been out of the mountains for several years, and now he is an official in the Imperial Censorate. Yesterday, when he adjudicated a case fairly, it was a gift from heaven to be appointed.

A Case of Doubt in Huainan A case of doubt in Huainan was presented to the authorities, and the emperor selected thorough and clear officials to re-examine it. Jinglei was chosen for this task, hence his journey. Although he did not reach high office, compared to a mountain hermit, he considered himself slightly better. His brother willingly endured hard work, still as before, how remarkable! How remarkable! What do you need now, I will provide it. Chen replied, "We rustic people, our hearts are close to the clouds and cranes, cannot be frightened by something as insignificant as a rotten mouse. I sink, you float, fish and birds each find their own place, why must you boast or show off? What the world needs, I will provide for you, what can you give me? I with my friends in the mountains, sometimes buy medicine in Guangling, also have a place to rest one's burden. East of Qingyuan Bridge, there is a cherry orchard several miles long, the carriage gate north of the garden, is my house. If you have a little free time from your official duties, you should find me here." Then he suddenly left.

Jingbo arrived in Guangling for more than ten days, with few affairs and leisure. Thinking of Chen's words, he went out to look for him. Indeed, there was a carriage door. He tried to ask about it, and it turned out to be Pei's residence. People led him in. At first, it was still desolate, but as he moved further, it became better. After walking several hundred steps, he finally reached the main gate. Towers and pavilions were repeated, and flowers and trees were fresh and beautiful, seeming not of this world. Smoke and greenery were lush, and the scenery was beautiful and charming, beyond description. A fragrant wind came swiftly, refreshing his spirit and clearing his mind. He felt light and airy as if he could touch the clouds, no longer considering his carriage important, seeing himself as a rotten mouse and his followers as ants. Soon, he heard the sound of swords and belts. Two young men came out and said, "Master is coming." Soon, there was a person, wearing splendid attire, with extraordinary beauty and grace. Jingbo bowed before him, and he saw that it was Chen. Pei comforted him, saying, "Officials of the mundane world, long accustomed to the stench of meat, the flames of desire and sorrow burned in their hearts, carrying it with them, indeed very tiring and exhausting." He then invited him in and sat in the main hall. Windows, doors, and beams were decorated with precious materials, and screens and curtains were all painted with clouds and cranes. After a while, four young men carried a jade table and plate, with precious and rare utensils, all not found in the mundane world. Fragrant wine and delicious food, things never seen before. Soon, the day was ending, and he ordered the seating to be moved closer, lighting lamps with nine lights, the light filling the room. Twenty female musicians, all of extraordinary beauty, sat in front of him.

Pei Gu called to the small yellow-haired servant and said, "Mr. Wang, who was once my friend in the mountains, had not held firm to the Dao, abandoned me and went down the mountain. We have not seen each other for nearly ten years, and only recently became an associate of the Chief Justice. Now his worldly desires have settled, and he needs secular courtesans to entertain him. But among the courtesans, there is no one worth summoning; we should summon the daughters of scholars and officials who have already been married. If there is no beauty nearby, one may choose from anywhere within five thousand li." The small yellow-haired servant replied with a polite nod and left. The courtesans began tuning the jade-green zither, but before the tuning was complete, the yellow-haired servant returned with a message, leading a courtesan up from the western steps and bowing before Pei’s seat. Pei pointed and said, "Respectful Sir." Jingbo returned the bow, then looked closely at her and realized she was his wife, Zhao. Jingbo was stunned and could not speak, and his wife was also greatly shocked, unable to stop looking at her. Pei then ordered her to sit on the jade steps, and a servant in green clothing presented her with a tortoiseshell zither, which Zhao had always been skilled at playing. He then ordered her to play a duet with the courtesan to accompany the wine.

During the gathering, Jinger took a dark red plum and threw it to Zhao, who glanced at Jinger and secretly tied it to his belt. The courtesans played music, but Zhao could not keep up with the melody. Pei then instructed the musicians to pause at the moments Zhao played, highlighting his performance. Although the music and dance were not the divine music of the Ninefold Chimes, they were still clear, deep, and graceful, bringing great joy to the audience. As dawn approached, Pei summoned a servant and said, "Escort Lady Zhao." He also addressed her, saying, "This hall is the Nine Heavens' Painted Hall, a place rarely visited by ordinary people. In the past, I had a spiritual friendship with Wang, who was lost in worldly concerns, throwing himself into danger, burning himself with wisdom, and destroying himself with clarity, caught in the cycle of birth and death, unable to find a shore. I arranged for him to come here to wake him up. Today's gathering is truly a rare opportunity, also part of your predestined fate, allowing you to briefly visit, facing countless mountains and hardships, with no regrets." Zhao bowed and departed.

Pei told Jingbo, "If the envoy's carriage stays here for one night, would it not disturb the troops? It is best to temporarily stay in the guesthouse, and you may visit me before the time of reporting to the imperial court. The dusty road is far away, and a thousand worries assail one; take care of yourself." Jingbo bowed and thanked him before departing. Five days later, as he was about to return, he secretly went to bid farewell. The door no longer had a house; it had become a desolate place, with grass and tobacco plants stretching to the horizon. He returned with a sense of sadness and longing. After finishing the report in the capital and returning to his private residence, the various Zhao angrily said, "The woman is indeed ugly and clumsy, not fit to serve a gentleman. Yet, having already received great respect, it is also proper to respect her. To honor the ancestors above and continue the affairs of the future below, how could it be just a casual matter? How could one use magic to bring her from afar to entertain people? The red and black (colors) are still there; the banquet is sufficient proof. Why hide it?" Jingbo spoke everything, and added, "At that time, Jingbo was also uncertain. This is because Pei's method has succeeded; he is showing off with this." His wife also remembered Pei's words and did not rebuke him further.

Oh! The transformations of immortals, are they truly like this? Or is it that the deceiver sells his tricks to cause confusion? It is certainly beyond the reach of ordinary wisdom. Let alone the transformations of sparrows into clams, pheasants into sea dragons, people into tigers, decaying grass into fireflies, beetles into cicadas, and the giant fish into the giant bird. The transformations of all things, as recorded in books and traditions, cannot be understood by intelligence, how much more so beyond what we can see and hear! The daughter of the Wei family of Jingzhao, two years after she reached the age of marriage, her mother told her: "There is a scholar named Pei Shuang who wishes to marry you." The girl laughed and said: "He is not my husband." Her mother remembered this, even though matchmakers came every day, greatly praising Pei's talents, his family greatly admired her, but it still did not work out. Another year later, her mother said: "There is a man named Wang Wu, who previously served in the military of Jingzhao. His recorder, Zhang Shenyue, is your uncle, and he is the matchmaker for Wang. He will marry you." The girl also said: "No." Her mother then said: "Zhang also knows me well, and he is the matchmaker for Wang. His words are not false." But it still did not work out.

After another two years, the Jinshi Zhang Chujin sought her. His mother told him about it, and the woman smiled and said, "My husband is this person." His mother agreed, and he then chose an auspicious date. After the ceremony was completed, he gently asked his mother, and she replied, "This is a sign from my dream. However, all the events of this life were seen, how could it be only the knowledge of being married to Zhang Chujin first? I had already reached the age of笄 (a coming-of-age ceremony for girls), and in my dream, I was twenty years old and married to Zhang Chujin of Qinghe. As a Shangshu (Minister of State) controlling Guangling, he governed for seven years, and then Zhang Chujin was executed. The entire family died, only I and the new bride survived, and we survived and entered the imperial harem, where we served for eighteen years, eating vegetarian food. We received an imperial decree to be released. From noon when I received the order, it was evening when I left the palace gates, and together with the new bride, we crossed the water. When it became dark and we reached the shoal, looking around, it was already dusk, and we did not know where to go. So we wept together on the shoal, encouraging each other, 'We cannot stay here for long, we should cross quickly.' We then went south. After walking several hundred steps to the shore, there was a ruined building there.

Entering from the western gate, he followed the wall to the north. The eastern main gate, which was constructed there, was wide open and unoccupied, so he entered. When he reached the broken gate of the halberd, it was also open, and he entered again. Crossing the screen and the four-sided corridor, he found a hall that was already locked. In front of the steps, there were four large cherry tree groves, and the flowers were in full bloom. When the moonlight filled the courtyard, it seemed as if no one lived there, and he did not know what to report. So he lay down with his new wife at the base of the steps. Not long after, an old man came to scold and chase them away, and he told him of the previous situation before leaving. Then he heard the sound of footsteps and shoes in the western corridor, and a young man came to scold, calling out to the old man to follow. He pleaded with him, and the young man lowered his head and walked away. Slowly, he appeared in a white shirt and plain shoes, crying and bowing at the base of the steps, saying, "I am the nephew of a certain Minister of Works." And then he wept bitterly, saying, "There was nowhere to inquire about news, not knowing that my mother and sister-in-law had arrived, as if they had descended from heaven. This is the old house, and what was locked inside the hall were all old possessions." He opened the door and cried, finding it just as he remembered from nine years ago (this sentence may have an omission). His mother was greatly surprised. Moreover, the prosperity and decline of people are all predestined, and I have always heard this. How could the dream be so true, and yet so real? He then remembered it in his heart.

Shortly afterward, Chu Jin was granted command in Guangling. During the *Shénlóng* era, Xu Jingye was implicated in a plot to restore the throne and was executed by collective punishment, with only his wife and female relatives spared from death, though they were sentenced to serve in the *Yechting* for eighteen years. On the occasion of Wu Zetian’s birthday, she pardoned many of the registered laborers, and these women were among those granted clemency. In the afternoon, he received an imperial decree, and as he was about to depart, the chief eunuch in crimson robes hurried to stay and eat, waiting for him. After finishing the meal, it was already evening. He then lifted his robe and crossed the water while weeping, and upon reaching the house, there was no dream at all. Ah! If dreams are indeed signs, then how can the previous account of the *Fufeng Gong* be reconciled with this? ○ Yuan Wu You During the *Bǎoyìng* era, there was a man named Yuan Wu You, who once, at the end of the second lunar month, walked alone in the outskirts of Weiyang. It was late in the day when a sudden downpour began. At that time, it had been after a period of war and famine, and people had fled their homes. He entered an empty farmhouse by the roadside. Soon the rain ceased, and a slanted moon appeared. Yuan Wu You rested in the northern pavilion when he suddenly heard footsteps in the western corridor. Shortly afterward, four figures entered the hall. They all wore unusual clothing and engaged in lively conversation and recitation. One of them said, “Tonight is like autumn, with such a fine wind and moon—should our group not compose a poem to record our life’s experiences?” Their composition was a *haokou lianju* (a form of collective verse). As they recited, Yuan Wu You listened intently. One of the tall men, dressed in unusual attire, said:

The fine silk from Qi and Lu is as pure as frost and snow, and a clear and resonant voice is raised for you. The second person, dressed in black and short and unremarkable, said: "At a gathering of distinguished guests during a clear night, I can manage brilliant lamps and candles." The third person, dressed in an old yellow robe and also short and unremarkable, recited: "A cool and clear spring waits to be drawn in the morning, with a mulberry rope frequently being used to draw water." The fourth person, also dressed in black and short and unremarkable, recited: "I burn firewood to store water and frequently boil it, laboring to fill others' stomachs." The host did not find these four people strange, and the four people did not suspect that the host was in the hall. They praised each other, envying their self-pride, even though Ruan Ji's "Elegies" could not surpass it. The four people returned to their old place late in the morning, and the host searched for them, finding only an old pestle, candle holder, water bucket, and broken pot in the hall. Thus, it was known that the four people were these objects. ○ Duke Guo Duke Guo Yuanzhen, during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, failed the imperial examination and traveled from Jin to Fen. He was walking at night in the dark and lost his way. After a long time, he saw a distant light of lamps, thinking it was a human dwelling, and went straight to it. After eight or nine miles, he reached a mansion with a very imposing gate. After entering, the corridors and hall were brightly lit with lamps and candles, and the food was arranged as if it were a wedding feast, yet there was no one around. The duke tied his horse in front of the western corridor, climbed the steps, and wandered around in the hall, not knowing where he was. Soon, he heard the sound of a woman crying in the east pavilion, sobbing endlessly.

The gentleman asked, "Who is weeping in the hall? Is it a human or a ghost? Why is it set up like this, with no one here but she is weeping alone?" She replied, "In this village's shrine, there is a deity known as the Black General, who can bring misfortune or blessings to people. Every year, he seeks a spouse from the villagers, and the villagers must choose the most beautiful unmarried girls and marry them. Although I am ugly and clumsy, my father profited from the villagers' offer of five hundred strings of cash and secretly agreed to my being chosen. Tonight, the villagers' daughters, who were all participating in a feast, came to this room, got me drunk, locked me up, and left to take me to the Black General. Now, my parents have abandoned me to die, and I am filled with fear and grief. If you are truly a human, can you save me? I will serve as a cleaning woman for the rest of my life and obey your commands." The gentleman angrily said, "When will he come?" She replied, "At the second watch." He said, "I am unworthy to be a man of virtue, but I will do my utmost to save you. If I fail, I will kill myself to save you, and I will never let you die in vain at the hands of a lustful ghost." The woman's weeping stopped a little. Then, he sat on the western steps, moved his horse to the north of the hall, and had a servant stand in front of him, treating him as a guest.

Shortly thereafter, the light of the fire shone brightly, and carriages and horses were crowded together. Two officials in purple robes entered and then exited, saying, "The prime minister is here." Hesitantly, two officials in yellow robes entered and then exited, also saying, "The prime minister is here." The scholar secretly delighted, thinking, "I shall become the prime minister and will surely overcome this ghost." Soon after, the general gradually descended, and the guide officials reported to him again. The general said, "Enter." With spears, swords, bows, and arrows as accompaniment, the scholar then bowed and said, "Deacon Guo is here." He proceeded to pay his respects. The general asked, "How did you come to be here?" The scholar replied, "I heard that you are having a joyous occasion tonight and merely wish to be a minor official." The general was pleased and invited him to sit, and they ate together, speaking and laughing very joyfully. The scholar had a sharp knife in his bag and thought to kill him, so he asked, "Have you ever eaten deer jerky?" The general replied, "It is hard to find here."

The official said, "I have something rare and precious, obtained from the imperial kitchen, and I wish to carve and present it." The general was very pleased. The official then rose, took the deer jerky and a small knife, carved it, placed it in a small container, and allowed the general to take it himself. The general was pleased and reached out to take it, with no suspicion of any other intention. The official waited for the right moment, then threw the jerky, grabbed the general's wrist, and broke it. The general cried out and ran away, and the officials accompanying him were suddenly scattered in alarm. The official held his hand, removed his clothes, and wrapped them around it. He ordered the driver to look outside, but saw nothing. He then opened the door and said to the weeping person, "The general's wrist is now here. Following the blood trail, death will not be far off. Since you have been spared, you may go out to eat." The weeping person then came out, about seventeen or eighteen years old, and very beautiful. She knelt before the official and said, "I swear to be your servant and concubine." The official encouraged and advised her. It was just dawn, and when he opened his hand, it was a pig's paw.

Soon, the sound of weeping grew closer, and it turned out to be the girl's parents, brothers, and the elders of the village, all carrying the coffin together, intending to collect the body for burial. Seeing the gentleman and the girl, they realized they were still alive. Everyone was surprised and asked about it, and the gentleman explained in detail. The village elders, enraged by the desecration of their god, said, "General of the Crows, this is our village deity. The villagers have worshipped him for a long time. We provide him with a girl each year, and only then do we avoid other calamities. If this ritual is slightly delayed, wind, rain, thunder, and hail will wreak havoc. How can a lost traveler harm our clear god and bring violence upon our people? What has this village done wrong? We should kill you to sacrifice to General of the Crows. If not, we will bind you and send you to our county." They gestured to the young men to seize the gentleman, but he explained to them, "You may be old in age, but you are not experienced in handling such matters."

I, who understand the principles of the world, address you all. The deity, who upholds the heavens and stabilizes the earth, is not as worthy as the feudal lords who receive their mandate from the Son of Heaven and govern the realm, is he not? The people responded affirmatively. I said, "If the feudal lords indulge in lust within the central lands, would the Son of Heaven not be angry? If they oppress and harm people, would the Son of Heaven not punish? If indeed the one you call 'General' is truly a deity, a deity would not have pig's feet. Would Heaven send a lustful and monstrous beast? Moreover, a lustful and monstrous beast is the guilty creature of heaven and earth. I will uphold righteousness and punish it, is this not possible! You people have no righteous ones. If your young girls die year after year at the hands of this monstrous beast, accumulated sins move Heaven. How do you know that Heaven does not intend for me to avenge this? If you follow my words, I will eliminate it for you, and you will never again suffer from the burden of bride prices. What do you say?" The villagers understood and were pleased, saying, "We wish to follow your command."

The official ordered hundreds of people, armed with bows, arrows, swords, spears, shovels, and other tools, to form a circle around themselves and follow the trail of blood. After traveling only twenty li, the blood led them to a large tomb's cave. They surrounded the area and began digging, and as they dug, the opening gradually widened like the mouth of a jar. The official then ordered bundled firewood to be lit and thrown into the opening to illuminate it. Inside, they found a large room containing a pig with no left front hoof, lying on the ground in blood. The pig burst out of the smoke and died within the enclosure. The villagers rejoiced together and gathered money to reward the official. The official declined, saying, "I am here to eliminate harm for people, not to sell hunting." The woman who had been spared, addressing her parents and relatives, said, "I am fortunate to be human, entrusted with blood relations, and have not yet left the inner chambers, so there is no fault in killing. Greedy for fifty thousand coins, I chose to marry an evil beast, enduring the lock and leaving, is this suitable for a person? If not for the benevolence and courage of Official Guo, how could I have this day? I have died to my parents and been reborn through Official Guo. I will follow Official Guo, no longer thinking of my old hometown." She wept and bowed, following the official. Despite many attempts to persuade and stop her, the official eventually took her as a concubine, and they had several children.

The person's rise to prominence was marked by holding high-ranking official positions. The matter was predetermined, and even if born far away, the gods and spirits could not harm him, clearly showing the inevitability of his success. ○ Junchuo During the reign of Emperor Yang of Sui, when he launched a campaign against Goguryeo, all twelve armies were defeated. General Lai Hu was executed for legal reasons, and Emperor Yang wanted to execute all of his sons. Junchuo, fearing that he would be implicated in the execution, formed a close friendship with the scholars Luo Xun, Luo Ti, and Li Wanjin, and they fled together to Haizhou. At night, they were lost and disoriented. They saw lights along the road and decided to seek refuge there. They knocked on the door several times, and an old servant greeted Junchuo with a bow. Junchuo asked, "Who lives here?" The servant replied, "This is a young scholar named Wei, surnamed Wei, a local scholar from the prefecture." They then opened the door and closed it again, knocking on the inner door and saying, "Wotou, there are four or five guests outside." Wotou was another young servant. They opened the door and lit candles to guide the guests to the guest room, where the beds and cushions were well-prepared. Soon, two young boys carrying candles came out from the inner door and said, "Sixth Master, come out." Junchuo and the others descended the steps to meet the host. The host spoke eloquently and persuasively, introducing himself as "Wei Wuhuo." After exchanging pleasantries, he invited the guests to sit on the main seat and said, "Wuhuo is honored to share the same state with you, and a pleasant meeting on a clear night is a great wish." He then ordered wine and sat down with them. Gradually, they became merry, and jokes and teasing alternated, leaving everyone unable to respond.

Jun Chuo was quite unable to be at peace, wanting to use reason to defeat him/her, but having no plan, he then raised his cup and said, "Jun Chuo proposes to start a rule: those whose names in the company have homophones will be punished according to the law." Jun Chuo said, "Wei Wuhuo," which was actually a pun on the person's name. Everyone clapped and laughed loudly, thinking they had hit the mark. When it came to Wuhuo, he changed the rule and said, "To make the surnames of the people in the company into songs, from two characters to five characters." The rule was, "Luo Li, Luo Lai Li, Luo Li Luo Lai, Luo Li Luo Li Lai." Everyone was ashamed of their quick wit. Luo Xun then asked, "You are a gentleman of refined tastes, worthy to compare oneself to a dragon. Why do you belittle yourself with such a name?" Wuhuo said, "I have been a guest scholar for a long time, often being humiliated by the examiners. What difference is there between a small caterpillar in a dirty pool?" Luo Xun asked again, "You are of noble lineage, why is your clan not recorded?" Wuhuo said, "I am originally from the Tian family, descended from Duke Wei of Qi, also like the Huan and Ding families. How is it that you are not well-read?" Soon after, a large tray was brought, filled with delicacies from both land and sea. Jun Chuo and the servants all ate their fill. As night deepened and the candles were extinguished, they slept on the same couch. At dawn, they parted ways, both deeply saddened and could not bear it.

Jun Chuo and his companions walked several miles, still thinking about the "污蠖" (a type of creature), and then returned. They saw the place where they had met the previous day, but there was no sign of human habitation, only a dirty pond. By the pond's edge, there was a large earthworm, several feet long. There were also snails and something called "丁子," all of which were several times larger than usual. They then realized that the "污蠖" and the two little devils were all the same kind of creature. As a result, they all became disgusted with what they had eaten the previous night and each vomited out several liters of green mud and dirty water. Volume Two ○ Cui Huan Cui Huan of Anping was the son of Cui Xuan, a military officer. In the fifth year of the Yuanhe era, during the summer of May, he fell ill at his estate in Zhengyang. Suddenly, he saw two officials in yellow robes, holding a summons, who came to escort him. They walked several hundred steps and entered the city. The city had streets on both sides, with government forests facing each other, and there were no houses at all. They walked straight north for several miles to a gate, which was inscribed with the words "Judicial Office." They saw two officials walking north, and there were also trees, men wearing trousers and boots, and those wearing headgear, carrying swords, and holding bows and arrows, scattered among them, numbering several hundred people. There were thousands of people who had come with them, some bound, some tied, some bound, some in bags with their heads, some with their necks connected, some dressed neatly, some with hairpins and skirts, all showing signs of fear, some crying and some sighing. One of the officials in yellow robes stayed to accompany Cui Huan, while the other entered to report.

Soon he heard the sound of people being separated, and then a messenger came out and said: “The Judge has conveyed this message: Why do you neglect the young, fail to establish a family, recklessly destroy estates, and indulge only in wine and sensual pleasures? Considering that you are a minor with no authority, I have shown leniency and will release you with a light beating. You should now cleanse your heart and not repeat your offenses. If you persist in your past wrongs, there will be no place for you to be accepted.” He then ordered his attendants to send him back. Huan asked, “Who is this Judge?” The attendant replied, “It is the Officer of Military Affairs.” Huan wept and said, “I have been estranged from you for many years, and my cries to heaven have gone unanswered. Fortunately, you have pursued me, and your kind face is not far away. I beg only for a single sight of you, and I shall die with no regrets.” The two officials said, “The bright and the obscure are different; there is a boundary between staying and going. We cannot meet.” Huan said, “Earlier, the message said that I had already been reprimanded. If this body cannot enter, how can I receive punishment?” The official replied, “If you enter, you will not be allowed to return. All people have three souls: one remains at home, and two receive the beating. Do not believe me—look at the young man’s legs and see if there are marks from the beating.” He then lifted his robe to look, and on each of his legs there were four marks from the beating. In great pain, he crawled along, finding it extremely difficult to raise his feet. Those who had accompanied him heard the sounds of admiration and sorrow echoing at the crossroads.

Walking southeast for over a hundred steps, they reached a large forest on the eastern side of the street. Two officials approached and said, "We serve the judge in the underworld day and night, and though we have done so for a long time, as minor officials in the underworld, we are inevitably poor. Each of us has been offered gifts and wealth, but we have no time to take them. Without sending you on your way through the underworld, we cannot go to seek them. Please stop temporarily under the tree, and we will go together, returning shortly. All places are offices of evil spirits, and it is not appropriate to go there. Please do not move from your spot." After saying this, they departed, and did not return for a long time. Feeling restless, the person tried to walk west along the street and came to a gate with a sign that read "Human Ore Institute." The gate was also very clean. The person, who had always been brave and relied on their father being a judge in the underworld, having also been granted release, entered the place. Passing through the barriers, they saw a large stone, several miles around. A military officer was seated at the northern hall of the stone, sitting at a desk, with people gathered around the stone and on it. There were dozens of large spirits with different appearances, using large iron hammers to turn people into ore. To the east, there were thousands of people bound with shackles and chains, crying and fearing, beyond description. They were called out by name, dragged to the stone, and then hammered, once broken, their names were called. The military officer judged them, and one clerk read from behind the desk, "Fou (a name) has completed his sentence." The spirits also confirmed it. Among them were those who were to be sent to the furnace, those to be sent to the fire, and those to be sent to the soup.

Huan approached directly and examined it before the stone. The military officer pointed to it and said, "Under Cao's strict legal system, one should not enter without cause. Who is that person, and how dare you come to look casually?" Officials and commoners came to ask and relay information, but Huan stood silently without responding. The military officer angrily said, "Since you looked without reason and did not answer when questioned, how can mere observation be as thorough as experiencing it firsthand?" He then ordered an official to drag Huan and torture him. Huan's soul still stood, and he saw another body on the stone. The body was dragged and fell on the stone, and he was beaten with a large hammer. He suffered greatly, and the pain was truly unbearable. In a moment, his bones and flesh were all broken, and he was almost reduced to mud. Two officials came and pounded their chests and said, "Lord, you repeatedly begged not to wander around, why did you come here?" They then told the military officer, "This is the judge's lord, whose earthly life has not ended. He was called back but then released, one who came in temporarily. In the no-gap hell, one enters without a moment's delay. He has suffered like this. What can be done to restore the original state?" The military officer also feared and said, "At first, he did not speak, but then handled it angrily. What should be done?" He then asked the ghosts, "What can be done to restore the original state?" They all said, "Only Pu Yang Xia is the one." He asked, "How far is he?" They replied, "A thousand miles away. Yesterday, the Beihai King transformed and went out to play, was captured by sea people. The king requested his release, but he has not returned yet." He then ordered a ghost to summon him.

After a short while, he arrived, and it turned out to be an old man with a beard and one blind eye. He came in a hurry, still breathing heavily. The military officer pointed to the ring and asked, "What's the plan?" Pu Yang Xia said, "It's easy." Then he took off his clothes, tied them around his waist, took out medicine powder from his chest, and sprinkled it on the ore to form a shape. Flipping and sprinkling, he shaped the ore into a head and limbs, carved out the internal organs, connected them as the digestive system, and carved the nine orifices. It quickly took shape. He placed his hand on its neck and said, "Rise!" It rose up, merged with him, and could walk. The two officials greatly valued this. They then continued to travel south together. As they were about to leave, Pu Yang Xia patted his shoulder and said, "Scholar, you were found alive in the mine, but you can't leave without paying a penny." Ring agreed to pay thirty million coins. Pu Yang Xia smiled and said, "The old official is busy, so I'll send the little demon 'Xiao'er' to fetch it, and he'll hand it over when he sees you."

As they reached the city gate, they saw a clerk running south, saying, "The Yellow River wishes to divide a branch; previously, heaven decreed to take one out of three, counting merit but not counting, now requesting to take one out of two." The two clerks, having committed the sin of private action concerning the ring, feared that the announcement of the ring's anger would summon them, and told feng, "If he sees and asks, just say that you want to see the methods of hell as a warning, so you are here." When the clerk saw and asked, feng answered as they said. Then they parted and continued their journey. A short while later, they arrived at Yingyang, and the two clerks said, "Certainly will return to life. I will take something, can you see it once?" Feng said, "It is indeed what I wish." Together they entered the county town, to a house in the courtyard, one clerk tied a woman's head to a bed with a rope from his pocket, pulling her with all his might, the other clerk slowly collected her breath with a leopard-skin bag, when her breath was exhausted, then pulled her down, binding her. Together they sent feng home, upon entering, the two clerks shouted loudly, "Feng!" Mistakenly hitting the door frame, he woke up. His family wept, waiting for him, it had been seven days. After several days, a crow cawed in the courtyard, feng said, "The son of Old Man Puyang has come." So he ordered his family to carve paper money and burn it, then he left. After his illness subsided, he secretly searched for the house of the woman he saw, it was the wife of the county official Guo Pei. At that time, there was no proposal to divide the river, but a few days later, the Governor of Hedong, Sima Xue, proposed to divide a branch of the river, hoping to reduce the force of the river against the city. Initially proposed to take one out of three, but later, fearing it was insufficient, proposed to take one out of two, ultimately, it was as feng saw in the underworld.

**Liu Guishun** Liu Guishun from Wu Province, during the twentieth year of the Kaihuang era of the Sui Dynasty, traveled from the southern part of China to Baling. A strong wind blew him to the foot of Junshan Mountain. He moored his boat and went ashore, looking for a small path. Unconsciously, he walked several miles, in a state of high spirits, leaping over streams and ravines, not following the usual path. Suddenly, he saw a large stone by the side of the road, transparent inside and out, round and smooth, surrounding six or seven acres. Outside it was all covered with green bamboo, round and large like a pot, over a hundred feet tall, with leaves trailing white clouds, densely arranged and reaching up to the sky, and a gentle breeze blowing, sounding like the music of silk and bamboo. A tree grew from the center of the stone, over a hundred feet tall, with branches and trunk bending in the shade, appearing in five colors. The green leaves were like plates, the flowers over a foot in diameter, deep blue in color, leaves deep red, with a strange fragrance turning into smoke, floating gently on objects. There were thousands of parrots, with red beaks and green feathers, tails two or three feet long, soaring among them, calling each other by name, with clear and resonant sounds. One of them sang a song and said, "This song is what the Lady Gouyi of Emperor Wu of Han often sang." The lyrics were: "Wearing the cicada, clearly conveying the words of the emperor."

In the Jianzhang Palace, unable to return, a red curtain, a golden lamp, and two phoenixes danced. A person named Ah So said, "I remember Princess A-Jiao weeping in the deep palace, singing: 'Once I asked Sima Xiangru to write the *Changmen Fu*, but it cost a hundred gold pieces, and the emperor still ignored it.'" Another person recited Sima Xiangru's *Daren Fu* and said, "When I first learned to write fu, I was struck by Consort Zhao with a seven-treasure hairpin, and the pain was not yet fully over. Today, I recite it, and it remains a lifelong skill." A person named Wu You Lang said, "I once saw Emperor Wu of Han riding a boat with a jade paddle, sailing on the Jade Green Pool, blowing a purple jade flute, with a clear and pleasant sound, and the emperor's mood was joyful. Lady Li sang along, singing: 'Looking back, I am humble and lowly, serving you with my utmost devotion. May our lord live for ten thousand years.'" A person named Wu Xian Lang asked Gui Shun, "What is your name and rank?" Gui Shun replied, "My surname is Liu, and I am the twelfth." He said, "Mr. Liu the twelfth, where do you come from?" Gui Shun replied, "I was heading to Baling, encountered a storm, and docked my boat, arriving here in a state of intoxication." Wu Xian Lang said, "Mr. Liu the twelfth, you happened to encounter a storm and arrived in a strange land, which is what they call beauty arising from illness. However, I am a bird, unable to exert effort for living beings, and I will convey your message to Miss Thirty of the Gui family." He then called out, "Ah Chun, there is a guest here." Soon, several purple clouds flew in from the southwest. After rising a few feet, the clouds dispersed, revealing jade towers and green curtains, multiple balconies and flying beams, encircling the stone edges.

A young woman dressed in fine attire emerged from the door. She was about thirteen or fourteen years old, adorned with pearls and jade, and her appearance was extremely beautiful. She spoke to Guishun, saying, "San Shi, the lady, sends A Chun to deliver a message to the young man: She lives in poverty and in a remote place, and she is grateful for the effort made to visit her. She does not know if you have eaten breakfast this morning. Please sit down for a moment, and she will prepare a simple meal." A servant then brought out a bed made of water crystal. Guishun refused twice before sitting down. A Chun then called out to the parrot named Feng Huatai, asking, "Why are the guests not being entertained?" Feng Huatai replied, "San Shi is too shy to interact with the young man directly. If you act carelessly, you might be punished again." A parrot flew over and said, "I am Feng Huatai. I recently visited the mythical place of Penglai Jade Pavilion and composed a poem. Would you like to hear it?" Guishun replied, "I have always loved poetry, and this truly aligns with my desires." Feng Huatai then began to recite the poem she had composed:

The morning dew meets the rising sun, and the sea waves resemble crystal. A jade tower overlooks the vastness, with the sky and earth illuminating each other. At this moment, returning to rest, one returns to the same pillars. What shame is there in me, daily serving the company of immortals? Guan Shun said, "It is indeed beautiful. Who is your teacher?" Feng Huatai replied, "I have been in the service of Wang Dan for more than a thousand years. Du Lanhua taught me the true script, and Dong Fangshuo imparted to me his secrets. During the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, I saw the works of Yang Xiong and Wang Bao at the Stone Channel Office, and thus learned the principles of admonitory discourse. During the chaos of Wang Mang, I was able to return to Wu. Later, I was acquired by Zhu Ran and passed on to Lu Sun. I then saw the works of Ji and Yun, and began to learn how to compose poetry and prose. After Ji and Yun were executed, I came to be here. I do not know who the master craftsman is now?" Guan Shun said, "It is Xue Daoheng and Jiang Zong." He then recited several pieces to show him. Feng Huatai said, "Recent works are not without their elegance, but they lack substance and spirit." Soon, Ah Chun brought a red jade tray, with a myriad of precious delicacies, things one has never seen, and an intense fragrance that seems to split the nose.

After finishing his meal, two Taoist priests suddenly appeared, descending from the sky. They turned to see Gu Shun and exclaimed, "It is very rare! To be in the presence of a parrot. Are you not Mr. Liu Shier? Your boat is taking advantage of the favorable wind, and they are looking for you very urgently. Why don't you hurry back?" They then threw a piece of brocade to him and said, "Use this to cover your eyes, and you will depart immediately." Gu Shun followed their instructions, and he suddenly felt as if he were flying, only to fall back to Ba Ling. He reached the place where his boat was, and the boatman was about to set sail. When he asked the boatman, he was told that Gu Shun had been missing for three days. Later, he returned to this place, moored his boat to search, but never saw him again. During the reigns of Emperor Xuanzong in the Kaiyuan and Tianbao eras, there was a scholar named Cui who lived near the Luogu Pass in Eastern Zhou. He enjoyed planting flowers and bamboo, and outside his home, he cultivated exotic flowers. In the late spring, the fragrance of the blossoms was so strong that it could be smelled from a hundred steps away. Every morning, Cui would wash and brush his teeth before admiring the flowers alone. One day, he suddenly saw a beautiful woman riding a fine horse from west to east, with a few elderly attendants following behind. The woman was exceptionally beautiful, and her horse was swift. Cui did not have time to take a closer look before the woman had already passed by. The next day, she passed by again, and Cui, in anticipation, prepared wine and tea on a mat and chair under the flowers. He approached the horse and said, "I enjoy flowers and plants, and everything in this garden is planted by hand. The fragrance and beauty seem worthy of your attention. I have seen you pass by frequently, and I assume your servants must be tired. I have prepared some wine and food, hoping you will rest here." The woman did not look back and continued on her way. Later, one of the attendants said, "Just prepare the wine and food, and there is no need to worry." The woman turned and scolded, "Why do you speak so lightly to strangers?" After saying this, she rode off.

The next day, Cui prepared a special wine at the foot of the mountain and awaited the arrival of the young lady. Upon her arrival, Cui whipped his horse to follow her, arriving at the villa where he dismounted and bowed to make his request. After a long wait, an elderly servant approached the young lady and said, "The carriage and horses are very tired; a brief rest will do no harm." The servant then took control of the young lady's horse and led it to the hall. The elderly servant turned to Cui and said, "Since you are not yet married, may I act as your matchmaker?" Cui was greatly pleased, bowed twice, and knelt, asking the servant not to forget him. The servant replied, "The matter is decided; in fifteen days, on a very auspicious day, you should prepare what is needed for the wedding and prepare food and drink here. The young lady's elder sister is in the Logu area and has a slight illness, so the young lady visits her daily to check on her. I will leave and consult and inform everyone by the appointed time, when they will all be here." The servant then urged the group to leave quickly. Cui followed behind and immediately prepared what was needed for the auspicious day. By the appointed time, the young lady and her sister arrived. The sister was also extremely beautiful in appearance and demeanor. Thus, the young lady was married to Cui.

Cui Sheng's mother was living in their old residence and was completely unaware that her son had taken a wife. Cui Sheng, having not informed his mother, only notified her about the engagement and the secondary wife. When the mother saw the young lady, the young lady had all the wedding rites fully conducted. After more than a month, a person suddenly brought food to the young lady, which was exceptionally sweet and fragrant. Later, Cui Sheng noticed that his mother's face had become pale and worried. He asked her while kneeling, and she said, "I had hoped that you would live long. Now, the new wife you have taken is beautiful and cunning beyond compare. I have never seen such a person in the books of scholars and literati. I fear she may be a deceitful woman who could harm you, causing me great worry." Cui Sheng went into the room to see the young lady, who was weeping and said, "I intended to be a loyal wife, hoping to be with you forever. But I did not know that your mother considers me a deceitful woman. By tomorrow morning, I will request to leave, and our love will last only tonight." Cui Sheng could not speak while holding back tears.

The next day, the young lady arrived with a grand procession. She rode a horse, and Feng Sheng accompanied and sent her off. They entered the Luo Valley for more than thirty miles, where there was a river in the mountains. The river contained exotic fragrances and precious fruits, too numerous to count. The lodging was in a house, more luxurious than a king's. About a hundred maids in green dresses welcomed and bowed to the young lady, saying, "Young lady, Feng Sheng has no virtue, why bring him here?" They then carried the young lady inside and left Feng Sheng outside the door. Not long after, a maid conveyed the words of the young woman's sister: "Feng Sheng, your lack of virtue has made our grandmother suspicious and hesitant. It would be best to end this matter and not meet again. However, since my younger sister has treated you kindly, she should also receive a gift." Soon after, the sister called Feng Sheng inside, scolding him repeatedly. His words were clear and gentle, and Feng Sheng only bowed and accepted his blame. They then sat in the inner chamber to eat, and after finishing, they ordered wine and summoned female musicians to entertain them. The music was varied and beautiful. After the music ended, the sister said to the young lady, "You must make Feng Sheng return. What do you have to give him as a gift?" The young lady then took out a white jade box to give to Feng Sheng, and Feng Sheng also kept something as a keepsake. They both wept and left. Walking to the Luo Valley, they looked back at the thousands of mountains and valleys, with no paths in sight. Feng Sheng cried bitterly and returned home. He often saw the jade box, feeling melancholy and unhappy.

Suddenly, a foreign monk knocked on the door asking for food. Cui Sheng went out to see him, and the monk said, "You have a precious treasure, I beg you to show it to me." Cui Sheng replied, "I am a poor scholar, what do I have that you would ask to see?" The monk said, "Do you not have a gift from a strange person? I, a poor monk, can sense it through my qi." Cui Sheng then took out the box and showed it to the foreign monk. The monk stood up and bowed, saying, "I will buy it for a million." He then took the box and left. Cui Sheng asked the monk, "Who is the lady?" The monk replied, "Your wife you have taken is the third daughter of the Queen Mother, the Lady of the Jade Cup. Her sister also has a beautiful reputation in the celestial realm, let alone in the mortal world. It is a pity that you cannot keep her for a long time. If you stay for a year, your whole family will surely become immortals." Cui Sheng sighed and lamented until he died.

A Tale of Cao Hui In the early years of the Wu德 era, there was a man named Cao Hui who was appointed as a military secretary in Jiangzhou by imperial decree. In his official residence, there was a Buddhist hall, and in the hall were two wooden figures, each about a foot long, intricately carved and decorated, though their paint had faded. Cao Hui took them home and gave them to his young son. Later, when the child was eating a cake, the wooden figures reached out and asked for it. The child was startled and reported this to Cao Hui, who laughed and said, "Bring the wooden figures here." The figures then spoke, saying, "We are known as Light Red and Light White; why do you call us wooden figures?" They then turned and walked around as if they were real people. Cao Hui asked, "When did you come into existence, and are you capable of causing strange events?" Light White replied, "I and Light Red were made by a servant named Xie in the household of the governor of Xuanzhou. At that time, no one in the world could match the craftsmanship of the old servant Xie from the household of Shen Yinhou. Light Red and I were made by Xie. Shen Yinhou, moved by the innocence of the people of Xuanzhou, gave these figures to us on the day of the burial. At that time, I was in the tomb, holding water to wash the feet of Lady Yue. I heard the sound of someone outside with weapons claiming an imperial edict. Lady Yue was afraid, and her bare feet transformed into white ants. Soon after, two bandits with torches arrived and took all the valuables. Lord Xie was wearing a jade ring around his neck, and the bandit knocked it off. The bandits saw Light Red and others, saying, 'These two ritual objects are not bad; they can be toys for children.' So they took them out, and it was the second year of the Tianzheng era. Since then, they have passed through several households. At the end of the Chen dynasty, Wei Tiezhang's nephew named Jiao brought them here, and they have remained until today."
A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

The official asked, "You two are so extraordinary, I wish to release you, what do you think?" They both happily replied, "By transforming into light and color, there is nothing we cannot do. If Your Excellency does not release us, we ultimately cannot escape. The god of Mount Lu has long desired to take light and color as his dancers. Now that we are leaving, we will receive his honor and wealth. However, if Your Excellency can fulfill this favor, please command the painter to create a portrait and bestow upon us cosmetics." The official then ordered the workers to make the portrait, and they were dressed in brocade. One of them, Light and Color, smiled and said, "This time, it's not just about being a dancer, but also his wife. There is nothing to repay, so please leave this subtle message as a farewell. In a hundred generations, if anyone else understands this, they will surely become loyal ministers and hold high positions. The message says: 'The rooster's horn penetrates the bone, the purple crane eats the yellow rat's skin (there may be an error here, 'yellow rat's skin,' the *Guangji* records 'yellow rat's tail,' the 'tail' character might belong to the next line), it will not harm the five-communication spring chamber, bringing good fortune for six generations.'" After speaking, they vanished. Later, someone prayed to the god of Mount Lu, and the female shaman said, "The divine lord has recently taken a new wife, who requires a jade flower hairpin. You should seek it and burn it, and you will receive great blessings." The one who prayed sought the hairpin and burned it, and as a result, his wish was fulfilled. The official could not understand the subtle message, and when he consulted learned scholars, none of them could decipher it. Some said that the chancellor Cen Wenben recognized three of the sentences, but he did not share them with others.

In the first year of the Wenming era, the official of Piling, Teng Tingjun, suffered from a chronic fever illness that had plagued him for many years. Whenever it flared up, his body felt as if it were on fire, and the fever lasted for several days before subsiding. He called for a doctor, but the doctor could not cure him. Later, he went to Luoyang for promotion, and on his way, he reached a place about fifteen or sixteen miles west of荥yang as dusk approached. He had not yet reached his destination, so he sought shelter at a nearby farm. The owner was temporarily absent, and Teng Tingjun felt bored and sang to himself, saying, "Being a guest brings much hardship and bitterness, at dusk, there is no host." Just then, an old man with sparse hair and shabby clothes came out from the west side of the hall and said, "Although I have no special skills, I enjoy literature. I didn't know you were coming, and I was just composing poetry with Hé Qiěyē. I heard you sing, 'Being a guest brings much hardship and bitterness, at dusk, there is no host.' Although Cao Pi's line 'guests often fear people' cannot surpass it, I am a humble servant in the household. My name is Ma Shùhé, and you can call me Ma Dà. Although I am poor, I still have a jug of wine to entertain you with a pleasant conversation." Teng Tingjun was surprised and asked, "Where does the old man live?" The old man replied, "I am a humble servant in the household, and my name is Ma, and I am the first elder. Why don't you call me Ma Dà?" Teng Tingjun politely declined, saying he was not quick-witted enough, but he accompanied the old man. They walked around the west corner of the hall and saw a door. When they opened the door, they found a splendid hall with multiple floors, beautifully decorated. There was a feast prepared in the hall with a jug of wine and plates and cups. The old man bowed to Teng Tingjun and invited him to sit down.

After a long time, a guest emerged from the door. Ma Da said, "The poem is here." Tingjun immediately descended the steps to show respect, returned to sit, and Zhuo Ye said to Ma Da, "Just now composing verses together with you, has the poem's opening line arrived yet?" Ma Da wrote the title himself, saying, "Collaborative poem at the Plain Gate of the Common People. I have already composed four lines." Ma Da's poem read: "From the beginning, I am a neighbor of the end, the fragrance fills my body. It is irrelevant to good cleanliness, yet it removes dust." After a long time, Zhuo Ye said, "I wrote in seven-character lines, and the rhyme is different, how can I proceed?" Ma Da said, "Just write your own section, it's not bad." Thus, Zhuo Ye immediately recited: "In winter, I often go to rely on the warmth of the hearth, in spring I return to raise my descendants. I once sat at the pen of Fu Wang, lately I seek food at the doors of the common people." Tingjun still did not understand, seeing the grandeur of his residence, he had the plan to stay and rest his horse, then he wrote four-character lines as follows.

Lord Tiwen is known for his hospitality, but how many people does he support? If Feng Huan were still alive, he would be among the lowest-ranking guests today. Cai and Ma laughed and said, "How can you mock me? If you had been with your wife, one day would have been enough." They then had a lavish feast with multiple rounds of food and drink. When the host arrived, he couldn't find Ting Jun. He sent someone to call him, and Ting Jun replied, "Yes." But the guest rooms and Ma and He were nowhere to be seen. Ting Jun was in the latrine, with only a large fly and a broom nearby. Ting Jun had previously suffered from a fever, but after this, he was completely cured and the fever never returned. In the first year of the Tianjian era of the Liang dynasty, Gu Zong was a county official. He was often beaten and often felt depressed and resentful. He escaped to a place between tombs, wandering and feeling melancholic, not knowing where to go. Suddenly, two yellow-clad men saw Gu Zong and said, "Lord Liu, do you remember our past interactions?" Gu Zong was surprised and said, "My family is the Gu family, and I have never met you before, so why are you asking about our past interactions?" The two men replied, "We are Wang Can and Xu Gan. You were Lord Liu Zhen in life, a Grand Councilor of Kunning, but you were demoted to a minor official for accepting bribes, and you probably don't remember this now. However, your words are still clear, and you still have the style of a secretary." They then took out five volumes of books from their sleeves and showed them to Gu Zong, saying, "These are your collected works. You should read them carefully." Gu Zong tried to read them, and suddenly he understood everything clearly and felt a surge of creative inspiration.

Most of his collections are available, but only a few poems are remembered after his death. One poem, titled "Following the Emperor on a Tour of the Beautiful Palace, Recalling Past Literary Gatherings at the Western Garden and Sending This Poem to Cai Bojia, the Right Minister of the Ministry of Culture," reads as follows: In the Han dynasty, the laws and norms were in chaos, and the rivers and lakes were turbulent. The illustrious Wei Emperor, Cao Cao, rescued those in peril and calmed the waves. The heavenly mandate has been established, and the people are also secure and complete. He greatly opened the Chancellor's office and gathered all the fragrant orchids. At first, I followed the many gentlemen, daily serving the wise ruler with joy. The cultural emperor was in the spring palace, his filial piety exceeding the usual inquiries about health. The supervisor had extra leisure time, the gardens were freely visited. The lowly minister wore a hairpin and pen, supporting the holy one with harmonious music. Walking out of the palace at night, it was cool; precious trees were bathed in clear dew. The celestial phenomena are indeed splendid, resounding like jade and stone. Being commanded to compose a harmonious piece, looking back, it was difficult to complete. My weak nature could not sustain itself, it was fragile, decaying, and withered. Who would have thought for over a decade, the tombs were cold, with paulownia and Chinese parasol trees. Today, in the country of K'un-ming, again, wearing the official cap. Serving the emperor in the distant palace, climbing high into the clouds. But recalling the time at the Western Garden, for a moment, sorrow and bitterness filled the heart.

You were once a high official in Han, ranking among the foremost among the multitude of talented men. If you recall your lifetime, seeing this will make us both sorrowful. The other seven pieces, their transmission has lost the original meaning. Wang Can said to Zong, "I was originally short, and why did I take the daughter of Le Jin? She resembles her father, even more so in her shortness. After parting from you, I remarried the daughter of Liu Jingzhou. Soon after, I had a son, named by Liu Jingzhou like a servant, now eighteen years old, seven feet three inches tall. What I regret is not being able to meet my father-in-law. When he was eleven, he and I looked at the mirror together. I told him, 'Your head is more imposing than mine.' He immediately replied, 'Fangfeng's bones are so large they require a cart, but it's not as good as Bai Qi's small yet sharp head.' I then told him, 'You will grow up to be a general.' He responded, 'Confucius, even as a three-foot-tall child, would be ashamed to speak of tyrannical rule. Especially since I have been taught by my elders, how dare I think about the ways of mutual slaughter?' I knew he was exceptionally intelligent. I wonder if you have any sons or daughters since then?" After a long period of deep thought, he slowly seemed to recognize someone, and said, "Since the two gentlemen are friends of Zong, what plan can save the small official from his plight?" Xu Gan said, "You just need to present your previous collection and appeal to the county magistrate, and you will be saved." Zong asked again, "What is the country of Kunming?" Gan replied, "It is the capital of the Wei dynasty, located in Ye." You were once a Grand Attendant in the founding state, how could you forget so soon? You and your family in Kunming are all well, the virtuous young lady, Xiao Xiuniang, has a piece dedicated to you, which I recited to your father-in-law yesterday. The poem says:

Remembering Father abandoned Daughter and did not return home, he did not become a Shizhong but a minor official. He endured hardships, abandoned glory, and wished Father would remember and meet early, with me, buy plums and sweet melons. After reciting, he suddenly couldn't help but cry, and wrote a chapter to send to the shy and delicate lady: Remembering Daughter's appearance, thinking of Daughter's heart, looking at Daughter but not seeing, tears wet the collar. Times are different, world is changed, hard to meet; abandoned and thanked this life, should seek out. Later, Wang Can and Xu Gan were kind and bid farewell to Zong. Then carried the five volumes of Liu Zhen's collection, and detailed the encounter with Wang Can and Xu Gan, also saying the previous life was Liu Zhen. The county magistrate saw the poems after Liu Zhen's death, and was greatly surprised, saying, "Cannot let Liu Gonggan be a minor official." Immediately released, treated with courtesy. Later, did not know where Zong was, the collection was also lost. People at the time encouraged their children, all said, "Even dead Liu Zhen still protected living Gu Zong, how can one not strive to improve!"

During the reign of Emperor Jing of Northern Zhou, the tribal leader of Juyan, Bo Du Gu Di, was known for his oppressive behavior, extravagant lifestyle, and love for pleasure. He lived in great splendor. Suddenly, several people arrived at his door, and one of them first presented a card, saying, “I am the leader of the tribe, Cheng Duo Shou.” He then hurriedly entered. Bo Du Gu Di asked, “Why did you name the tribe?” Cheng Duo Shou replied, “We several people are all unique, and our names are not specially created. There are those surnamed Ma, Pi, Lu, Xiong, Zhang, Wei, and Ban, but all of us are named Shou. Only my leader’s name is Duo Shou.” Bo Du Gu Di said, “You all seem like court jesters, what do you understand?” Cheng Duo Shou replied, “We are skilled in playing with bowls and pearls.
