We do not love the common, and our words are all based on classical principles.” Bo Du Gu Di was greatly delighted and said, “I have never seen anything like this before.” One of the jesters stepped forward and said, “We are all hungry, happy and content, with our skin wrapped around our bodies three times. If the host’s food is not sufficient, we will not stop opening our mouths.” Bo Du Gu Di was pleased and ordered more food to be brought. One of them said, “I will perform a trick of the big and small, the beginning and the end.” He then had tall people swallow short people, and fat people swallow thin people, until two people were left. The taller one then said, “Please perform the beginning and the end.” He then spat out one person, and the one who spat out another person. One after another, they spat out, and the number of people was restored. Bo Du Gu Di was greatly surprised and heavily rewarded and sent them away.

The next day, he came again and played tricks as before. For half a month in succession, Bone Low was quite troubled and could not prepare food. All the musicians were angry and said, "Master, we should use you and your family as subjects for our illusions. Please let us borrow your son and daughter to try it out." They then swallowed Bone Low's children, nieces, nephews, and other relatives into their stomachs. Those inside the stomachs all cried out for mercy. Bone Low, terrified, descended from the steps and bowed, appealing pitifully for his family. The musicians all laughed and said, "There is no need to worry; this is harmless." They then expelled the relatives, who were completely as before.

Bone Low was deeply enraged and sought an opportunity to kill him. He then ordered a secret investigation. They discovered that the person had disappeared at the site of an ancient house foundation. Upon hearing this, Bone Low ordered the site to be excavated. After digging several feet deep, they found a large wooden cage beneath the broken tiles and debris. Inside the cage were thousands of leather bags. Beside the cage was some grain and wheat, which turned to ash upon contact. In the cage, they found bamboo slips with faded and unreadable text, except for three faint characters that resembled the word "陵" (mound). Bone Low realized that the bags were supernatural and intended to burn them. The bags cried out from within the cage, saying, "We are doomed and will soon be destroyed. We have been preserved here because Commander Li left mercury with us. We are the grain bags of Commander Li Shaoqing. The house collapsed and crushed us, and over many years, we have now been granted a new life, taken by the god of Juanyuan Mountain to become entertainers. We beg the god to have mercy and not to destroy us. From now on, we will not disturb the high and noble." Bone Low coveted the mercury and burned all the bags. The bags emitted sounds of suffering and lamentation, and blood flowed and splattered. After the burning, the halls, corridors, doors, and windows of Bone Low's house were filled with sounds of sorrow and pain, just as they had been when the bags were burned, for more than a month without stopping. That year, Bone Low's entire family fell ill and died, one after another, with no survivors remaining after a year. The mercury also disappeared.

In the year of Wenming, Liu Feng, an official of Jingling, spent the night at an empty inn in Yiling. Under the bright moonlight, he took a rest. Suddenly, a young lady appeared from the west pavilion, her demeanor graceful and her appearance beautiful. She sang softly and walked leisurely, slowly reaching the central pavilion. She turned and ordered a maidservant, saying, "Zi Sui, fetch the flower mat from the west hall, and also invite Aunt Liu Six, Aunt Uncle Fourteen, and Miss Qiaoqiao from the south neighbor, along with Ouyi the servant. Tell them that the wind and moon here are beautiful enough for enjoyment. Playing the lute and reciting poetry is a great thing. Even though there is a judge in Jingling, this person is already asleep under the bright moon, so there's no need to avoid him." Soon, three young ladies arrived, accompanied by a child, all exceptionally beautiful. Zi Sui then laid the flower mat in the courtyard, and they bowed and took their seats. At the table were placed a rhino horn wine vessel, an ivory ladle, a green carpet flower cup, and a white glass cup. The fragrant wine could be smelled far away. The young ladies chatted, sang, and recited poetry with clear and graceful voices. One of the young ladies was a magistrate, and another was a low-ranking official. The magistrate's daughter raised her cup to pour wine and said, "May Aunt Three live as long as Mount Zhi Guo, and Aunt Six live as long as Aunt Three. May Aunt Liu's husband become a judge in Taishan Prefecture, and Miss Qiaoqiao marry the crown prince of Zhiyu Kingdom, and Ouyi become the prime minister of Zhiyu Kingdom. May my three or four companions all become clerks in the Department of Literature in the underworld, or else marry the sixth and seventh sons of King Pingdeng. Then my lifelong aspirations would be fulfilled." They all laughed and said, "We must also share a toast with Madam Cai."

The Recorder, who was light-hearted and quick-witted, outdid everyone else and decided to fine Lady Cai, saying, "Uncle Liu is talented and handsome, why didn't you give him the role of Five-Path Master, leaving him to pretend as a Judge, fearing Aunt Six would be unhappy, so he must drink a cup deeply." Lady Cai immediately took the cup and said, "I truly know I am being punished, but it's because Uncle Liu is old and has weak eyesight, and he might not be able to read the important yellow paper documents, causing a problem for the high-ranking official. Drinking a cup is no harm." The other ladies all laughed heartily. Then another lady stood up, passing on a verbal command, still pulling out a green hairpin, and said quickly, "The green hairpin must be passed, if it's not passed, the command is invalid and you will be punished." The command was, "Old Master Lu has a good mind, a good mind Old Master Lu." The command was passed around several times, and then the order was given for Lady Zi Sui to sit down and recite the command. Lady Zi Sui, who was usually hesitant and slow to speak, when it was her turn, could only repeat, "Old Master Lu, Old Master Lu." All the ladies laughed and said, "In the past, General He Ruobing played with Cao Luan, the high-ranking official, because he was old, stuttered, and was bald, so he invented this command."

After the third watch, everyone played the qin and zhu, singing in unison and taking turns. They sang: "Tomorrow, the wind will be clear, and this fine evening will bring us together. The Milky Way shifts easily, and joy is fleeting. With green cups and jade ladles, I pour wine for you. If we don't drink tonight, when will we find joy?" Another song was sung: "Willow, willow, swaying gently in the wind. In a dream in the west pavilion, a beautiful woman lies with her green curtains fluttering, her hair or garment flowing like a thousand strands." Another song was sung: "Jade doors and golden lamps, I wish to accompany the king. In the Han Palace at Handan, the music of gold, stone, silk, and bamboo resounds. The beautiful women of Wei and Qin stand in rows on either side. Their colorful silk garments and green brows and red makeup are a sight to behold. The king's joy is evident in his gaze, and the women dance and sing for him. May the king always be happy and free from misfortune."

The song ended, and it was already the fourth watch of the night. Suddenly, a man in a yellow robe, with horns on his head and an imposing appearance, entered and bowed, saying, "King Bodi summons Lady, please come quickly!" The young ladies all rose and accepted the command, then replied, "We did not know the king had summoned us; we just came here to admire the moon. Having been called by the king, how could we not hasten to his presence?" They then ordered the servants to clear the table. One of them coughed loudly, and when they looked around the courtyard, there was nothing left. The next morning, upon careful examination, they found several jade hairpins. They intended to show these to others, but did not know what they were. ○ Dong Shen In the first year of the Daoyi era of the Sui Dynasty, Dong Shen, an assistant official in Yanzhou, was upright and knowledgeable in legal matters. If any subordinate official used the law unfairly, he would boldly advise them. Even if punished, he would not be afraid and would wait for justice to be served before retreating. One day, when he was free and returning home, he met a messenger dressed in yellow who said, "The Lord of Tai Mountain has called you to serve as a recorder, do you know this?" The messenger then showed a document from his chest. The document read, "Dong Shen is renowned for his reputation and solid achievements, and his case files are well-prepared. He is to be appointed as a recorder in the right department." The seal was clear, and the document was signed "A." Dong Shen asked the messenger, "The lord calls me, how could I not go? But I do not know the lord's name." The messenger replied, "Recorder, do not worry; you will know at the lord's place." The messenger then took a large sack, placed Dong Shen inside, carried him out of the city, and sealed his eyes with mud.

Once Fan Shen's eyes were opened, he saw nothing, completely unaware of how far he had traveled. Suddenly, he heard a loud call: "Fan Shen, pursue Dong Shen to his destination." The messenger replied, "Yes," and hurriedly entered. The magistrate asked, "Where is the recorder we were pursuing?" The messenger replied, "The underworld is secretive, and there is fear of leakage or exposure. Previously, I requested the Left Department to hide the shadow in a cloth bag and contain him." The magistrate laughed and said, "To send one Fan Shen to pursue one Dong Shen, and to take a cloth bag from the Left Department to contain a recorder from the Right Department—this can be said to be able to prevent caution." He immediately ordered the recorder to be written out, the mud from his eyes removed, and then granted him a blue silk robe, a fish whisker tablet, and leopard skin boots, which were quite mottled. He invited Fan Shen to ascend to the secondary platform, ordered the attendants to fetch a couch for him to sit on, and said, "Because of your fairness, I have this request. Now, there are six people, including the governor of Minzhou, Lingfu Shi, who are placed in a no-judgment prison. According to a decree from the celestial department, since Shi is a third-degree relative of Lady Taiyuan, the punishment is reduced by three degrees according to the regulations. Yesterday, one hundred and twenty criminals, including Cheng Zhu, cited examples, causing noisy and chaotic litigation that could not be stopped. The names have already been listed and submitted to the celestial department. The celestial department considered that punishment should be lenient in doubt and also ordered a reduction of two degrees. I fear that later people will cite many examples. What do you think should be done?" Fan Shen said, "Water reflects beauty and ugliness, and people do not get angry because it is extremely clear and impartial. How much more so for heavenly laws? How can they be lenient with wickedness? But I am just a minor official, have never written anything, and even though I know it is wrong, my words lack coherence. The scholar Zhang Shentong from Changzhou is eloquent and outstanding and is qualified to serve as your secretary." The magistrate ordered a summons to be issued.

Soon, Shentong arrived and said, "This is easy. You should judge and submit a report." The magistrate said, "Yi should speak well for me." Immediately, he was appointed as Left Censor and was also given clothes like those of Dong Shen, each was given one black fox, and each time they went out, they rode it. Shentong judged and said, "Heaven is originally impartial. The law should be uniform. If one grants mercy, it would be to indulge in wicked deeds. Linghu Shi was previously ordered to reduce the sentence, which is the same as a private request; Cheng Zhu later submitted a petition, and there is a difference in doubt. If one opens the door to successive reductions, it would be to lose the public's opinion. Please follow the previous order to send to the hell of no return, and record the report to be submitted to the heavenly court." There was a man in yellow clothes who took the report and went. Soon, he returned with a heavenly decree saying, "The submitted report contains many heretical things. In accordance with the master's will, it should only be followed. The *Rites of Zhou* have the Eight Deliberations, the first is deliberation for relatives. The *Yuanhua Kui* in *Shi Chongfu* also says there is no one who is not a relative. The rules are clearly established, what is there to not accept? How can the great virtue of Taiyuan not protect three grades of relatives? Still dare to violate, there must be punishment. The magistrate should be punished by not wearing purple robes for sixty years. The rest should follow the previous disposition." The magistrate was very angry at Shentong and said, "You speak out of sentiment, making me suffer punishment." He immediately ordered his attendants to take a piece of flesh and plug one of his ears, so he could no longer hear. Shentong pleaded, "I beg you to judge and submit again. If you do not agree, I will willingly accept the punishment again."

The magistrate said, "If you remove my sins, then give me one more chance." Shentong then ruled, "Heaven and earth are vast, but they are fundamentally impartial; if it is to serve the ruler, how could one achieve unity? If one wishes to change the law based on emotions, it will inevitably lead to falsehood and the loss of truth. Before the ancient times, people were still very simple; in the middle ages, one began to hear about various forms of kinship. How can one allow the ancient heart of nurturing all things to give rise to Confucius's sigh at the sacrificial ceremony? If there is no distinction in kinship, it is not fair or just; why should one follow it? Please forgive the offense of hearing unpleasant truths, and I dare to recommend a remedy for the heart. Perhaps by examining the facts, fairness can be achieved. I ask that Linghu Shi and others be dealt with according to the proper law. And record the case for submission to the heavenly court." A yellow-robed messenger took it away, and shortly thereafter another heavenly edict arrived, saying, "Upon reviewing what has been submitted, it is indeed appropriate. The magistrate can be appointed as a deputy to the Six Heavenly Officials. Linghu Shi, Cheng Zhu, and others will be dealt with according to the proper law." The magistrate was pleased and said to Shentong, "Without you, this case could not have been resolved." He then ordered his attendants to cut a piece of flesh from his own ear and had a small child divide it into one ear, which he placed on Shentong's forehead, saying, "I block one of your ears and give you three ears; how is that?" He also said to Shentong, "I greatly rely on your recommendation of worthy individuals to complete my good deeds, but I cannot keep you here for long; I will reward you with an additional year of life. You will live for twenty-one years in total." He then immediately sent him back home.

The envoy sealed two people with clay and sent each in a cloth bag to their homes, as if suddenly written out. He then asked his wife, and she replied, "Your spirit has left for over ten days." Shen indeed died twenty-one years later. Shen Tong felt itching on his forehead for several days, and suddenly a new ear grew out, making him have three ears in total, with the new one being especially intelligent. People at the time laughed and said, "Heaven has a nine-headed bird, and earth has a three-eared scholar." They also called him the Scholar with a Chicken's Comb. Shen initially called the magistrate "neighbor," but later realized that Jù was his neighbor. Volume Three Chapter on Emperor Xuanzong's Visit to Guangling In the eighteenth year of the Kaiyuan era, on the night of the full moon in the first month, the emperor said to Leaf Master, "The grandeur of the four directions is displayed on this night. Teacher, do you know where it is most splendid?" The master replied, "The lights and candles are splendid, and various performances are set up. Men and women vie for beauty, their faces adorned with rouge and powder. There is none more splendid than Guangling in the world." The emperor asked, "What method can allow me to see it once?" The master said, "Anyone can wait for it, why only Your Majesty?" Suddenly, a rainbow bridge rose in front of the palace, its wooden pavilion built on empty space, its railings like paintings. The master said, "The bridge is ready, please go, but do not look back." Thus, the emperor stepped onto it, followed by Tai Zhen and the attendants, including Gao Lishi, Huang Fanchuo, and dozens of musicians. Step by step, they rose higher, as if ascending into the clouds.

In a moment, they had already arrived at Guangling. The moonlight was as bright as daylight, the streets as straight as a string, and the splendid arrangement of temples and observatories, with the lights shining brightly on the palaces and halls. The men and women were splendidly dressed, as if they were walking in a transformation, and all looked up and said, "The immortal appeared in a cloud of five colors." They then danced and bowed, and the streets were filled with people. The emperor was greatly pleased, and he said, "This is truly Guangling?" The teacher replied, "Please order the music officials to play the *Nirvana Robe* melody, which can be verified later." Then the music was made in the clouds, and the people who watched and listened were all over the place. When the music ended, the emperor's thoughts were about to return, and in a short while, they had already arrived at the palace gates. The emperor was extremely happy. Some people said the immortal teacher's magic was extremely subtle, temporarily dazzling the eyes and ears. For a long time, there was no decision. After several weeks, Guangling reported, "On the 15th day of the first month at midnight, there was an immortal riding a colorful cloud coming from the west, arriving at the Daoist platform of Xianggan Temple, towering tens of feet high. After a while, they played the *Nirvana Robe* melody again, and when the music ended, the immortal went west. Officials, gentlemen, and ladies all looked up. This is because Your Majesty's filial piety and sincerity have moved the heavens, and Your Majesty's virtuous nature is clearly evident. Your Majesty's name is recorded in the immortal books, and Your Majesty's virtue surpasses that of emperors. Otherwise, why would auspicious clouds appear in the morning of the first year's court ceremony, and why would immortal music be played in the humble abode of a lowly official? Those who wear robes only hear of Your Majesty's virtue, and those who sing the *South Wind* only touch people's hearts. How could they be compared to a prosperous dynasty?" The emperor read the report and was greatly pleased, and then believed the teacher was not lying.

Governor Yuan Hong'er of Zhu Ya, nicknamed Kuanglang, was twenty years old. From birth, he had a fondness for books and enjoyed a quiet life. He lived in a separate courtyard and was quite capable of speaking in a profound manner. Once, while out in the wild, he saw a green bird and ordered it to be captured. Yuan greatly enjoyed this bird. On a clear night under the bright moon, he lit candles and recited poetry: "Dew wets the grass in the cold pond, the moon reflects on the clear river." Suddenly, he lost track of where the green bird was. He saw a pair of maidens with braided hair standing to his left and said, "This piece by Master Yuan is very excellent, but I wonder if my 27th son, Feng Lang, can handle the complex rhyming. People write three-character and four-character poetic lines, one line starting and one line ending. The spring poem says: 'Flowers fall, butterflies dance, why do people suffer so much, it's enough to cause worry and distress.' There are over a hundred such poems, but I cannot remember them all."

Kuāláng was greatly surprised by it and said, "Who is this young lady in service, and how did she come to be here? And you, Fēngláng, can I invite you here?" The servant girl replied, "I am the twenty-seventh daughter of a certain king, originally named Fěicuì. I was once transformed to travel and made to serve as the son of Yuánlángzǐ, Luódé." Kuāláng then said, "Fēngláng is not far from here. Just prepare the host's courtesy, and in a short while, Fēngláng will arrive immediately." Soon, Fěicuì arrived and said, "Fēngláng is at the door." Upon seeing a young man, about twenty years old, with gentle and refined speech and graceful and spirited demeanor, they bowed and yielded, sat down, discussed Confucian and historical texts, spent the entire day and night together, and got along well. Kuāláng said, "Since you reside in a high position, you should explain this to me." Fēngláng replied, "Píngzhòng will have vegetable dishes to invite you the next day, but I am not from my original residence; I am currently staying in Lángyá." They repeatedly bid farewell sincerely.

By the next day after noon, a little boy came forward to bow and say, "Lord Feng has sent me to deliver a letter, ordering Second Brother to lead the way." Opening the letter to read it, it said, "On a fine day with abundant vitality, I desire a good meeting. Standing on a high platform, I wait eagerly for your presence. Just ride eastward, my lord." Lord Kua immediately mounted his horse and followed. After traveling ten miles, he suddenly saw springs and stones winding through, strange flowers growing in pairs, and a guesthouse that was spacious and grand, filled with the most exquisite treasures. The door hung with green silk curtains, below which was about a foot thick, all heated with animal charcoal. Lord Kua met with Lord Feng and was just about to look at it in wonder. Pingzhong called back a little boy and said, "The servant who holds the brush, why didn't you have the fire-washed curtain ready? Why is it not finished when the guest arrives?" Just remove the fire, and the curtain color is even fresher. Not long after sitting, four more people came out of the house, all scholars of refined taste. Lord Feng said, "The host's Second Brother, Third Brother, Fourth Brother, and Sixth Son, their names are Quan, Tui, Wei, and Huai."

During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. She was all dressed in pearls and jade, carrying a large tray filled with a myriad of delicacies, none of which were lacking. Wang Huai said, "There is some family entertainment; please enjoy this." Then, a group of young women and foreign entertainers performed a ritual or dance, bowing to the officials in a graceful manner. Wang Huai pointed to two courtesans and said, "They are the concubines of Shi Chong, known as Xian'e Nang, whose fame is second only to that of Lu Zhu." Then the string and wind instruments played together, sounding clear and resonant. By evening, the Wang brothers had drunk themselves to sleep. Feng Sheng said to Kua Lang, "This is also enough for wealth and honor, but since your father is the governor, it is not yet grand enough." Kua Lang said, "Not because of my lowly status, but how can I achieve it?" Feng Sheng said, "If you can truly unite in heart, I will ask to be your messenger. My humble home has an aunt who is beautiful, virtuous, and skilled in music. Please consider her, Mr. Yuan." Kua Lang said, "I am only afraid that it will be difficult to become a fish under the Dragon Gate." Feng Sheng then went inside to inform the respected elders of the Wang family, and upon returning, said, "It is agreed! Tomorrow is auspicious; it will be the day of the wedding." Kua Lang was greatly pleased and agreed.

The Next Day, the brothers of the Wang family had just set up the arrangements in the hall, with cushions, beds, and curtains, brightly dazzling the eyes. When the young man entered, a young woman under the curtain said, "Yuan Lang's movements are still as graceful as when he was carrying books to school." Another elderly servant passed by, and the young man bowed in thanks and looked at him. The servant then laughed and said, "It has been a long time since Qinfen has had milk, why does Yuan Lang look at it!" As dusk approached, all the attendants arrived, and a servant held a note with a poem to hurry the bridegroom. The young man then wrote a poem in response: Beautiful flowers naturally have the warmth of spring, not coinciding with the red makeup of the fair lady, disrupting her graceful form. If one uses the face powder of He Lang, one can borrow as much of his radiance as needed. The other auspicious rituals were all fully prepared. There were many poems, but not all of them can be remembered. Only the poem about the fan can be recalled: The round fan with a new square design, golden flowers illuminating the brocade mat. How can one see it under the lamp, even more so, encountering the person in the moonlight. The young man's wife is extraordinarily beautiful, surpassing the realm, with graceful and elegant manners. Her pet name is Congcong, and her formal name is Xie. The second wife or concubine is also similarly graceful and beautiful, quick-witted and good at joking, and gave a poem to Yuan Lang saying:

A beautiful woman must often be worried, for she is frequently sought after by unattractive men outside her door. Yesterday, I encountered her at the feet of the Buddha statue; how could I have come to this place today? Later, we sat under the shade of a paulownia tree, and Feng Pingzhong played the qin. He turned to the arrogant man and said, "Uncle, do you have any words of advice for me?" The arrogant man immediately composed a poem: The guest opened the jade qin, and the tall paulownia tree chased away the heat. The qin strings played a piece of music, and the white clouds floated up. Why go to the east of Cangwu? Play the qin with a heart full of resentment. The arrogant man daily indulged in his conceit, thus he had no thoughts of returning. Suddenly, he felt his wife was sad, so he prepared his traveling clothes. The arrogant man asked Feng Pingzhong, who replied, "My father-in-law is Jin Shizhong Wang Ji, who has been the governor of Jiaozhou for a long time. Recently, he was appointed as the governor of Bingzhou. If you are here with your respected father-in-law, we cannot go together. Then we will have a final farewell here." His wife wept and said, "We were originally on different paths, and it was unexpected that we met. This separation today is also the fault of Feng Lang's second brother." Then he heard the calls of people outside. He went out, looked back, and found everything had turned gray and nothing was seen. The magistrate sought for him for nearly a year. When he arrived, for several months he was still dazed. Often, he rushed to the place where he met her, but saw nothing else. He cried and withdrew, finally returning to normal after a year.

A Former Candidate Zhang Zuo Zhang Zuo, a former candidate for the imperial examinations, once told his uncle: When I was young and traveling south to the Hu Du region, I was walking in the outskirts when I saw an old man riding a green donkey. The donkey's four legs were white, and he had a bag made of deer skin on his back. His face was very joyful, and his demeanor was extraordinary. The old man came from a side path that met the main road. I was very surprised by him and asked where he had come from. The old man just smiled and did not answer. After asking him repeatedly, the old man suddenly became angry and shouted, "Young man, how dare you press me! Am I a thief or a murderer? Why do you need to ask where I came from?" I apologized humbly, saying, "I admire the lofty conduct of a sage and wish to learn from you. Why do you blame me so severely?" The old man replied, "I have no skills to teach you, only the gift of longevity. You will probably mock my poverty and want to laugh at my aspirations." He then whipped his donkey and hurried away, and I also spurred my horse to follow, and we both arrived at an inn. The old man lay down with his deer skin bag as a pillow and had not yet fallen asleep. I was very tired and took out wine to drink, and I invited him, saying, "Let us share this small amount of wine together." The old man jumped up and said, "This is exactly what I like, how did you understand my meaning?" After drinking, I noticed that his face was very pleased. I then politely asked, "I have little taste, I wish for the teacher to impart words to broaden my knowledge and vision, but I dare not hope for much." The old man said, "What I have seen is the Liang, Chen, Sui, and Tang dynasties. The wise and foolish, the well-governed and chaotic, are all recorded in the national history. However, let me tell you what I have personally recorded."

When I lived in Qi during the Yuwen Zhou period, I was a native of Fufeng, with the surname Shen and given name Zong. I admired the martial prowess of Qi Shen Wu and therefore changed my name to Huan. At eighteen, I followed General Yan Gong Yu Jin in his campaign against Emperor Yuan of Liang in Jingzhou, where I was captured by a great general. Soon after, I dreamed of two men in blue robes who said to me, “Lü goes the natural lifespan; people face the master of longevity.” After waking, I went to a dream interpreter in the market of Jiangling. The dream interpreter said to me, “‘Lü goes’ is the character for ‘return,’ and ‘people face the master’ is the character for ‘stay.’ Does this mean that you will live long if you stay?” At that time, troops were stationed in Jiangling, and I then submitted my petition to the military officer Tuoba Lie, who agreed.

Then he went to consult a dream interpreter and said, "If you stay, everything will be fine, but is there a method to prolong life?" The dream interpreter replied, "You were previously the man named Xue Juncao from Zitong. You enjoyed taking the wood essence powder, often sought out unusual books, and daily recited a hundred sheets of Huang-Lao. You moved to live at the foot of Crane Chirping Mountain, where you had a thatched cottage with three rooms. In front of the house, flowers and bamboo were planted in rows, and the area was surrounded by springs and stones." On the 15th day of the 8th lunar month, Xue Juncao sang loudly and drank alone. As he became drunk and cheerful, he said aloud, "Xue Juncao is so reclusive and indifferent. Why hasn't any extraordinary person come to visit?" Suddenly, he heard the sound of carriages and horses in his ears. He then leaned back and thought of sleeping. As soon as he reached his mat, a small carriage appeared. The carriage had a red wheel and a green canopy, driven by red calves, each about two or three inches tall. He did not know how difficult it was for them to come out of his ear. There were two children in the carriage, wearing green headbands and blue cloaks, also about two or three inches tall. They leaned on the carriage's front rail, called the driver, stepped on the wheel, and helped Xue Juncao down. They said to him, "We have come from the Land of Douchuan. We heard you singing loudly under the moon, and the tone was very clear and piercing. We privately admire and respect you and wish to engage in a clear and profound discussion." Xue Juncao said, "How can there be a country inside my ear, and how can the people of that country all be tiny insects?" The children replied, "Why is that so? Our country is no different from yours. If you don't believe us, why not come with us on a journey? Perhaps we can make you stay, and then you would no longer suffer from the pain of birth and death."

A child leaned forward to show the gentlemen, who observed it and found themselves in a different world, lush with flowers and plants, with well-constructed buildings connected by clear springs and green bamboo, winding around fragrant fields. They touched their ears and were thrown into a grand city, with walls, towers, and palaces reaching the limits of beauty. The gentlemen wandered, unsure of where to go, and saw the two children they had met earlier standing by them. The children said to the gentlemen, "This country is as large as yours. Since you have arrived here, why not follow us to pay respects to Lord Meng Xuanzhen." Lord Meng Xuanzhen resided in a grand hall, with walls and steps all decorated with gold and jade, and hanging jade-green curtains. In the middle, Lord Meng Xuanzhen sat alone, wearing a robe adorned with patterns of clouds,霞 (霞), sun, and moon, and wearing a high ceremonial hat with tassels equal to the height of his body. Four jade boys stood on both sides, one holding a white fly whisk and one holding an ivory wish-fulfilling object. The two gentlemen entered, bowed and knelt, daring not to look up. A person with a high hat, long hair, and a red silk robe proclaimed on blue paper, "Originally divided from the great primordial, the country is already a hundred billion. You have fallen to the lower realms, lowly and humble beings, gradually reaching this place, truly due to a mysterious union. How much more so, then, for you who are pure and sincere in your actions, in harmony with the true ruler. High-ranking officials and rich titles should be enjoyed appropriately. You can be the Chief Recorder." The gentlemen bowed and danced out of the door, and immediately there were three or four people in yellow cloaks, leading them to a certain office. Among the documents, many were not recognized, and there were no monthly requests or receptions. But if they thought of something, the attendants would know beforehand and immediately provide it. Taking a break, they climbed the tower to look far and suddenly felt a desire to return home. They composed a poem, saying:

Soft winds and harmonious, beautiful scenery; the fragrance of flowers fills the forest and pond. Climbing high and gazing longingly, truly beautiful, but not my hometown. I recited a poem to two children, and they angrily said, "We thought you had a natural disposition that was calm and secluded, but you have not yet shed your vulgar and ordinary manners. What are you thinking about?" They then swiftly chased you, as if you were falling into the earth. Looking up, you saw yourself falling from the ear of the child, already in your old residence. Following their gaze, the children also disappeared. I then asked the neighbors, and they said, "You have been missing for seven or eight years." You were there for a few months. Not long after, you died, and then you were born into the Shen family, which is your current body.

The soothsayer further said, "In my previous life, I was a child emerging from your ear. Because you loved the Dao in your past life, you were able to reach the Douxuan State, but you have not fully transcended worldly thoughts, so you cannot achieve immortality. However, you will live a thousand years as a result. I have given you a talisman, and you should return now." "He then spat out a red silk cloth about a foot long and instructed you to swallow it. The soothsayer then resumed his child form and disappeared. From then on, you no longer suffered from illness, and you traveled around the famous mountains of the world. Until now, nearly two hundred years have passed. However, I have seen many strange things and have recorded them in a deer hide." He then opened his bag and took out two large scrolls, the characters of which were quite fine. The left scroll could not be read, so he asked the old man to explain it. He briefly recounted about ten events, half of which were clearly recorded. This scroll contains eight events, none of which are different from what the old man had said. As the night was about to break, the assistant went to sleep, and upon waking up, the old man was gone. Several days later, someone saw him at the Carbon Valley Spring, and the old man said, "Please convey my regards to Zhang Jun." The left person hurriedly searched for him, but he was gone again. At that time, it was during the Zhenyuan era. ○Xiao Zhizhong During the reign of the Tang Dynasty, Xiao Zhizhong, the Grand Secretary, was the Governor of Jizhou in the first year of Jingyun. He planned to hunt on the day of the winter solstice and set up traps for a grand occasion.

The day before, a woodcutter was chopping wood in Huoshan when he contracted malaria and could not return home. He therefore took refuge in a cave, moaning and unable to sleep. As night was ending, he seemed to hear a rustling sound of human voices. At first, he thought bandits were approaching, so he crawled into the woods. The mountain moon was very bright, and there was a man over ten feet tall with three horns on his nose, clothed in a leopard skin, whose eyes flashed like lightning. He roared towards the valley. Soon, there were tigers, rhinos, deer, pigs, foxes, rabbits, pheasants, and geese gathered around for about a hundred steps. The tall man then declared, "I am the messenger of Xuanming, carrying out the order of the Northern Emperor. Tomorrow is the day of the winter solstice, and Xiao the magistrate will follow the season to hunt and sacrifice. You will die by arrows, spears, nets, clubs, dogs, and eagles." After speaking, the animals all cowered in fear, as if pleading for mercy. The tiger and the old elk both bowed their knees and spoke to the tall man, saying, "Our lives are at your disposal. However, Xiao the magistrate is kind-hearted and does not intend to harm creatures; he is simply following the seasonal command. If there is a slight reason, then it will stop. Does the messenger have no way to save us?" The messenger replied, "It is not my intention to kill you; I am merely fulfilling the divine command by announcing your fates. From now on, you must fend for yourselves. However, I have heard that Elder Yan in Eastern Valley is good at strategy; you may seek his help." The animals all rejoiced and called out. The messenger then headed east, and the animals followed him.

The Hourly Wage Earner's Illness Had Slightly Improved, So He Followed Along to Check on Him. Upon Arriving at the Eastern Valley, There Were Several Thatched Huts, And Inside One, A Taoist Priest Was Soundly Sleeping With a Tiger Skin Hung on a Frame. Startled Awake, He Saw the Messenger and Said, "Long Separated, I Often Think and Hope. Today You Have Come Here, Is It Not to Determine the Fate of the Beasts Through Hunting and Punishment?" The Messenger Replied, "Just As Your Eminence Has Guessed. But They All Seek Help from the Fourth Elder Brother, And the Fourth Elder Brother Should Plan for Them." The Tiger and the Old Deer Then Kneeled and Pleaded, And the Taoist Priest Said, "The County Magistrate Xiao Always Uses People, But He Always Cares About Their Hunger and Cold. If You Pray to Tengliu for Snowfall and Xun'er for Wind, He Will No Longer Go Hunting. I Received a Letter from Tengliu Yesterday, Knowing That He Has Lost His Wife. Also Heard That the Fifth Daughter of the Suoquan Family, A Singer, Was Dismissed Due to Jealousy. If You Can Find a Beautiful Woman to Marry Him, Then Snow Will Immediately Fall. And Xun'er Loves to Drink, If You Can Find Strong Wine to Bribe Him, Then the Wind Will Immediately Come." Two Foxes Claimed to Be Very Charming and Could Get Them. "The Third Sister of Cui Zhi, the County Magistrate of Hedong, Is Beautiful and Graceful. Lu the Household Officer of Jiangzhou Is Good at Brewing Strong Wine, And When His Wife Gives Birth, There Will Certainly Be Fine Wine." After Speaking, He Left. All the Animals Had Joyful Sounds. The Taoist Priest Then Said to the Messenger, "Remembering When I Was in the Celestial Realm, How Could I Have Thought I Would Be a Beast for a Thousand Years, Depressed and Unfulfilled. Just Have a Chapter Called 'Expressing Aspirations.'" He Then Recited:

Once a fairy, now a tiger, wandering on the edge of darkness, feet soaked in wind and rain. Further, the speckled fur is draped over my body, a thousand years in the empty mountains, myriad sufferings. "Yet, the punishment of exile has been completed, only eleven days remain until I return to the Purple Palace. Having stayed here for a long time, parting is not without sorrow. Therefore, I inscribe several lines on the wall, so that future generations may know that I once lived here." He wrote on the north wall: "Below the dark, eight hundred billion cycles of the Heavenly Stems and Earthly Branches, Master Dan Fei, Yan Hanzi, exiled to the middle heaven, covered in speckled fur, sixty cycles of the Heavenly Stems and Earthly Branches, eating blood and drinking from streams, among apes, descending to the impure world, in the first year of Jingyun, ascending to Taiyi."

A Person Who Earns by the Hour A person who earns by the hour, having always been skilled in reciting books, secretly memorized something. Shortly afterward, an old fox carried a beautiful woman, who was just coming of age, her red sleeves wiping her eyes, her makeup unfinished and charmingly seductive. Another fox carried two bottles of fine wine, their aroma strong and pungent. The person named Yan Si, then placed the woman and the wine bottles into separate bags, wrote two red talismans, sprinkled water on them, and the talismans flew away. Fearing that he would be seen, the person quickly found a way to return. Before dawn, a violent storm arrived, lasting all day, and as a result, the magistrate no longer went hunting. ○ Li Ruiyan Li Ruiyan, an official from Hanzhong, said: During the Tianbao era, there was a scholar surnamed Cui who served as a military officer in Bashu. He died shortly after arriving in Chengdu. At the time, the governor, Zhang Qiu Jinqiong, felt pity for his young widow, who had no place to go, and so provided her with a summer house in Qingcheng Mountain. Additionally, because of her beauty, he considered marrying her but had no plan, so he told his wife, "As a wife of a feudal lord, why not host a grand banquet and invite female guests from within a 500-li radius? All of them can be invited." The wife was very pleased. The governor then ordered his officials to invite all the women within 500 li to gather in Chengdu the same day, intending to keep the widow during the gathering, but he did not know that she had already been married to his cousin-in-law, Lu Sheng. Lu Sheng, knowing the governor's intentions, had the widow feign illness and not attend, which greatly angered the governor, who then ordered his attendants to capture her.

Lu's uncle was just finishing his meal when soldiers and cavalry surrounded the house. Lu remained calm and composed, laughing and talking as usual, and was not at all concerned. After finishing his meal, he told the wife of the magistrate, "The intentions of Jianqiong are known. The lady cannot avoid going. In a short while, they will send white-colored clothes. You may wear them and go." After finishing his words, he rode a donkey out. The soldiers and cavalry tried to grab him but could not. He left slowly, and they could not catch up. Soon, he sent a young boy to carry a box, inside which were old blue skirts, white shirts, green cloaks, and red silk gauze. All of these were not things that ordinary people possessed. The wife of the magistrate wore them and went to Chengdu. All the young ladies arrived ahead of time, and Jianqiong peeped from behind the curtain. When the wife of the magistrate entered, radiant light surrounded her, her beauty shone out, and it was too dazzling to look directly at. Everyone present was awestruck and involuntarily stood up to bow. She returned home to eat and died three days later, with the red color fading away immediately.

Jianshao was greatly alarmed and prepared a report, which he submitted to the emperor. The emperor asked Zhang Guo, who replied, "I know about it, but I dare not speak. Please ask the old man of Qingcheng." The emperor immediately summoned Jianshao and requested him to find the old man of Qingcheng and bring him to the capital. Jianshao searched the areas around Qingcheng Mountain but found no trace of him. Only a pharmacist at a grass market said, "There are usually two men who come every day to buy yam, claiming they are sent by the old man." When these two men arrived, Jianshao ordered an official to follow them. They entered the mountain for several miles and came to a thatched cottage where the old man of Qingcheng sat upright with white hair, resting his head on a table. The official followed in and then announced the imperial edict, conveying Jianshao's intentions. The old man of Qingcheng said, "This must be the talkative little Zhang Guo." He then agreed to a specific time to arrive in the capital and ordered the report to be published first, refusing to travel by official carriage, and Jianshao followed his wishes. The envoy arrived at the Silver Pavilion, and the old man of Qingcheng also arrived. The emperor summoned them, and Zhang Guo was still standing by the side of the seat. Seeing the old man, he was shocked and bowed twice. The old man of Qingcheng scolded Zhang Guo, saying, "Why didn't you speak up! You sent someone far away to fetch me." Zhang Guo replied, "The little immortal dares not, waiting only for the words of the immortal elder." He then reported, "Lu Er's second uncle is the servant of the Lady Taiyuan, who went down to play and took his deceased magistrate's wife, who had a bit of immortal bone, as a concubine. Soon after, she stole the clothes of Lady Taiyuan and wore them, and has already been severely punished, becoming the ruler of the Pure Abodes Heaven. The deceased magistrate's wife, by wearing the clothes of Lady Taiyuan, has fallen into the realm of eternal punishment." After the report, the old man of Qingcheng strongly refused to stay, and the emperor allowed him to return. After leaving, no one knew where he was.

Governor Nan Zuan of Guanghan once told someone: During the era of great virtue, there was someone selected to be the supervisor of Tongzhou. His surname was Cui, but his name is forgotten. He rode a light horse to take up his post. As he went out through the Chunming Gate, he saw a man in a green robe riding a horse out. He did not know the man's name, so they bowed to each other and walked together. Slowly, he asked what the man's position was. The man in the green robe said, "I have just been appointed as the supervisor of Tongzhou." Cui said, "I have just been appointed to this position, are you not mistaken?" The man in the green robe smiled and did not answer. They continued walking together, and both said they were going to take up their posts. After traveling dozens of miles from Tongzhou, on a side path, officials came to greet them. The man in the green robe entered and said to Cui, "You are the officer of the yang aspect, and I am the officer of the yin aspect. Our paths diverge from here, should we not bid farewell?" Cui recorded this and immediately rode alongside the man in the green robe into the side path, and they eventually arrived at a city. The streets and government offices were also very grand and magnificent.

The green-robed official arrived at the hall and sat down with Cui Sheng to receive an audience. After announcing the presence of clerks, monks, and Taoists, he proceeded to handle litigants and prisoners, including Cui Sheng's wife. Cui Sheng was greatly surprised and asked the official, "How did my humble wife come to be here?" The official stepped back behind the large desk and instructed Cui Sheng to speak with his wife himself. The wife replied, "I was forced to come here and have been here for several days. You should plead with the recorder on my behalf." Cui Sheng immediately begged the official, who then ordered the clerk to hasten the release of Cui Sheng's wife. Cui Sheng asked his wife what crime she had committed to be brought here, and the official explained, "You left your family in Tongzhou. All the deceased from Tongzhou have been examined here. I am in charge of the Yangdao, and I am in charge of the Yindao." Cui Sheng stayed for half a day and then requested to return. The official ordered the clerk to see him off, saying, "Although there is a difference between Yang and Yin, we are both from Tongzhou. How can we not bid farewell to the local official?" The official also accompanied them to see them off, offering repeated hospitality, and waved his sleeves, instructing them to go down a side road. When Cui Sheng arrived in Tongzhou and asked his wife about her condition, she said, "I have been ill for seven or eight days, unconscious and unaware, with my spirit not recognizing the living. I only recovered a day ago." Cui Sheng calculated that it was the same day she was released. His wife did not remember seeing Cui Sheng in the Yindao, but when Cui Sheng mentioned it, she began to realize it was like a dream and did not clearly remember it either.

In the early years of the Kaihuang era of the Sui Dynasty, Hou Yu, a filial and honest official from Guangdu, entered the city and reached the outskirts of the Jiemeng Pass. Suddenly, he saw four yellow stones, all as large as a dou. Hou Yu loved them and collected them in a cage, carrying them on a donkey. He stopped to rest and looked at them, and they all turned into gold. Hou Yu sold the gold in the city and made a million coins, buying more than ten beautiful concubines, building a large mansion, and purchasing nearby good farmland and villas. Later, he went out for a spring outing, loading all his concubines and maidservants to follow. He got off the carriage and set up wine and food. Suddenly, an old man appeared, carrying a large bag and sitting down. Hou Yu became angry and scolded him, ordering the servants to escort him out. The old man did not move and was not angry, but instead took a full cup, ate roasted meat, and laughed, saying, "I came here to demand repayment of a debt from you. Do you not remember when you took my gold?" The old man then took more than ten of Hou Yu's concubines and threw them into his bag, which did not seem narrow at all. He carried them away, running like a bird. Hou Yu ordered the servants to chase him on horseback, but soon the old man disappeared. After that, Hou Yu's family gradually became poor, returning to their former circumstances. Ten years later, he returned to Sichuan and reached the Jiemeng Pass. He saw the old man from before, who was traveling with the concubines he had taken, accompanied by many attendants. When they saw Hou Yu, they all laughed. Hou Yu asked them, but they did not respond. When he pressured them, they disappeared again. He visited the area around the Jiemeng Pass, but there was no one like him, and he could not understand or measure what had happened. ○巴邛人 In Baqing, there was a man who, after drinking a cup of wine, suddenly transformed into a bird and flew away. The people of Baqing were amazed and followed him, but they could not catch him. He flew to a distant mountain and disappeared. The people of Baqing never saw him again.

A Man from Ba and Qiong There was a man from Ba and Qiong, whose name is unknown, who had an orange grove. After the frost, all the oranges were harvested, leaving two large oranges, each as big as a three-dou bowl. The people of Ba recorded this, and they were instructed to climb and pick the oranges, which were as heavy as ordinary oranges. When cut open, each orange contained two old men, with white hair and eyebrows, rosy and smooth skin, who all playfully interacted with each other, each about a foot long, chatting and laughing naturally. They were not frightened after being cut open, but they only gambled with each other. After the gambling, one old man said, "You owe me ten taels of the seventh daughter of the Dragon King of the Sea's hair, twelve strands of Zhijiong's forehead yellow, a purple silk shawl, two庾of the precious powder from the Red Platform Mountain, nine斛of the jade dust from Yingzhou, four zhou of the bone-marrow curing wine from Mother, and eight两of the dragon silk stockings from Mother's daughter Tai Ying. You will return them to me at Mr. Wang's Qingcheng Hermitage in the future." Another old man said, "Mr. Wang promised to come, but he never arrived. The joy in the orange is no less than in Shangshan, but they cannot have deep roots and a strong stem, as they are picked by foolish people." Another old man said, "I am hungry, and I need to eat the dragon root meat." He immediately pulled out a grass root from his sleeve, one inch in diameter, shaped like a dragon, with every detail perfect. He cut and ate it, and it refilled as he cut. After eating, he spat water on it, and it turned into a dragon. The four old men rode it together, and clouds rose under their feet. Soon, there was wind, rain, and darkness, and they disappeared. The people of Ba say that it has been like this for over a hundred and fifty years, and it seems to have occurred during the Chen and Sui dynasties, but the exact year is unknown.

During the reign of Emperor Dezong in the Tang Dynasty, there was a Daoist priest named Liu at the Yuntai Temple in Huazhou. He had been cultivating qi and abstaining from food for nearly twenty years. Every time the three major festivals arrived and rituals were performed, Liu would see a man dressed in simple robes with a dark, emaciated appearance. This man would sit in the last seat during the rituals and leave after they concluded. This happened for over ten years, and the man's clothes and complexion never changed. The priest found this strange and asked him, "What is your name?" The man replied, "My name is Zhang Gongbi, and I live on the eastern slope of Lotus Peak." Liu thought that this place was a remote and uninhabited area and asked Zhang if he could accompany him. Zhang agreed happily and said, "This place is very pleasant. If you can come here, you will also find no boredom." Liu then followed Zhang as they traveled for about thirty to forty miles, climbing through dense vegetation where only birds could pass. They passed through extremely dangerous cliffs and valleys, places even apes and monkeys could not cross, but Zhang walked through them as if they were flat roads, and Liu followed without difficulty. They eventually reached a sheer cliff, standing over a thousand feet high, overlooking a bottomless valley below. A narrow path, only a few inches wide, led to a spot where Liu and Zhang stood sideways. Zhang then tapped the stone wall with his finger, and someone inside asked, "Who is it?" Zhang replied, "It's me." Suddenly, the wall opened, revealing a door with its own sky, earth, sun, and moon. As Zhang was about to enter, Liu followed him, and the person inside angrily said to Zhang, "Why have you brought outsiders here?" The person then closed the door, and it reverted to a solid stone wall.

Gong Bi said, "This is not someone else; it is the Daoist Master Liu of Yun Tai. I was engaged in battle, so I asked to come here. Why are you rejecting me so deeply?" He then opened the door and let Gong Bi and the Daoist Master in. Gong Bi said, "The Daoist Master has come here very hungry; you may give him a generous meal." The person then asked the Daoist Master, "Can you stay here?" The Daoist Master requested to stay for a later time. The person then took a bowl of water and sprinkled a small amount of powder from a green bag behind his elbow to drink to the Daoist Master. The taste was very sweet and fragrant, and after drinking, the thoughts of hunger and thirst were dispelled. Gong Bi said, "I said yesterday that the mountain was very enjoyable; why don't you perform a play, so the Daoist Master can watch?" The person then sprayed water into the eastern valley, and there appeared one blue dragon and one white elephant, dancing together, with their dance being very wonderful. There were also pairs of majestic phoenixes and colorful cranes singing, with their songs being very clear. After a while, Gong Bi sent the Daoist Master back, and when he looked back, he only saw green cliffs and red ravines, and the previous dancing and singing were no longer visible. As they were about to leave, Gong Bi bid farewell.

The monk arrived at the temple, handled all the affairs, and then went back to look for Gong Bi, but every step was fraught with danger, utterly unreachable. Regretting not stopping earlier, he cried out to the heavens and the earth, and thus developed a backache. Gong Bi no longer came. Zhao Ying County Magistrate Xue Gonggan spoke these words on behalf of his uncle Sengru. Diao Junzhao, a performer from Ankang, had a wife named Ba Yu, who suffered from a goiter. Initially, it was as small as a chicken egg, but gradually grew to the size of a jar or pot holding three to four sheng. After five years, it became as large as a bag holding several dou, so heavy that she could not move. Inside the goiter were sounds resembling those of zithers, flutes, bells, and pipes. When listened to carefully, they seemed to follow the rules of music, clear and pleasant. After several years, tiny holes formed on the surface of the goiter, likened to needles, with an unknown number. Before rain, white smoke would be emitted from these holes, rising and spreading, forming clouds, and rain would fall immediately. The family, young and old, feared this and all requested to send her far away to a cave. Junzhao was deeply attached and could not bear to part. He said to his wife, "I am pressured by public opinion, and I will not be able to protect you in marriage. To send you to a place where no one lives, what do you think?" She replied, "My disease is truly disgusting, whether sent away or removed, I will die. You should remove it for me, and see what is inside." Junzhao immediately sharpened a sharp knife and was about to cut into her when the goiter split into four pieces with a loud sound, revealing a large monkey that jumped away, running and jumping. He then wrapped it in cloth and cotton. Although the goiter disease was suddenly cured, she soon became very ill.

The next day, a Taoist priest knocked on the door and said, "I am the monkey that came out of the goiter yesterday. I am originally a spirit of a macaque, capable of summoning wind and rain. I frequently interacted with the old dragon in the Ghost Sorrow Pool of the Han River, often observing boats. When a ship approached, I made it capsize to seek food from it to feed my descendants. Yesterday, the Supreme Being punished the dragon and searched for accomplices, so I borrowed the neck of your wife, the Qiuqi, to hide my life. Though we are not directly related, it has also been a great burden. Now I obtained a little bit of magical ointment from the god of Phoenix Mountain. Please apply it as instructed, and it should heal immediately." Junzhao applied it as instructed, and the wound healed immediately. Junzhao then kept the Taoist priest, cooked chicken, and set a meal. After eating, he bought wine to drink, and the Taoist priest then sang a high-pitched song, also playing sounds of silk, gourds, and jade, all resonant and lovely. Afterwards, he left, and no one knew where he went. It was during the Dading period.

Gu Yuanzhi, a descendant of Gu Bi, the Chancellor of the Later Wei Dynasty, was raised by Gu Bi from a young age. One day, he died from drinking. Gu Bi was extremely saddened and, after three days of burial rites, began to reflect on him, wishing to say goodbye again. He then ordered the coffin to be chopped open. When it was opened, Gu Yuanzhi had come back to life. Gu Yuanzhi said: "At the time of being drunk, I suddenly felt as if I was in a dream. Someone poured cold water on my body. Looking up, I saw a divine figure in red robes and a colorful cloak, with an imposing appearance. The figure looked at me and said, 'I am Gu Shuo, your distant ancestor. I just wanted to go to the land of peace and spirits, but there was no one to carry the bag or accompany me, so I came to take you.' He then ordered me to carry a large bag, weighing about one catty. He also gave me a bamboo staff, about twelve feet long, and told me to ride after him. We flew up very quickly, always in mid-air, traveling southwest, not knowing the distance. The mountains and rivers became more distant, and suddenly we landed on the ground, having arrived at the land of peace and spirits. This country has no big mountains; the highest ones are no more than several dozen zhang high, all covered with green jade. Green and colorful bamboo grows at the edges of the stones, with strange flowers and precious fruits. Soft grass with a fragrant and charming scent, and beautiful birds chirping and chattering. The tops of the mountains are flat like grindstones, with clear springs gushing down in three or four hundred streams. The plains have no ordinary trees, all growing a hundred kinds of fruits and things like the *xiangsi* tree and pomegranates."

Every tree and flower blooms together, with the fruit vividly red, reflecting on the green leaves among fragrant clusters, creating a colorful display on the trees. All seasons are not timid; only once a year, the flowers and fruits change subtly, and new, tender ones grow, but people do not notice. All fields are filled with large gourds, which are filled with five grains, sweet, fragrant, and precious, not comparable to the rice and grains of China. People have enough food, without the need for farming. The plains and lowlands are lush and abundant, with noxious weeds not growing. Between the branches and trunks of trees, all kinds of colored silk and cotton grow, and people can collect them according to color, and weave them at will. The fabrics are exotic, fine, and delicate, without the need for silkworms or looms. The four seasons' air is always warm and pleasant, like the air in China during February and March. There are no mosquitoes, flies, frogs, ants, lice, bees, scorpions, snakes, vipers, geckos, centipedes, or spiders. There are no owls, hawks, crows, falcons, sparrows, magpies, or bats. There are no tigers, wolves, jackals, leopards, foxes, or hyenas. There are no cats, mice, pigs, or dogs causing disturbance. The people are all of equal height and beauty, with no one having desires, love, or hatred.

In this society, every household has two sons and two daughters. Being neighbors leads to generations of intermarriage. Girls come of age at 15 and marry, while men marry at 20. People live to be 120 years old, free from premature death, illness, deafness, muteness, or lameness. Those under 100 years old have perfect memory, but those over 100 lose track of their age. When life ends, one disappears suddenly without trace, and even close relatives and descendants forget the deceased. They eat one meal a day at noon, consisting mainly of wine, fruit juice, and fruits. The food is not digested in the usual sense, and there is no need for a toilet. There is no private storage of grain; surplus grain is left in the fields for those who need it. There is no market for vegetables; wild plants are sufficient for food. Every ten acres has a spring of wine that is sweet and fragrant. Citizens gather daily to stroll, sing, and enjoy themselves, dispersing at dusk without ever getting drunk. Each person has a servant who is naturally cautious and anticipates their needs without being told. Houses are built according to one's will, all of which are magnificent.

The country raises only horses among its six livestock. These horses are extremely well-trained and swift, requiring no fodder as they feed themselves on wild grass and do not approach stored grain. People ride them when they need to, and after riding, they are released. There are no owners or caretakers. All officials are sufficient, but they do not know they are officials, mixed among common people because there are no duties to manage. Even though there is a king, the king does not know he is a king, mixed among the officials because there are no duties to promote or demote. The country also lacks sudden thunderstorms or heavy rains; the wind is always gentle and warm, not shaking or damaging things. It rains once every ten days, always at night, moistening and refreshing the land without causing flooding. All the people in the country are naturally close to each other, like family members, and generously help one another. There are no market transactions or trade activities because they do not seek profit. An ancient saying has reached the country, and the speaker turned to Yuan Zhi and said, "This is a harmonious and divine country. Though it is not a land of immortals, the customs are not bad. You return, and you should tell the world about it. I have already arrived here, and Yuan Zhi will find someone else to carry the bag; I will not use you anymore." He gave Yuan Zhi wine to drink, and after several rounds, Yuan Zhi did not realize he was drunk. Afterward, he woke up, and his body was alive.

Since then, Yuan Zhi distanced himself from worldly affairs, completely forgot about the desire for office, and wandered among mountains and waters, giving himself the name Zhihezi. Later, no one knew what happened to him. Lu Gonghuan, a Yellow Gate Attendant, served as the Governor of Mingzhou. In the county of Xiangshan, there was a place in the remote valleys where bandits were digging up a tomb. They reported: "At first, we saw flower bricks in the wheel tracks. We uncovered them and found that it was an ancient tomb. We then gathered ten people and went to the county to submit a petition, requesting to stay by the roadside. The county magistrate agreed. We then planted hemp to hide our activities. We then worked hard to excavate, entering the tunnel, gradually reaching the interior of the tomb, where there were three stone doors, all sealed with iron. The bandits could recite spells and fasted to purify themselves. The next day, two doors opened, and in each door, there were hundreds of bronze figures and horses holding spears and halberds, the craftsmanship being exquisite. The bandits fasted for three more days, and one of the middle doors opened. A man in yellow robes appeared and said: 'General Liu of the Han dynasty's Southern Expedition has come to inform you. He had great military achievements in life, and upon his death, the emperor ordered his tomb to be protected and bronze figures and horses to be cast to represent his ceremonial guards. We have come here, but you must seek treasures. The room we occupy has nothing else, and official burials do not bury valuables. Why bother to use divine spells to intrude? If you do not leave, both sides will suffer.' After speaking, he returned, and the door closed as before."

The thief continued reciting spells for several days without ceasing. The door opened, and a person in green clothes came out to convey a message. The thief did not agree to the terms, and the two doors suddenly opened. A great flood surged, drowning all the thieves. One thief managed to swim out, tied himself up, and went to the authorities, detailing the whole story. The Huangmen Ling carefully examined the tomb. Inside the inner gate, there was a stone bed, with bones and pillows. The water had carried it halfway down, and therefore, the two gates were sealed, and the tunnel was blocked. ○ Wu Quansu Wu Quansu, a native of Suzhou, was recommended as a filial and honest official but failed five times in the imperial examinations. In the twelfth year of the Yuanhe era, he was staying in Yongxing Lane in Chang'an. On the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, after he had gone to bed, he saw two men in white robes holding tablets, as if they were officials from the examination hall summoning him. Wu Quansu said, "The imperial examinations are divided into categories with a set time, so why bother to summon at night?" The messengers insisted, and Wu Quansu, reluctantly, got out of bed and followed them. He did not realize that they had passed through the inner city and exited the Kaiyuan Gate after two hundred steps, traveling north on a road about two feet wide, with deep mud everywhere else. He saw men and women being dragged, pulled down, shackled, bound, with their clothes torn, monks, Taoists, heads in sacks, hands tied behind their backs, and hundreds of people being driven through the mud, while Wu Quansu alone walked on level ground. After walking about three miles, he entered a city where he saw an official building, with over a thousand people lined up, and military officers with swords dividing the people into groups of fifty. As each group passed, Wu Quansu was in the third group.

In the main hall, there was a grand hall, with beds and tables set up in the center. A man wearing red robes was seated, and dozens of officials stood on both sides. The hall officials called out names, and the judge immediately judged and sent people to the prison officials, to the prison of grinding, to the prison of mining, to the prison of boiling, to the prison of fire, and to the prison of case. Hearing that he was sent to prison, he realized he was dead. Seeing that all forty-nine people had been called out and assigned, only Quansu remained. He asked the person, "What is the position of the person in charge of the hall?" The person replied, "He is a judge." Quansu then complained, "I have always followed the Confucian path with respect, and my years of service are not yet complete. I should not die." The judge said, "The records in the underworld are clearly detailed. According to the records, he is summoned. How can you falsely accuse?" Quansu said, "If it is true that his years of life are not yet finished, I request to verify the records now." The judge then ordered to bring the household register of Wu Prefecture, and found Quansu, who had passed the imperial examination in 818 as a scholar. In the following three years, he had no official salary. The judge said, "Three years in the world are like a blink of an eye, and he has no honor or salary. Why return? Once gone, he will come back, and it will only complicate the records." Quansu said, "I left my parents for five years. Returning would be honorable. Not to mention that he still has three more years to achieve fame. I humbly ask for your pity and consideration." The judge said, "Go ahead." He then warned the guide, "This person has a short life. Should be sent away quickly. If delayed, dawn will break soon." The guide accepted the order and led him away. Out of the hall, those who envied and cried could not be counted.

After leaving the city, the mud (or metaphorical obstacles) was no longer visible. Returning to the Kaiyuan Gate, two officials told Quansu, "Your command is very meager; if you return suddenly at dawn, you will not be able to return. Have you seen the order from the Judge? We are all poor, so each of us will give you 500,000 coins, and you will have no worries." Quansu replied, "I am a distant guest and also poor; how can I accept such a gift?" The officials said, "The husband of your mother, who lives in Xuanyang and is a wealthy official in the Ministry of Finance, can achieve this with just one word." They then went together to his home. The two officials refused to go up the steps, and Quansu entered to inform him. The household was just eating fried pancakes when Quansu bowed before the lamp and said, "Auntie, may you be blessed with longevity!" He then said, "Uncle, may you be at peace!" But there was no response. Quansu then covered the lamp with his hand, and the entire room became dark. The uncle said, "Why don't you throw something small? Eating fragrant things at night will anger the spirits." Quansu, already frustrated by the lack of response, saw the situation as ghostly and became quite angry. A servant holding food was directly in front of him, so Quansu struck him with force, and the servant fell immediately. The family rushed to pull out his hair and splash water on him, calling him for a long time before he regained consciousness. Quansu, having failed to express his feelings, went down the steps to ask the two officials. The officials said, "Indeed, you have not returned to life; you must be a ghost. Ghosts speak, but no one else can hear. Covering the lamp and striking, this is certainly enough to frighten people."

"Asked, 'Then how can one discuss matters?' The response was: 'By applying my spittle to the main gate, the entire household falls asleep; by applying it to the inner gate, those inside fall asleep; by applying it to the hall gate, everyone in the hall falls asleep. One can use one's hands to catch my spittle and apply it.' Quansu clenched his hands, and the two officials spat alternately. After hesitating, he clenched his hands and applied it to the hall gate. Suddenly, everyone in the hall yawned and stretched, quickly removing their food dishes, and then went to sleep. The two officials said, 'When you enter, stand three feet away from the bed to speak. Be careful not to get too close to the bed, and do not shake it with your hand, or else the sleeper will not wake up.' Quansu spoke according to what was said, and his aunt was startled awake, crying and telling her husband, 'Quansu came home late tonight, and suddenly he died. Now he appears in a dream asking for money, saying he left something behind. What should we do?' Her husband replied, 'He was just worried about his nephew and had a vivid dream. Why should we believe it so quickly!' She went back to sleep, and again she had a dream, startling awake and crying, she asked for paper from the cabinet, and there happened to be two hundred sheets. She then ordered them to be quickly cut and burned, and when the fire went out, a thousand strings of cash appeared on the ground. The two officials said, 'There is too much money, I cannot carry it. But your strength, the strength of a living person, can bear it all. Please carry it and deliver it to the Temple of Jie Gong.' Quansu initially thought it was difficult, but he tried using both hands to support it, and when he lifted it with his shoulders, it seemed extremely high, but it was actually very light. He then carried it to the Temple of Jie Gong, where the owner, dressed in purple and wearing a gold belt, ordered the officials to receive it."

After completing the ritual, the two officials said, "Your return to life is certain. Consider returning easily, for is there also something to be seen? Now we wish to take one person to be reborn, can you briefly observe?" Quansu replied, "It is indeed my wish." They then led him to the southern end of the silk shop in the Western Market, where the houses were dimly lit with flickering lights and soft weeping. Several monks were reciting scriptures at the door, and the house was filled with incense smoke. The two officials dared not approach, so they climbed onto the eaves behind the main hall, making it the sleeping area. They pulled out tiles and broke beams, creating a large hole. Looking down into the hole, they saw an elderly man faintly breathing, with those weeping around his bed. One official took out a rope from his chest, as thick as a finger and over two zhang long, and instructed Quansu to sit calmly and hold it, with one end hanging into the hole. He warned Quansu, "I will seek out that person. When the person comes, pull the rope." He then descended the rope and, with his right hand, seized the elderly man, while his left hand pulled the rope. Quansu quickly pulled him out and dragged him in front of the hall, binding him with the rope. The two officials carried him out and looked at each other, asking, "Where is the largest slaughterhouse?" One of them replied, "The largest slaughterhouse is at Wang's family in the south end of the cross street in the Bu Zheng坊." They then went there together. Upon arriving, they placed the elderly man on the slaughterhouse table, removed his clothing and tied it around his body, and pushed and struck him.

The old man said it was bitter, his voice moved people. Quansu said, "If there is a crime, punishment should follow, but this is also not legal. If there is no guilt, why should he suffer?" The two officials said, "Why are you asking this question so late? All people with good deeds and purity of virtue, those who should be born in heaven, are welcomed by heavenly music, colorful clouds, rainbow flags, and crane-drawn chariots. How can I see this? If there are serious crimes or filthiness, those who should fall into hell, are taken by ogres with ox heads, iron forks, shackles, and manacles. How can I see this? This old man has no merit for heaven, and no sin for hell. Although he can cultivate himself, he has not yet transcended the mundane world. But he keeps his body clean, pure and without blemish. After giving up this body, he should only be reborn as a man. When he was planning his next life, his mother was pregnant. This life has ended, that life should be born. Now if we do not roll him up, to make that woman, how can she give birth?" They rolled and rubbed him with all their might, and they felt it getting smaller. In a short time, its form is just the size of a fist, all bones and nine orifices, nothing is different. Then they carried him gently, passing by the southern part of the outer city, the Great Success Avenue, the southwest corner, turning east, the second curve, and the northern wall, entering the first house. There are lights shining brightly in the house, with urgent conversation. Two Buddhist monks are reading the *Eight Sun Sutra* by the window. Therefore, they dare not disturb the monks, and go directly up the steps, seeing the hall door slightly ajar. One official takes the old man into the hall, just as he reaches the bed, the newborn baby has already cried.

One official said, "The matter is concluded, now I shall escort you away." They then entered the inn in Yongxing Lane together and reached the bedroom, which was still dark, and nothing could be seen. Two officials followed from behind, and they then pushed Quansu and shouted, "Wu Quansu!" As if he had lost his footing and fallen, he regained consciousness, suffering from dizziness, and it took a long time before he stabilized. The official drum had just started beating, and his father-in-law came running on horseback from Xuanyang, by which time he had already regained consciousness, though his servant was unaware. He rode in a sedan chair and rested in Xuanyang, and after several days, he returned to his old self. He again entered the house of his son-in-law in the inner city, and everything was clearly in his mind. He considered himself to have passed the imperial examination through the *mingjing* route, but it was not worth much honor, and he wanted to quickly serve his parents. He chose a date for his journey, but sometimes he was dizzy and did not leave, or the donkey was injured, or it rained or snowed for days, or relatives and friends came and went, through these delays, he was then close to the exam day. He entered the examination hall and passed, and he no longer cared about his previous expectations. Soon he gained fame, laughed, and left Chang'an.
