He then realized that his fate was to succeed, and it was not possible to abandon it; if the time was not right, what would be the point of being impatient? By way of this example, it is enough to warn those who know how to advance but not how to retreat. ○Plundering Leftovers Du Ling's brother-in-law, Wei Yuanfang, Pei Pu, served as the County Magistrate of Xinping County in Bin Prefecture and died in office in the fifth year of the Yuanhe era (810 AD).

In the early years of the Changqing era, Yuan Fang failed the imperial examinations and was traveling to the region west of the Long Mountains. After leaving the Kaiyuan Gate and traveling dozens of miles to a remote inn, he was about to rest when he encountered a military officer riding a horse, accompanied by several attendants. The officer appeared to resemble Pei Pu. Seeing Yuan Fang, the officer seemed to recognize him and quickly dismounted to avoid him, entering a teahouse and hanging a curtain in a small room, while his attendants sat scattered outside the curtain. Yuan Fang was puzzled by this and decided to visit his residence. Upon lifting the curtain and entering, he found it was indeed Pei Pu. Yuan Fang was surprised and greeted him, saying, "Brother, after leaving the mortal world, you have taken up a military post again. Why do you appear so stern and imposing?" Pei Pu replied, "I am an official in the underworld, responsible for overseeing warriors, hence my martial appearance." Yuan Fang asked, "What position is that?" Pei Pu answered, "I am the Seizer of Surplus from the Three Rivers of Longyou." Yuan Fang inquired, "What is your duty?" Pei Pu explained, "My role is to seize surplus wealth from people." Yuan Fang asked, "What do you mean by surplus wealth?" Pei Pu said, "When people trade goods or seek alms, their fate is predetermined, but sometimes they encounter rare items or receive more than what was expected. This excess wealth is what we call surplus, and we seize it." Yuan Fang asked, "How do you know it is surplus and seize it?" Pei Pu replied, "Every drink and bite of food a person has is predestined; how much more so wealth? The underworld keeps records of what people are entitled to. If they acquire more than what is recorded, the underworld officials report it, and we seize it." Yuan Fang asked, "What do you mean by seizing? Do you steal it from their bags or pockets?" Pei Pu said, "No. It is determined by fate, and each person receives what is due to them. Any wealth beyond what is due is taken by me."

Some people squander resources, others handle strange matters, and still others trade at prices below the usual; these things have nothing to do with me. When I was born, I used to think that merchants gain wealth through diligence, farmers gain grain through diligence, and scholars gain official rank through diligence, only lamenting those who do not work hard and therefore do not achieve anything. Merchants who lose their ships, farmers who face drought, and scholars who repeatedly fail—how hard do they not work? But now I understand that diligence is the foundation of virtue, and learning is the root of goodness. Virtue is the way to cultivate oneself, but it is not enough to gain wealth or seek official rank. Your meeting with me was predestined; you were supposed to receive two pounds of silver, but after giving it to you, I will lose it again, so it is not a large amount. Your journey to Qì is rich, but to Bīn it is poor, and in Jing, you gain nothing at all; the other regions are average, nothing remarkable.

Life is determined by fate, and time does not vary; observe calmly through the Dao, no longer to resist or disturb, strive hard! Pú entered the city on official business, and the dark and ominous limits were such that they could not be crossed. He then presented two pounds of genuine white gold to him, bowed, and mounted his horse. Yuánfāng insisted, saying, "Having been apart for many years, we suddenly met, had a brief conversation, and now we are separated by day and night. Why are you leaving so suddenly?" Pú replied, "The official residence of my bureau is located in the Qian and Long regions, blocked by the Tibetan people. We are concerned that they will invade, so we must hold a council and make an alliance with the governor of the capital. Although this is not a long-term plan, it is a way to alleviate the crisis and a rough plan for temporary security. The military is already mobilized, and the arrival is not far off. The matter was not planned early, so we cannot prepare for it. Go! Go!" After riding several miles, he was no longer seen. What he left behind was genuine white gold. Disappointed and went westward, what was experienced and gained matched what was said.

Those who are content with their fate and know their destiny understand that all events are predestined. Suddenly, the foreign tribes became restless, and the court was aware of it, fearing potential rebellion. They thought of seeking a minister's advice for a strategy, and the prime minister presided over an alliance. The prime minister, Cui Gong, did not want to go to the border, so a humiliating peace agreement was made, and it turned out as he had said. **○ Ye the Taoist Master** During the Kaiyuan period, the Taoist master Ye Jingneng lectured at the Xingtang Temple in Fenghua County, Mingzhou. When he ascended the dais, there was an old man in plain clothes with a beard. He always arrived first and left last, lingering slowly, as if he had something to say but couldn't. As the lecture was about to end, he lingered even more. After all the students left, the master then called him to ask. The old man wept and bowed while speaking, calling himself a fish position, and said, "I have something to plead, but dared not speak for myself. Since I was not questioned, I dare to express my sincerity fully. My position is not human; I am actually the guardian dragon of the hidden treasure. My duty is in the small sea to the south of the temple. If I do not fail for a thousand years, I will be promoted slightly. If I fail, I will be punished by the scorching sands. Now it has been over nine hundred years, and I have been forbidden by a foreign monk for nearly thirty years. The monk is devout and has great magical power. Now I fear that on the day of the fifth month and the fifth hour, the monk's spell will be completed. The monk will come to drink up the water, and the treasure will be revealed. I will die, but I dare not hope for promotion. However, a thousand years in the scorching sea is truly unbearable. Only you, the immortal master, can pity me and save me from this difficulty; I will never forget your kindness." The master agreed, and the old man wept and thanked him before departing.

The teacher, fearing he might forget, wrote in large letters on the pillar: "Rescue the dragon at noon on the noon day." On that day, he went to dine with the townspeople and, upon returning, was just resting when his disciple suddenly read from the pillar: "Rescue the dragon at noon on the noon day. It is now just before noon, and my teacher is resting; how could he have forgotten it?" As the disciple was about to enter, the teacher had already heard and quickly asked, "What time is it now?" The disciple replied, "It has not yet reached noon." The immortal teacher then sent a green-clad disciple to hold a black talisman and rush to the sea. After a mile or so, they saw black clouds darkening the sky and toxic winds arising from all sides. There was a Brahmin wielding a sword, riding on black clouds, and shouting spells repeatedly over the sea, causing the seawater to soon reduce by half. The green-clad disciple also fell along with the sound. The teacher then sent a yellow-clad disciple to hold a red talisman and rush on horseback. After a hundred or more steps from the sea, they shouted again, and soon fell, causing the seawater to almost completely dry up. There was a white dragon jumping in the shallow waves, breathing heavily.

Again, he sent a messenger dressed in red robes with a yellow talisman to go. The monk shouted at him again, and he did not fall despite the shouting. When they reached the shore, the sea water was only one or two feet deep. The white dragon raised its mane and opened its mouth on the sand. The messenger in red robes threw the talisman into the sea, and the water immediately returned to its original state. The Brahmin touched his sword and sighed, saying, "Thirty years of diligent practice, all lost in an instant. How many skills does this Taoist have!" He left in anger. Afterwards, the sea became calm, and the waves and wind ceased. The two messengers who had fallen before gradually got up and returned together, reporting everything to the master. Before the report was finished, the old man arrived, wept, and said, "Earlier, I almost died from the foreign magic, but without the power of the immortal master, I could not have escaped. I, Wei, fear I cannot repay you, but I will always be attached to you. I wish to be your disciple, and you can command me. If I receive your command, even if there are great distances between Qin and Yue, or mountains and rivers, with just a thought, I will be at your side." From then on, he came to visit every morning and evening, like a disciple.

The master, seeing that his residence was on high ground, realized that it was impossible to dig a well. As a result, children had to fetch water from ten miles away, causing the entire community great trouble. One day, the master said to the bearded father, "I have lived here for a long time and feel pity for the children who have to fetch water so far. I am thinking of having a spring around the residence to help them. Can you bring it?" The bearded father replied, "The flow of water is something in the heavens, not something that can be brought by force. However, since you have seen the living, you have also escaped the suffering of a thousand years. How can I refuse? Although the spring cannot be brought by force, it can be brought after a battle. Order everyone to move elsewhere. On that day, after three cycles of darkness and light, they returned, perhaps there would be some success in following the command." The entire community followed the instructions. After the designated period, they returned to find stones laid around the residence, with clear water flowing gently. After circling around, the water flowed south into the sea, and the Daoist priests relied on it. They then named the channel "Fairy Master's Channel." The master's marvelous skills spread widely throughout the world, which was due to the help of the dragon. ### Translation of the Second Part Xu Yuanchang, a sorcerer from Jiangling, was a guest in Huainan. When the censor Lu Jun served in Guangling, he had a virtuous wife whom he treated with exceptional care and affection. Soon after, his wife passed away, and Lu Jun was deeply grieved, his sorrow even surpassing that of ordinary people. Whenever the spring wind blew or the autumn moon shone bright, the sounds of music would evoke sadness, and the cries of geese in flight would choke his throat. Sometimes he would toss and turn, unable to sleep, tormented by grief and fear; at other times, he would stand motionless, feeling utterly desolate, his heart heavy with sorrow. This state of mind lasted for over a year, and he completely lost his youthful appearance, his hair turning white overnight.

One day, Yuan Chang arrived, and Lu Sheng, knowing of his extraordinary skills, tried to tempt him with the story of Emperor Wu of Han and his favorite concubine, Lady Li. Yuan Chang replied, "This is very easy." Lu Sheng asked, "Then can you bring the spirit of my deceased wife for me?" Yuan Chang said, "What I can bring is only her spirit, and you will only get a brief glimpse of her." Yuan Chang found this strange. Lu Sheng asked, "Then what can you bring?" Yuan Chang replied, "I can bring her body as if she were alive, and we can interact at ease, fulfilling all the intentions of life." Lu Sheng, extremely happy, bowed and said, "Master, if you truly bring her, my family and I will be overjoyed." Yuan Chang asked, "Can you remember the entire outfit of your deceased wife?" Lu Sheng replied, "Yes." So they chose the day of Gui Chou, with the direction of Gen Gong and the direct sound. They emptied the room, setting up only incense and nothing else. They then prepared a feast at midnight, having Lu Sheng dress formally and wait. An old servant stood by. Yuan Chang said, "Lady Li's arrival depends on me being here. If I leave, she will disappear. Your wife has been deceased for a long time, and your bed is now empty and lonely, her spirit (the rest is missing)."

Inspector of the Right Privy Councilor Ma Zong, in the late Yuanhe era, was in charge of Dongping. On the sixth day of the sixth month of the second year of Changqing (822 AD), at noon, he was asleep and had a dream in which two military officials rode horses into the main gate, dismounted at the steps, and one of them held a knife and bowed, saying, "The Grand Commander summons you." Ma was startled and asked, "Who is the Grand Commander?" The official replied, "You will know when you see." Ma wanted not to go, but the messenger said, "The Grand Commander's command, the Privy Councilor should not refuse." Without realizing, he dressed and mounted his horse. One official led him, and another followed, and they rode out of the northern gate of Yuncheng (modern-day Shandong) hundreds of miles, then entered the city again for dozens of miles, where they saw a gate inscribed with "The Sixth Battalion Grand Commander's Office." The gate guards were militarily adorned and had a very stern demeanor. After entering for a hundred or two hundred steps, they came to a grand hall. A hundred or more steps to the north, there was a nine-roomed hall, and below the curtain, a loud voice said, "Ascend the steps." Secretly knowing the voice, it was Duke Du You. He hurried to ascend, and two eunuchs unrolled the curtain. Upon seeing him, it was indeed Duke Du You. Ma had always been acquainted with him, having a deep bond, and met with great joy, comforting and consoling as usual. They bowed and sat down, and the Grand Commander said, "Do not blame me for inviting you. I am about to be rewarded for my years of service, and superiors allow self-selection for replacement. Those in the capital who can be entrusted with heavy responsibilities, today, no one surpasses you in capability, and I wish to entrust this position to you. This position is called 'Sixth Battalion Grand Commander,' it is not too much, and it will greatly protect relatives and friends. Life in this world is like a white horse passing through a crevice, who can avoid death?"

However, good fortune does not come twice, and good times are easily lost. Unless there is a deep understanding, how could one recommend oneself in place of another? Once power and position have been attained, do not be complacent or negligent. The chancellor replied, "If one lives as a general, how can one die as a commoner? Official rank and salary are just rising; who cares about the afterlife? Even if made king, one would not want it, how much more so as a general?" Du said, "The emperor requests to grant this to you, and heavenly mandate is hard to resist. Once the official document is issued, how can one defy heaven!" The chancellor replied, "Heaven listens closely, and also follows human desires. How can one seek to replace oneself, thus falsely claiming heavenly mandate?" Du said, "I will ultimately grant it to you, how can you avoid it?" The chancellor replied, "I will ultimately refuse, how can the general grant it? If it's forced by ghosts, isn't there still heaven?" Du then turned to the officials and said, "Since the chancellor has refused, the matter will not be successful!" The chancellor said, "I am thirsty, please bring two cups of tea." Du still urged the preparation of tea. The attendant said, "Since the chancellor does not stay, it is not appropriate to drink this tea. Especially in the heat, one cannot stay long, it is best to leave quickly."

Soon after, the official led the horse to stand at the old place and respectfully declined to stay, preparing to leave. The marshal walked step by step to see him off. After descending the steps, they took each other's hands and said, "Strive to improve your future plans; this position will eventually be yours." Then he rode south on horseback, his former subordinates following as before, and returned along the old path. (The following text is suspected to be missing or incomplete.) ○ The Guest of Mount Hua Dang Chaoyuan was a native of He County, Tongzhou. In the second year of the Yuanhe era, he retired to live south of Luo Fu Water in Mount Hua. On the sixteenth day of the twelfth month of the following year, near the second watch of the night, the sky was clear and the moon was bright, the scenery was very beautiful. Suddenly, he heard the sound of knocking on the door. He ordered the servant to wait for her, and she said, "A young woman, about seventeen or eighteen years old, with an extraordinary appearance, and a strange fragrance filled the road." Dang Chaoyuan invited her in and sat with her. Her words were clear and eloquent, her grace was very high, and she was certainly not of this world. After a long time, she said, "Do you know who I am?" Dang Chaoyuan replied, "If you are not a goddess, then you must not be an ordinary person." The woman said, "No." She then asked, "Do you know why I have come here?" Dang Chaoyuan said, "I am not ashamed of my lowly status, but I seek only the pleasure of your bed." The woman smiled and said, "Certainly not."

I am a woman, not a divine being, but a fox spirit from the Southern Graveyard. After studying the Dao for many years, I attained the state of being a spirit. Now that my spiritual goals are fulfilled, I must follow the rules of the ordinary world, and I beg you to save my life. The pleasures of our intimate moments and the laughter and conversation we shared have not been in my thoughts for years. I ask you not to doubt me because of this. If you agree to this small request, I will entrust my life to you. My fate is to die tomorrow at the hands of the archers in the imperial stables. Any hunter who passes by late in the evening should be prepared with food and drink for him. He will ask what you need, so you should say: "My beloved is ill and needs a hunting fox to fulfill a private wish, and there will be a special reward." If you make this request, he will surely agree. The gifts he will bring, I leave for you now. I am giving you this silk as a token of my gratitude. If you find my body, please send it back to my old den at night. After achieving enlightenment, I will repay you generously. She bowed and wept before departing.

By dawn, he had sold a roll of white silk to buy wine and meat, preparing for the guest. That night, indeed, ten hunters from the Five Fences came to request lodging, and he treated them generously. The ten men said to each other, "We are hunters and should be disliked by the literati. Now that Lord Tang has treated us so generously, how can we repay him?" They then asked what was needed, and Chao Yuan said, "My relative is ill, and the doctor needs a hunting fox. His illness is worsening, and it will not improve without this." He then asked the others, "If you could kindly give it to me, I would be willing to offer five rolls of white silk as payment for the wine." The ten men agreed and left, walking south for more than a hundred steps. Suddenly, a fox ran around a large tomb, and they surrounded it, shooting it with an arrow and killing it instantly. His companions were delighted and said, "Last night, Lord Tang earnestly requested it, and today we have indeed obtained it." They then brought it to Chao Yuan and gave him five rolls of white silk. After they left, Chao Yuan washed its blood, laid it on the bed, and covered it with a quilt. By midnight when everyone was quiet, he secretly sent it to a den and sealed it with earth.

Seven days after midnight, there was again someone knocking at the door. Chao Yuan went out to see, and it was the same woman who had come before. He invited her in again. She wept and thanked him, saying, "Even though my spiritual path has been completed, by rule, one should die. Being consumed by others, there is no way to be reborn. Now I have received your deep grace, and you have specially preserved my mortal body. I have been repaired and thus live again, to prove this body. From head to toe, there is nothing to repay. The dust of the human world has departed, and the chariot of the clouds has a set time. The path to immortality is long and distant, and it is difficult to meet again. Please allow me to take my leave. I have fifty pounds of medicinal gold, which I will use as a gift and thanks. This gold is worth forty strings of cash per pound, and do not show it to anyone except a foreigner." Then she took out the gold, bowed twice, and left, saying, "Before the golden bird (sun) has risen, if blue clouds emerge from the grave, that is the time when I am leaving. In the house of fire, the flames of sorrow are just beginning to blaze. If one can think of calm principles and slightly rest from worldly desires, one can temporarily reach a cool place. Encourage yourself! Encourage yourself!" After speaking, she left. The next morning, he observed, and indeed, blue clouds emerged from the grave, lingering for a long time before dissipating. The people tested the gold, and it was truly a rare treasure. That day, he carried it to the market, but the market people only paid the regular price. Several years later, suddenly, a foreigner came to visit, saying, "Knowing that you have unusual gold, I wish to see it once." Chao Yuan showed it to him, and the foreigner laughed, saying, "This is the gold from the celestial treasury. How did you obtain it?" Then he paid forty strings of cash per pound, took it, and left. Later, no one knew where he was.

尹纵之，元和四年八月在中条山西峰读书。月光明亮，风清气爽，他常常吟诗啸歌，弹琴自娱。一天晚上，他听到屋檐外有脚步声，像是女子走动。尹纵之远远问道：“你是谁？”女子回答说：“我是山下的王氏女子，住处不远，每次听到郎君吟诗弹琴的声音，总是倾耳聆听，凝神于茅屋之中。因父母管教严厉，不敢前来听。今晚父母有亲事，一同前往，我独自留下。又听到久慕之声，所以前来偷听。没想到被郎君听见了。”尹纵之说：“我们住得近，相见是常事。既然来听琴，为什么不进来坐坐？”尹纵之出门迎接，女子便行礼。尹纵之略施礼，将她请进屋，设下坐榻请她坐下。女子仪态风姿，绰约异常，只是耳朵稍黑。尹纵之认为她是村中女子中最美的。山居生活闲适寂静，他心中积了不少愁思，有了她的陪伴，心情十分舒畅。他吩咐仆人准备水果和茶水，弹琴以娱她。山深景静，琴声清远，女子听后非常高兴。于是尹纵之留她过夜，女子推辞说：“父母怎么办？”尹纵之说：“既然高兴相会，就不必连夜回家。五更时分，悄悄关门独宿，父母天亮来，也不会察觉。”女子笑着同意了。两人相处十分愉快，发誓要白头偕老。彼此的深情厚意，无不尽情表达。

The sky was just beginning to dawn, and the clothes were about to be put back on. He deeply pondered, worrying that if she returned, it would be difficult to call her back. He thought of keeping a pledge to bind her. Looking at the bed, there was a green-patterned felt shoe. He hastily got up and took one and locked it in a cabinet. The woman wept and said, "I am poor and have no other shoes. This is the only one I have to support my feet. If you keep it, I will go barefoot. What will I tell my parents when they ask? I won't refuse the cane, but it will cut off all future hopes." He did not listen to her, and she wept again, saying, "My parents are strict. If they hear this bad news, they will not spare my life. Is it worth my dying for one night of pleasure? Our affectionate words, their sound has not yet faded. Please forget my clumsiness and allow me to serve you again in bed. Every night after the elders go to sleep, I can still come secretly. If you keep it, you will eventually kill me. It is not the way of deep thought. The intimate pleasure will be abandoned in an instant, and where is the trust and oath?" She then bowed and begged, "Just give it to me. If I don't come for one night, you can tell the neighbors." From the fifth watch until dawn, she wept and bowed at the foot of the bed, speaking in countless ways. He was moved by her earnest words and became even more doubtful, so he firmly kept her. As it was getting light, she dared not stay, and she wept again, saying, "I owe you from my past life. To give my life here. But your heart's intent is punished by divine justice. Seeking fame through writing will ultimately be in vain!" She wiped away her tears and left.

Zong allowed the creature to rest from the exhaustion of the entire night. Suddenly, he fell into a deep sleep and awoke only when the sun reached the window. He smelled a foul, bloody odor at the foot of his bed, and upon rising to investigate, he saw a pool of congealed blood on the floor, with drops leading away. He opened the cabinet to examine the felt shoes and found them to be pig hoof shells. Immediately, he took up his staff and followed the blood trail, arriving at the pigpen of Wang Chao at the foot of the mountain, where the blood led inside. Upon looking, he saw a large sow missing the hoof on its right hind leg. The blood trail led to beneath the wall, where the sow glared at him and fled. Zong informed Wang Chao, who took up his bow and arrow and pursued it, killing it with a single arrow. That year, Zong went to the foot of the mountain to seek an offering, though his reputation and prestige were great, he ultimately achieved nothing. Was it the fault of the pig? ○ Wang Huang Wang Huang of Taiyuan, on the 5th day of the 5th lunar month in the 3rd year of the Yuanhe era (808 CE), at the hour of shen (around 3–5 PM), departed from Luoyang for Gou Shi Village. He traveled twenty-five li from Jianchun Gate, and on the left side of the road, he saw a newly dug grave. Before it stood a young woman in white mourning attire, offering a sacrifice and weeping bitterly. Huang secretly observed her and saw that she was about eighteen or nineteen years old, of extraordinary beauty. Two maids stood by her side, and there was no husband present. One of the attendants said, "This young lady is from the state of Qin. After her coming-of-age ceremony, she married Pei Zhi of Hedong. Less than two years later, Pei went to Luoyang and never returned. The young lady was surprised by this and, together with these two of us, came to Luoyang, only to find that Pei had already died. Her husband is buried here, hence she has come to mourn and offer a sacrifice." Huang asked, "If that is the case, where will she go?" The attendant replied, "The young lady was orphaned at a young age and has no family. Where could she go? Recently, her family arranged her marriage, but her uncle has since passed away. For now, she will stay in Luoyang and must certainly seek a husband." Huang was pleased and said, "I hold an official post and have been unmarried since youth."

Residing in Gou, she is not very poor. Now, I wish to convey this message, please try to communicate it. The maid smiled and slowly went to tell the lady. Upon hearing the message, the lady wept even more sorrowfully. The maid held her robe and stopped her, saying, "It is almost evening now, there is nowhere to stay in the wild, and returning to Qin has no means of livelihood. Now this young man, fortunately, is a high-ranking official and young, with generous traveling expenses, so there is no urgent need for clothing and food. If you must go elsewhere, where else can you go? If you cannot suppress your emotions and accept the change, you can return to your body, why not listen to his words?" The lady said, "I married and served Pei, and now died as a guest in Luoyang. Our deep, intimate feelings have already been separated by light and darkness. Even if I break my body and powder my bones, there is no gratitude that can repay Pei's grace. I have not yet expressed my grief and sincerity, how could I bear to go elsewhere. Do not speak, I will return to Luoyang." The maid reported this to Huang, who said, "Returning to Luoyang has no mansion or residence, decide to be a guest in Gou, what harm?" The maid relayed this again. The lady, looking at the approaching evening, returned to her destination, then gathered her grief and bowed to Huang, intending to express respect and propriety, and grieved and sobbed for a long time.

Huang summoned his attendant soldiers and traveled with him for more than ten miles, staying together at Pengbo Inn. He arranged a separate bed according to etiquette. Every time they heard Huang speak, they would weep uncontrollably, unable to help but treat him with courtesy. They arrived at the Zhitian Villa before dawn, in the main hall, they wept and said, "I am truly plain and clumsy, unworthy of the noble gentleman's attention. Now without a place to return to, I have been deeply thought of. Please prepare a banquet, to show the proper etiquette of meeting." Huang immediately ordered the preparations. After eating together, they proceeded to the formal marriage ceremony. From then on, their mutual affection grew ever more earnest. Seeing her graceful and gentle demeanor, and her elegant speech, the beauty of her appearance was unparalleled at the time. The sincerity of her vows was such that nothing but death could change them.

Several months later, Huang returned to Luoyang for a reason. In Luoyang, there was a Taoist named Ren Xuanyan, a master of magical arts, who had always been friendly with Huang. Seeing Huang's appearance, Xuanyan was greatly surprised and asked, "What has happened to you, dear friend? Why do you look so worn out?" Huang smiled and replied, "I've taken a wife." Xuanyan said, "The one you've taken is not a human wife, but a powerful spirit. If you can quickly break off this relationship, you can still be saved. But in another ten or so days, your path to life will be cut off, and even I will be unable to save you." Huang was not pleased, as the planned matter had not been successful, and he had not left anyone behind to request a return. His intention was even more urgent. His deep, lingering thoughts could not be described. Another ten or so days passed, and Huang returned to Luoyang again. He met Xuanyan at the South Market, took his hand, and said, "Your appearance is that of someone who is about to die. Not believing my words, you have come to this. Tomorrow at noon, that person will come, and when they do, you will die. Alas! Alas!" He then wept and bid farewell to Huang, who became even more confused. Xuanyan said, "You don't believe me? Please keep this talisman in your chest. Tomorrow at noon, when your beloved enters the door, please throw the talisman at her, and you will see her true form." Huang took the talisman and kept it in his chest. After Xuanyan left, he told his servant, "Tomorrow at noon, the spirit from the Field of Cinnabar will come. Your master must throw the talisman at her. You can observe her shape; she will either be a blue-faced, heavy spirit or a red-faced one. When she enters and sits down, your master will die. When he dies, look at him—did he die sitting down?" The servant secretly remembered these words.

At the right time, Huang sat in the hall, and the evil spirit from Zhitiang came. When it reached the door, Huang threw a talisman from his chest, and the spirit immediately changed its face into a Nai Zhong ghost. The ghost seized Huang, who was already dead, and asked his servant, "Like this, why did you take the words of the evil Taoist and let my form appear?" Instead, the ghost grabbed Huang, laid him on the bed, and crushed him to death with one step. At dusk, Xuan Yan came to visit Huang, who was already dead. Xuan Yan asked the servant, "What form did it take?" The servant then told him. "This is the Nai Zhong under the right foot of the Northern Sky King," he said. "It is customary for it to be replaced every three thousand years. When the ghost's term is up, it should naturally choose a replacement, so it transforms into a human and takes it." "Huang died sitting down," he continued, "and after three thousand years, he should also seek a replacement. Now that he has died lying down, he will never get a replacement again." Before his eyes, Huang's body was lying with his spine broken. Xuan Yan wept and left. This is the servant who told the story.

A Scholar's Encounter with the Supernatural: The Tale of Cen Xi During the reign of Emperor Ruizong, the scholar Zheng Zhiguo, a guest in the household of the high-ranking official Cen Xi, stayed there for some time. One night, he slept in the inner hall. At midnight, he heard from afar the clamor and cries for mercy. He leaned forward to listen, and the sounds became increasingly closer. Soon, he clearly heard the person being saved say, "Cen's family is humble and lowly, not widely known or respected. I am lucky to be alive. Cen Xi mistakenly holds the reins of government. His heart is filled with fear, and he never dared to harm anyone. If a woman were to hold power, her heart would be even more fierce than Cen Xi's. Even if Cen Xi were to rule over things, no one will resent me when I am alive, and no ghost will resent me when I die. What have I done to deserve such punishment? Only the messenger can forgive me. We will make Cen Xi offer a million hidden coins as a token of gratitude." The cries of pleading filled the road. Soon, he saw a large ghost, more than a zhang tall, with disheveled hair and red clothing, holding a long sword and leaping over the wall. Men, women, old people, and young people also followed him in, some throwing themselves at the wall and prostrating themselves, their words earnest. The large ghost paid no heed and then leapt over the middle gate. The crowd entered in a disordered manner. After a meal's time, he heard loud weeping from the entire house. He woke up and listened, and the large ghost took Cen Xi's head and left. The crying inside the door was extremely sad, as if a great calamity had befallen. The drum of the government office was about to sound, and gradually, it seemed to subside. Zheng Zhiguo was in a state of confusion and did not know what to do. He walked along the corridor, and the drum sounded.

Drums sounded, and the central gate was wide open, startling the stable official. The attendants stood solemnly at the gate. Zhi Gu secretly observed and heard that Xi arose and was embarrassed. Shortly after, the time for court audience arrived, and the torch-bearer informed him. Xi put on his ceremonial hat and board and went out, patting the horse and about to mount. Suddenly, he touched his neck and said, "I had a pain in my neck at midnight, and it has become worse since then, what should I do!" He urgently ordered the scribe to write a note, requesting an extension of his previous leave for a short rest. He then went back in, walked a few steps, and turned back, saying, "There is something to attend to this morning, and I must address it personally." Reluctantly, he handed over the note and mounted the horse. Zhi Gu's observation of the midnight event was slightly confirmed, and he became even more worried. Shortly after, a rider rushed back and said, "The chancellor was punished, and his family's property was to be confiscated!" Zhi Gu climbed over the wall and escaped, avoiding interrogation by the judicial authorities. The one who had previously knelt and wept to seek pardon was a descendant of the Cen family. I have often heard that a person's honor and disgrace are all determined by the unseen spirits. Only this ghost official, how swift and mysterious he is. Thus, I know that within the depths of the unseen, one cannot be careless!

○Li Shen Li Shen of Longxi was the son of a man who once received favors from Zhu Ci. After the rebels were pacified and punished according to the law, Li Shen escaped and was spared. Later, when amnesty was granted, he gave all his family property and servants to the Huilin Temple in Luobei and lived there temporarily. He read books and played the qin, just to pass the time. Today, Cui Qun, the chancellor of Jingnan and a high-ranking official from Qinghe, along with his brother, a jinshi (advanced scholar), both observed the etiquette of disciples toward Li Shen, and his reputation was well-known. He often had a close friendship with the reclusive scholar Li Zuo, as sworn brothers. In the autumn of the thirteenth year of the Yuanhe era, Li Zuo told Li Shen, "I have an old comrade who is heading to Song. I won't know when I'll return, so can you accompany me on a boat trip?" Upon hearing that Li Zuo was leaving, Li Shen felt a deep sense of separation, so he boarded the boat. They initially agreed to travel one stage, and when the journey was over, Li Zuo said, "Your affection hasn't reached its limit yet, has it?" As they continued, Li Zuo seemed to be moved by something, and they couldn't say goodbye, continuing straight to Suoyang. That evening, Li Zuo thanked the boatman and left, and he and Li Shen descended from the Bian River embankment. Under the moonlight, Li Zuo slowly said, "Your affection has been sincere for a long time. Do you know who I am, Li Shen?" Li Shen replied, "A learned and eloquent person." Li Zuo said, "That's not it. I am an official in the underworld, recently serving as the governor of Luozhou, which is why I stayed in Luoyang for a long time. My official duties are concealed, so I cannot be seen during the day, which is why I could travel with you at night. Now, I am transitioning from the underworld to the earthly realm and have been entrusted to carry a child for five years. That's why I stayed with you step by step, having something to entrust to you." Li Shen asked, "What is it?" Li Zuo said, "My body is difficult to match in skill. I only fear that if I let go of this body, I might be completely absorbed in my past karma. I ask you to wake me up."

Yet Ruo was pregnant for five years, in her mother's womb, and her family considered it an omen. They prayed to gods and worshipped Buddha, spending all their resources. Ruo had not yet been born, what could the gods do? Brother can go to their home, write the character "产" in red ink and make them swallow it, Ruo will be born immediately. They will certainly present the brother with fine silk and white cloth. The brother should leave, wait until Ruo is three years old, and then come to see him again, and say, "The master's grandson who has not been born for a long time, I made them swallow the red character, and the boy is very clever, now he is three years old, I think it is appropriate to tell him, so I came back." Can take the child and hold him, at night, when the caretakers have closed the door, then take him to a quiet place and call out, "Li Ruo, do you remember me?" If he cries, then pat him. Ask him repeatedly, Ruo will surely have a slight understanding. The brother should talk to Ruo about the places he lives and plays in Luoyang, Ruo will have a great understanding, after understanding, his life's work will be without any remnants. This matter must be clearly explained and returned, Ruo will later become prosperous and honored, the brother cannot afford to be leisurely anymore. The brother has a great reputation, soon he will be appointed to a high office, be cautious not to accept it, accepting it will not be good for longevity. North of this prefecture, thirty miles away, there is a village called Hu, in front of the village there is a carriage gate, which is where Ruo will live in the future. After finishing speaking, he wept and bowed, then left.

Just before dawn, Shen, walking with a staff, visited and indeed found the village of Hu. He knocked on the door to seek rest, and the elderly man of the household, over eighty years old, leaned on a cane and invited him in. After seating him, the elderly man seemed worried, and Shen asked him about it. The elderly man replied, "The new wife has been pregnant for five years, all methods have been exhausted, and there are no signs at all." Shen said, "I have studied the Daoist arts and am skilled in spells. The reason for the lack of birth, I can discern immediately." He immediately ordered the attendants to bring the new wife, and after examining her arm, said, "It is a boy, very bright and intelligent, with extraordinary talent, hence not bound by the usual lunar cycles." Then he ordered the preparations for the birthing room to be made quickly, including curtains, beds, and other furnishings. Shen took a brush as if performing a ritual, wrote the character '生' (birth) in red ink, and had her swallow it. After she swallowed it, a boy was born. The elderly man was extremely happy and presented thirty bolts of silk. Shen accepted it and said, "This child is not ordinary; I will return in three years to advise you further." After saying this, he left.

On the agreed-upon date, Shen returned, and said, "The child you had before is now three years old. I wish to take him for one night to observe and divine." The caretaker was pleased and agreed. At night, when people were quiet, Shen took the child to a distant place and called out, "Li Zuo, do you recognize me now?" The child cried in fright, and Shen held him, saying, "Li Zuo, why don’t you remember me?" He held him again, and the child cried even more. Shen asked him three or four times, and the child suddenly said, "Is it really true that Brother Li has come here?" Shen then spoke to him about matters in Luoyang, and the child laughed as if they were old friends, saying, "I have now understood everything." Shen then took him back and kept him overnight. In the morning, he told the caretaker, "This child will have a great official rank and will be a prosperous person in his family. It is advisable to keep him well." Hu was pleased and gave him fifty bolts of silk as a gift. Shen then left. He remembered the words of the Taoist priest, who had used the three celestial bodies and five stars to hint at the child's fortune. Because Shen was killed due to his official rank, but avoided disaster by not eating, it was indeed his fate. This story is sufficient to warn those who covet official rank and wealth without understanding their fate. **Compilation of Lost Texts** **Du Wu** When Du Wu was young and not yet successful, he once met a Taoist priest on Changbai Mountain who gave him a pill of elixir. He took it, and from then on, he did not want to eat. His face appeared joyful and healthy, and he was light and agile without illness. Later, he became the prefect of Shangzhou and thought that he had already reached a high position. He feared to startle the crowd, so he decided to remove the elixir from his body. Whenever he met guests, he would always ask about the method.

After more than a year, a Taoist arrived, and he was extremely young. The witch asked him, and the Taoist taught her to eat pork and drink blood. The witch followed his instructions and ate, and the Taoist ordered her to spit. Shortly after, the witch spat out a large amount of phlegm, and there was a lump in it like a chestnut. The Taoist took it. It was very hard. The Taoist cut it open, and it was like newly made glue that was not yet dry, with a pill inside. The Taoist took it and washed it, putting it in his hand, and its color was green and lustrous. The witch said, "Give it to me, I will keep it for my old age." The Taoist did not give it, saying, "My master told me, 'Witch Du regrets taking my pill, now wants to give it back. You can teach her to take the medicine back.' Now I am following my master's command, wanting to remove the magical object. Now it is gone, and she wants to keep it until old age. Even if you keep it, you will not be able to use it. You should calm your mind." And he swallowed it and left. After more than fifty years, the witch spent all his wealth on burning medicine, but it never succeeded. ○Cui Shang During the Kaiyuan era, there was a man named Cui Shang, who wrote the *Wu Gui Lun* (Treatise on the Nonexistence of Ghosts), which was very well-reasoned. After it was completed, he was about to submit it, when suddenly a Taoist came to his door, asking to see the treatise. After reading it, the Taoist said to Cui Shang, "The reasoning in your treatise is very skillful. However, in the world, if one says there are no ghosts, this is a mistake." Cui Shang asked, "Why do you say so?" The Taoist replied, "I am a ghost, how can one say there are none? If you submit this treatise, you will be killed by the spirits. It would be better to burn it." And so, the Taoist disappeared, and Cui Shang eventually lost the manuscript.

In the middle of the Qianyuan era, there was a man named Zheng Wang who traveled from the capital to the imperial city. At night, he checked into a guesthouse called Wild Fox Spring for lodging, but he was not yet five or six li away when it became dark. Suddenly, he saw a house on the side of the road. He asked the gatekeeper, who told him it was the residence of General Wang, and that his father had been friends with the general. Zheng Wang was very pleased and introduced himself, paying his respects. The general came out and greeted him, exchanging sad tears and discussing various matters. They decided to stay overnight and had a meal together. In the middle of the night, after drinking to their heart's content, the general asked for a musician to sing and entertain them. Soon, a young woman named Qu Chu San娘 appeared, her appearance very beautiful, and she was especially skilled at singing "The Salt of the Magpie." Until dawn, they parted ways. The general's wife conveyed a message, instructing Zheng Wang to buy silk trousers, a headband, red flowers, and rouge. A few months later, on his way back east, Zheng Wang stopped by to deliver the requested items. The general greeted him warmly and invited him to stay overnight, as before. Zheng Wang asked why he didn't see Qu Chu San娘 anymore. The general replied, "She has already returned to the capital with her husband." The next day, Zheng Wang bid farewell and left. As he stepped out, he saw no more signs of the house, only hills and mounds remained. Zheng Wang felt disheartened and turned back. When he reached Wild Fox Spring, he asked the locals, who told him it was the tomb of General Wang. Near the tomb, a musician came to the inn. The musician's wife had suddenly fallen ill and died. She was wrapped in a reed mat and buried beside the general's grave, hence she was called Qu Chu San娘. A few days ago, the musician had also moved her body to be buried in Chang'an.

### Yuan Zai In the spring of the ninth year of the Dali era, Yuan Zai, the Chancellor of the Secretariat, arrived at court early in the morning. Someone presented an article, and he ordered the attendants to collect it. If this person wanted Yuan Zai to read it, Yuan Zai said, "Wait until I am in the cabinet, then I will read it." The person said, "If you cannot read it, please recite one yourself." After reciting, the person disappeared, and it was only then that Yuan Zai realized it was not a human being. The poem said: In the old residence to the east and west of the city, the flying flowers in the city are as chaotic as fluff. The swallow carries mud and wants to descend, but the house is empty, yet it flies away. Later, Yuan Zai's family was broken, and his wife and children were killed. ### Wei Peng Wei Peng, the governor of Jianzhou, after his term ended, became a guest in Nanchang. He had never had a thought for poetry, but later, after falling ill, he became confused and lost his mind, as if someone was leading him. Suddenly, he picked up a pen and wrote a poem: A lonely grave by the clear river, every day I see the late white sun. The shadow of pines sways in the long wind, the moonlight falls on the rocky fields. My hometown is thousands of miles away, relatives seldom meet. Looking at the empty clouds and mountains, tears fall like snowflakes. Regret being a guest of the underworld, again in this foreign county.

I wish to cherish the past, do not abandon me because of my flaws or lowly status. The poem is like the late wife's gift to a friend. After more than ten days, the friend died. ○ Cen Shun Cen Shun from Runan, courtesy name Xiao Bo, was fond of learning and had literary talent when young, and especially skilled in military strategy when older. He lived in Shan Prefecture, poor and without a home. His cousin, the Lü family, had a mountain house that was about to be abandoned, and Cen Shun requested to live there. When someone advised him against it, Cen Shun said, "Fate is constant, so what is there to fear!" and eventually moved in. After more than a year, Cen Shun often sat alone in the study, and even family members could not enter. At night, he heard the sound of drums, not knowing where it came from. When he went out the door, he heard nothing, but he was alone in his joy, believing it to be an omen of Shi Le. He prayed, saying, "This must be the aid of hidden soldiers. If so, please show me the promise of wealth and status." A few nights later, he dreamed of a man in armor and armor reporting to him, saying, "The Golden Elephant General sent me to tell Cen Shun, the city is on alert at night, there are noisy disturbances, I am honored by your favor, how dare I not follow your command. You will have a generous salary, please take care of yourself. Having great ambitions, can you condescend to visit a small country? Now the enemy country is attacking, we are eager to entrust the wise, we admire your good reputation, and wish to hold the banner." Cen Shun replied, saying, "General, your natural qualities are brilliant, you are a true teacher, I am honored by your words, and you have condescended to visit me. However, my loyalty is to serve you." The messenger returned with his message. Cen Shun suddenly woke up, feeling as if he had lost himself, sitting and thinking about the meaning of the dream.

Suddenly, drums and horns sounded from all sides, and the sound grew more resounding and fierce. Shun tidied his headgear, got out of bed, and bowed twice to make a wish. In a moment, the wind blew through the doors and windows, and the curtains flew up. Under the lamp, hundreds of iron-clad cavalry suddenly appeared, flying around, all about an inch tall, wearing armor and holding sharp weapons, scattered like stars across the ground. In the blink of an eye, the cloud formations gathered from all sides. Shun was startled but then calmed down to observe the scene. In a moment, a soldier arrived with a message saying, "The general sends a summons." Shun received it, and it read: The land is connected to the Xiongnu, with continuous warfare for several decades. Old generals and soldiers are exhausted, lying in their armor like frost, facing a powerful enemy that cannot be stopped. Your Excellency has nurtured virtue and pursued your career at the right time, often receiving good news, and I wish to entrust our fate to a divine pact. However, Your Excellency holds a high office and should enjoy great fortune in this sacred era, so how dare I, a small state, hope for such things. Because the bandits from the northern mountains of Tianya State have united, they plan to attack at midnight, and the outcome is uncertain, causing great fear. Shun thanked him, lit more candles in the room, and sat to observe the changes. After midnight, drums and horns sounded from all sides. Earlier, there was a mouse hole on the eastern wall, which transformed into a city gate, with enemy fortifications towering up, three times the sound of metal and leather, four gates sending out troops, thousands of banners, rushing like wind and clouds, both sides arrayed in formation. On the eastern wall is the Tianya Army, and on the western wall is the Jinxiang Army. After the formations were set, the military advisor said:

A Swift Horse and a Mighty General A swift horse, slanting its flight across three stops, and a mighty general, advancing broadly to bind all directions. A carriage, directly entering without circling, and the six divisions of the army, proceeding in order without deviating from the path. The king said, "Good." Then, the drum was beaten, and both armies had one horse, which slanted away three feet and stopped. Again, the drum was beaten, and each had one foot soldier, who moved horizontally one foot. Again, the drum was beaten, and the carriage advanced. Thus, as the drumming became more urgent, each emerged, and objects, arrows, and stones were mixed together in chaos. In a moment, the heavenly army was defeated and scattered, with killed and wounded scattered everywhere. The king rode alone to the south, and hundreds of people fled to the southwest corner, barely escaping. Before that, in the southwest, there was a medicine mortar, and the king stayed in the mortar, which turned into a castle. The golden elephant army was greatly invigorated, gathered their armored soldiers, and carried bodies across the ground. Shun bowed down to observe, and at that time, a rider arrived at the palace, declaring, "The yin and yang have their place; those who obtain it will flourish. The majestic power of heaven, wind-driven and continuous, won in one battle. What do you think, distinguished sir?" Shun replied, "The general's brilliance shines through the day, riding the heavens and using the time, secretly observing the divine and spiritual writings, cannot help but feel joy and relief." Like this, battles continued for several days, with victories and defeats not constant. The king's divine appearance was magnificent, and his heroic appearance was rare. A feast of fine dishes, together with Shun, presented innumerable treasures, pearls, and gemstones. Shun was honored among them, and all his desires were fulfilled. Later, he gradually distanced himself from relatives and friends, did not go out.

The family found Shun strange, but none could fathom the reason. Shun's complexion became gaunt, as if he had been afflicted by a ghostly presence. Relatives all felt something was amiss and interrogated him, but he remained silent. They gave him strong liquor to drink, and when he was drunk, they questioned him, and he revealed the truth. His relatives secretly prepared shovels and, while Shun was in the bathroom, they separated him. They carried shovels and began digging frantically, digging eight or nine feet into the room. Suddenly, a hole collapsed, revealing an ancient tomb. The tomb had a brick chamber, and it was filled with many ritual objects. There were hundreds of armor and weapons, with a gold bed in front of a chessboard, lined with horses on the board, all made of gold and bronze, and all the implements of warfare were present. They then realized that the general's words referred to the formation of the chess pieces. Afterward, they burned it and leveled the ground. They found many treasures, all of which had been stored in the tomb. Shun examined them, and suddenly he woke up, vomiting profusely. From then on, he felt refreshed, and the house no longer seemed ominous. This was during the first year of the Baoying era.

The Brother of Wei Sheng, the Imperial Music Official Wei Sheng, an official of the Imperial Household known as the Imperial Music Official, had a brother who was exceedingly fearsome. He claimed that he had never been afraid of anything. Whenever he heard of a haunted house, he would go there alone to spend the night. His brother told this to their colleagues, and one of them decided to test him. They learned that there was a house belonging to Ma Zhenxi in the northeast corner of Yan Kang, which was often plagued by strange creatures. So, they led Wei Sheng’s brother to this house, providing him with wine and food, and then left him alone in a solitary pavilion west of a large pond to spend the night. Wei Sheng’s brother drank until he was slightly warm and then undressed and lay down to sleep. In the middle of the night, he suddenly woke up and saw a small child, about a foot tall, with a short body and long legs, and a rather dark complexion. The child emerged from the pond, slowly moving forward, climbing the steps, and approaching Wei Sheng’s brother. Wei Sheng’s brother did not move, and the child said, "You’re a vile creature, do you think you’re so important that you can look down on me?" Then the child began to walk around the bed. After a while, Wei Sheng’s brother turned over and lay on his back, and he felt the creature climb onto the bed. He remained still. After a moment, he felt two small feet climbing up his own feet, cold as water and iron, reaching up to his heart, and moving very slowly. Wei Sheng’s brother did not move, waiting for the creature to continue moving until it reached his belly. He then quickly reached out and touched it, discovering it was an ancient iron pot missing one leg. He tied it to the bedpost with his belt. The next morning, the others came to see what had happened, and he fully explained the incident. They smashed the pot with a pestle, and it turned a bloody color. From then on, people believed that Wei Sheng’s brother was so fearsome that he could banish the evil spirits from the haunted house.

Su Lushuang, a subordinate under Ma Sui, the military governor of Taiyuan, once served under Bo Fang, a previous military governor. During a campaign against the Uyghurs, Bo Fang, on the battlefield, pointed to a flag of Liu Mingyuan and ordered Su Lushuang to execute him for not advancing in battle. Su Lushuang accepted the order, but a few days later, Liu Mingyuan suddenly advanced and managed to escape the death penalty. Many years later, Su Lushuang died. He also wandered in the underworld, where he met Liu Mingyuan. Liu Mingyuan said to Su Lushuang, "In the past, I was saved by you, and I had no way to repay your kindness. Today, I will fulfill my long-held wish." He then pointed out a path, which was filled with thorny bushes, and said, "Just follow this path, and you will meet Sheli Wang. Sheli Wang was a subordinate of the Chancellor in life. If you see him and plead with him, you will surely be spared." He told Su Lushuang to leave quickly, and Su Lushuang walked about twenty miles, eventually meeting Sheli Wang, who was hunting. Sheli Wang recognized Su Lushuang and asked, "Why are you here?" Su Lushuang replied, "I was summoned by the underworld authorities." Sheli Wang said, "You should not have come here; you should return quickly!" He then ordered the judge Wang Fengxiang to release Su Lushuang early and to deliver a message. He told Su Lushuang, "Tell the Chancellor that two years from now, he will be dismissed from his position. Within a year, he must first go to court. Your son died young; be careful not to reveal this." Wang Fengxiang released Su Lushuang. When Su Lushuang reached a gate, he met several friends he had drunk with before, who said to him, "You have returned alone; we could not even hope to accompany you."

A person was born and lived for five or six days, then arrived at Fengxiang. Fengxiang had already anticipated and knew about something, and asked, "What words were spoken about Sarira?" The person replied, "There were words, but I was not allowed to tell others." Fengxiang said, "I also know about it. You may return for now; I will report to the Attendant General when the opportunity arises." After ten days, the person reported it with Lushuang. The Attendant General summoned Lushuang to interrogate him, and Lushuang also detailed what he had seen. Fengxiang then reported, and what was later verified matched exactly what Lushuang had said. It is said that Fengxiang was born from the Department of the Underworld, and the fact was hidden and unknown to others, but it was revealed because Lushuang returned to life. Jing Sheng, a man from Yishi in Hedong, was always highly skilled in the classics and taught dozens of students. At the end of the year, he was about to return home. On the way, he happened to meet the former chancellor Lü Tan, an old acquaintance, and was taken away by him. The students then scattered and reported to Jing Sheng's family. However, when Jing Sheng arrived home, he had already died. After several days, he woke up and said, "In the underworld, I saw the Yellow Gate Attendant General Yan Wu and the Military Governor of the Northern Frontier Zhang Huo."

Jing Sheng was proficient in *The Book of Changes*. In his early years, he also taught alongside Lord Lv, but before he could complete his term, Lord Lv passed away. Lord Lv then appointed Jing Sheng to complete the remaining term. At that time, Yan and Zhang were both officials in the imperial court. They looked at Lord Lv and angrily said, "Jing Sheng should not be here; it is certainly not something that can be detained in the afterlife. Why should private desires harm him?" They jointly requested to let Jing Sheng return. Lord Lv agreed. Minister Zhang then called Jing Sheng and instructed him: "Two sons, one named Zengzi, the other named Fuzi, on the third day of the leap month of the first month, they will start building next to each other's houses, which will harm Zengzi's new wife. Please report and stop it. Let them stop quickly, and he will escape a great disaster." After Jing Sheng regained consciousness for several days, he reported this to his family. By then, the houses had already been built, and his wife had already passed away. Minister Zhang also said, "Zengzi will eventually become a prefect, and Fuzi will also become a prefect, but not through proper appointment." Indeed, as predicted, the events unfolded as he had said. Lu Xū's aunt, who was the aunt of the Prefect of Mingzhou, often kept a small dog named Huāzi, and she would always think of it fondly. One day, the dog went missing and was killed by someone. Several months later, Lu Xū's aunt suddenly died. In the afterlife, she saw a judge surnamed Li, who told her, "Your fate is nearly over, but someone strongly argued, and you will be given another twelve years of life." She bowed in thanks and left.

Walking along the main street, he encountered a grand mansion. Inside, there was a beautiful woman accompanied by more than ten attendants, who were about to pass through the gate. Someone was sent to call the lady inside, and she was asked, "Do you recognize this lady?" The lady replied, "I do not recall her." The beautiful woman said, "I am Flower Girl. Throughout my life, I was not treated as a lowly animal, but was always nurtured. I am now the concubine of Judge Li. The request I made to you yesterday was indeed me. The underworld did not expand the petition, only granting a twelve-year extension. I secretly changed the twelve years to twenty to repay the kindness of being raised. Soon after, Judge Li arrived, and I humbly ask that you present my true name, not the title of 'Lady,' and I earnestly pray for your help." Judge Li hesitated and arrived. The beautiful woman first presented the petition to change the year to Judge Li. Judge Li was about to refuse, but she replied, "I have received much kindness in my life, and I am using this opportunity to report it. It is rare to succeed, but I believe it will not be difficult." Judge Li happily said, "The matter is not easy." Moved by the earnest request, he agreed. As they were about to part, the beautiful woman said to the lady, "Please collect my remains for burial. They are in the northern wall of the street in the Lu Xin Ward, among the refuse." The lady then revived, verified the location, and indeed found the remains. She buried him with the rites of a son. Later, she expressed gratitude in her dreams. Twenty years later, the lady passed away.

A Fox Reciting the *Tongtian Jing* Péi Zhongyuan lived in the northern part of Huxian, and one day, while chasing a rabbit into a large tomb, he saw a fox sitting on a coffin and reading. Zhongyuan tried to catch the fox but failed, and instead, he took the book home. The characters on the book were unreadable. Suddenly, a scholar of the Hu ethnicity appeared and asked to be seen. He introduced himself as Xingzhou, the same fox who had been reading the book on the coffin. Péi asked, "What book is this?" The scholar replied, "It is the *Tongtian Jing*, a text not studied by ordinary people. You truly have no use for it, but I wish to offer a hundred gold pieces to redeem it." Péi refused. The scholar then offered a thousand *yì* (an ancient unit of weight for gold or silver), but Péi still refused. Angered, the scholar waved his robe and rose to leave. Péi’s brother-in-law, Wei Danshi, who had passed away, suddenly appeared and said, "I heard you caught a rabbit and found a book. I can recognize its characters." He then showed the book to Péi. Wei said, "It belongs to the scholar Xingzhou." He then vanished and was no longer seen. Péi also died shortly after.

《玄怪录》 唐 牛僧孺 卷一 ○杜子春 杜子春者，周、隋间人。少落魄，不事家产，然以心气闲纵，嗜酒邪游。资 产荡尽，投于亲故，皆以不事事之故见弃。方冬，衣破腹空，徒行长安中，日晚 未食，彷徨不知所往，于东市西门，饥寒之色可掬，仰天长吁。有一老人策杖于 前，问曰：“君子何叹？”子春言其心，且愤其亲戚疏薄也。感激之气，发于颜 色。老人曰：“几缗则丰用？”子春曰：“三五万则可以活矣。”老人曰：“未 也，更言之。”“十万。”曰：“未也。”乃言：“百万。”曰：“未也。”曰： “三百万。”乃曰：“可矣。”于是袖出一缗，曰：“给子今夕，明日午时俟子 于西市波斯邸，慎无后期。”及时，子春往，老人果与钱三百万，不告姓名而去。

子春既富，荡心复炽。自以为终身不复羁旅也，乘肥衣轻，会酒徒，徵丝竹 歌舞于倡楼，不复以治生为意。一二年间，稍稍而尽。衣服车马，易贵从贱，去 马而驴，去驴而徒，倏忽如初。既而复无计，自叹于市门。发声而老人到，握其 手曰：“君复如此，奇哉！吾将复济子，几缗方可？”子春惭不对，老人因逼之， 子春愧谢而已。老人曰：“明日午时，来前期处。”子春忍愧而往，得钱一千万。 未受之初，愤发以为从此谋生，石季伦、猗顿小竖耳。钱既入手，心又翻然，纵 适之情，又却如故。不三四年间，贫过旧日。

复遇老人于故处，子春不胜其愧， 掩面而走，老人牵裾止之，曰：“嗟乎！拙谋也。”因与三千万，曰：“此而不 痊，则子贫在膏肓矣。”子春曰：“吾落魄邪游，生涯罄尽。亲戚豪族，无相顾 者，独此叟三给我，我何以当之？”因谓老人曰“吾得此，人间之事可以立，孤 孀可以衣食，于名教复圆矣。感叟深惠，立事之后，唯叟所使。”老人曰：“吾 心也。子治生毕，来岁中元，见我于老君双桧下。”子春以孤孀多寓淮南，遂转 资扬州，买良田百顷，郭中起甲第，要路置邸百余间，悉召孤孀分居第中，婚嫁 甥侄，迁祔旅榇，恩者煦之，仇者复之。既毕事，及期而往。

老人者方啸于二桧之阴，遂与登华山云台峰。入四十里余，见一居处，室屋 严洁，非常人居。彩云遥覆，鸾鹤飞翔，其上有正堂，中有药炉，高九尺余，紫 焰光发，灼焕窗户。玉女九人环炉而立，青龙白虎，分据前后。其时日将暮，老 人者不复俗衣，乃黄冠绛帔士也。持白石三丸，酒一卮遗子春，令速食之讫。取 一虎皮铺于内西壁，东向而坐，戒曰：“慎勿语，虽尊神、恶鬼、夜叉、猛兽、 地狱，及君之亲属为所囚缚，万苦皆非真实，但当不动不语耳，安心莫惧，终无 所苦。当一心念吾所言。”言讫而去。子春视庭，唯一巨瓮，满中贮水而已。

道士适去，而旌旗戈甲，千乘万骑，遍满崖谷来，呵叱之声动天，有一人称 大将军，身长丈余，人马皆着金甲，光芒射人。亲卫数百人，拔剑张弓，直入堂 前，呵曰：“汝是何人，敢不避大将军！”左右竦剑而前，逼问姓名，又问作何 物，皆不对。问者大怒，催斩，争射之，声如雷，竟不应。将军者拗怒而去。俄 而猛虎、毒龙、狻猊、狮子、腹蛇万计，哮吼拿攫而争前，欲搏噬，或跳过其上。 子春神色不动。有顷而散。既而大雨滂澍，雷电晦暝，火轮走其左右，电光掣其 前后，目不得开。须臾，庭际水深丈余，流电吼雷，势若山川开破，不可制止， 瞬息之间，波及坐下。子春端坐不顾。未顷而散。

将军者复来，引牛头狱卒，奇 貌鬼神，将大镬汤而置子春前，长枪刃叉，四面周匝，传命曰：“肯言姓名即放， 不肯言，即当心叉取置之镬中。”又不应。因执其妻来，捽于阶下，指曰：“言 姓名免之。”又不应。乃鞭捶流血，或射或斫，或煮或烧，苦不可忍。其妻号哭 曰：“诚为陋拙，有辱君子。然幸得执巾栉，奉事十余年矣，今为尊鬼所执，不 胜其苦。不敢望君匍匐拜乞，望君一言，即全性命矣。人谁无情，君乃忍惜一言。” 雨泪庭中，且咒且骂，子春终不顾。将军曰：“吾不能毒汝妻耶？”令取锉碓， 从脚寸寸坐刂之。妻叫哭愈急，竟不顾之。将军曰：“此贼妖术已成，不可使久 在世间。”敕左右斩之。

斩讫，魂魄被领见阎罗王，王曰：“此乃云台峰妖民乎？”促付狱中，于是 熔铜、铁杖、碓捣、硙磨、火坑、镬汤、刀山、剑林之苦，无不备尝。然心念道 士之言，亦似可忍，竟不呻吟。狱卒告受罪毕，王曰：“此人阴贼，不合得作男 身，宜令作女人。”配生宋州单父县丞王勤家，生而多病，针灸医药之苦，略无 停日。亦尝坠火堕床，痛苦不济，终不失声。俄而长大，容色绝代，而口无声， 其家目为哑女，亲戚相狎，侮之万端，终不能对。

同乡有进士卢珪者，闻者容而 慕之，因媒氏求焉。其家以哑辞之，卢曰：“苟为妻而贤，何用言矣，亦足以戒 长舌之妇。”乃许之。卢生备礼亲迎为妻，数年，恩情甚笃，生一男，仅二岁， 聪慧无敌。卢抱儿与之言，不应。多方引之，终无辞。卢大怒曰：“昔贾大夫之 妻鄙其夫才不笑尔。然观其射雉，尚释其憾。今吾陋不及贾，而文艺非徒射雉也， 而竟不言。大丈夫为妻所鄙，安用其子！”乃持两足，以头扑于石上，应手而卒， 血溅数步。子春爱生于心，忽忘其约，不觉失声云：“噫！”

“噫”声未息，身坐故处，道士者亦在其前，初五更矣。其紫焰穿屋上天， 火起四舍，屋室俱焚。道士叹曰：“措大误余乃如是！”因提其髻投水瓮中。未 顷火息。道士前曰：“出。吾子之心，喜怒哀惧恶欲，皆能忘也。所未臻者，爱 而已。向使子无‘噫’声，吾之药成，子亦上仙矣。嗟乎，仙才之难得也！吾药 可重炼，而子之身犹为世界所容矣。勉之哉！”遥指路使归。子春强登基观焉， 其炉已坏，中有铁柱大如臂，长数尺。道士脱衣，以刀子削之。 子春既归，愧其忘誓，复自效以谢其过，行至云台峰，绝无人迹，叹恨而归。

○裴谌 裴谌、王敬伯、梁芳约为方外之友。隋大业中，相与入白鹿山学道，谓黄白 可成，不死之药可致，云飞羽化，无非积学。辛勤采炼，手足胼胝，十数年间。 无何，梁芳死，敬伯谓谌曰：“吾所以去国忘家，耳绝丝竹，口厌肥豢，目弃奇 色，去华屋而乐茅斋，贱欢娱而贵寂寞者，岂非觊乘云驾鹤，游戏蓬壶？纵其不 成，亦望长生，寿毕天地耳。今仙海无涯，长生未致，辛勤于云山之外，不免就 死。敬伯所乐，将下山乘肥衣轻，听歌玩色，游于京洛，意足然后求达，垂功立 事，以荣耀人寰，纵不能憩三山，饮瑶池，骖龙衣霞，歌鸾飞凤，与仙翁为侣， 且腰金拖紫，图影凌烟，厕卿大夫之间，何如哉？子盍归乎？无空死深山。”谌 曰；“吾乃梦醒者，不复低迷。”敬伯遂归，谌留之不得。时唐贞观初，以旧籍 调授左武卫骑曹参军，大将军赵朏妻之以女。数年间，迁大理廷评，衣绯，奉使 淮南，舟行过高邮。
制使之行，呵叱风生，行船不敢动。时天微雨，忽有一渔舟突过，中有老人， 衣蓑戴笠，鼓棹而去，其疾如风。敬伯以为吾乃制使，威振远近，此渔父敢突过 我。试视之，乃谌也。遽令追之，因请维舟，延之坐内，握手慰之曰：“兄久居 深山，抛掷名宦而无成，到此极也。夫风不可系，影不可捕，古人倦夜长，尚秉 烛游，况少年白昼而掷之乎？敬伯自出山数年，今廷尉评事矣。昨者推狱平允， 乃天锡命服。

淮南疑狱，今氵献于有司，上择详明吏覆讯之，敬伯预其选，故有 是行。虽未可言官达，比之山叟，自谓差胜。兄甘劳苦，竟如曩日，奇哉！奇哉！ 今何所须，当以奉给。”谌曰：“吾侪野人，心近云鹤，未可以腐鼠吓也。吾沉 子浮，鱼鸟各适，何必矜炫也。夫人世之所须者，吾当给尔，子何以赠我？吾与 山中之友，或市药于广陵，亦有息肩之地。青园桥东，有数里樱桃园，园北车门， 即吾宅也。子公事少隙，当寻我于此。”遂倏然而去。

敬伯到广陵十余日，事少闲，思谌言，因出寻之。果有车门，试问之，乃裴 宅也。人引以入，初尚荒凉，移步愈佳。行数百步，方及大门，楼阁重复，花木 鲜秀，似非人境。烟翠葱茏，景色妍媚，不可形状。香风飒来，神清气爽，飘飘 然有凌云之意，不复以使车为重，视其身若腐鼠，视其徒若蝼蚁。既而稍闻剑佩 之声，二青衣出曰：“阿郎来。”俄有一人，衣冠伟然，仪貌奇丽，敬伯前拜， 视之乃谌也。裴慰之曰：“尘界仕官，久食腥膻，愁欲之火焰于心中，负之而行， 固甚劳困。”遂揖以入，坐于中堂，窗户栋梁，饰以录宝，屏帐皆画云鹤。有顷， 四青衣捧碧玉台盘而至，器物珍异，皆非人世所有，香醪嘉馔，目所未窥。既而 日将暮，命其促席，燃九光之灯，光华满座。女乐二十人，皆绝代之色，列坐其 前。

裴顾小黄头曰：“王评事昔吾山中之友，道情不固，弃吾下山，别近十年， 才为廷尉属。今俗心已就，须俗妓以乐之。顾伶家女无足召者，当召士大夫之女 已适人者。如近无姝丽，五千里内皆可择之。”小黄头唯唯而去。诸妓调碧玉筝， 调未谐而黄头已复命，引一妓自西阶登，拜裴席前。裴指曰：“参评事。”敬伯 答拜，细视之，乃敬伯妻赵氏也。敬伯惊讶不敢言，妻亦甚骇，目之不已。遂令 坐玉阶下，一青衣捧玳瑁筝授之，赵素所善也，因令与妓合曲以送酒。

敬伯坐间 取一殷色朱李投之，赵顾敬伯，潜系于衣带。妓奏之曲，赵皆不能逐。裴乃令随 赵所奏，时时停之，以呈其曲。其歌舞虽非云韶九奏之乐，而清沉宛转，酬献极 欢。天将晓，裴召前黄头曰：“送赵氏夫人。”且谓曰：“此堂乃九天画堂，常 人不到。吾昔与王为方外之交，怜其为俗所迷，自投汤火，以智自烧，以明自贼， 将沉浮于生死海中，求岸不得，故命于此，一以醒之。今日之会，诚难再得，亦 夫人之宿命，乃得暂游，云山万重，往复劳苦，无辞也。”赵拜而去。

裴谓敬伯曰：“评公使车留此一宿，得无惊群将乎？宜且就馆，未赴阙闲时， 访我可也。尘路遐远，万愁攻人，努力自爱。”敬伯拜谢而去。后五日，将还， 潜诣取别，其门不复有宅，乃荒凉之地，烟草极目，惆怅而返。 及京奏事毕，得归私第，诸赵竞怒曰：“女子诚陋拙，不足以奉事君子。然 已辱厚礼，亦宜敬之。夫上以承祖先，下以继后事，岂苟而已哉。奈何以妖术致 之万里而娱人之视听乎？朱李尚在，其筵足徵，何讳乎？”敬伯尽言之，且曰： “当此之时，敬伯亦自不测。此盖裴之道成矣，以此相炫也。”其妻亦记得裴言， 遂不复责。

吁！神仙之变化，诚如此乎？将幻者鬻术以致惑乎？固非常智之所及。且夫 雀为蛤，雉为蜃，人为虎，腐草为萤，蜣螂为蝉，鲲为鹏，万物之变化，书传之 记者，不可以智达，况耳目之外乎！ ○韦氏 京兆韦氏女者，既笄二年，母告之曰：“有秀才裴爽者，欲聘汝。”女笑曰： “非吾夫也。”母记之，虽媒媪日来，盛陈裴之才，其家甚慕之，然终不谐。又 一年，母曰：“有王悟者，前参京兆军事，其府之司录张审约者，汝之老舅也， 为王媒之，将聘汝矣。”女亦曰：“非也。”母又曰：“张亦熟我，又为王之媒 介也，其辞不虚矣。”亦终不谐。

又二年，进士张楚金求之。母以告之，女笑曰：“吾之夫乃此人也。”母许 之，遂择吉焉。既成礼讫，因其母徐问之，对曰：“吾此乃梦徵矣。然此生之事 皆见矣，岂独适楚金之先知乎！某既笄，梦年二十适清河楚金，以尚书节制广陵， 在镇七年，而楚金伏法。阖门皆死，惟某与新妇一人，生入掖庭，蔬食而役者十 八年，蒙诏放出。自午承命，日暮方出宫关，与新妇渡水，迨暗及滩，四顾将昏 然，不知所往，因与新妇相于滩于掩泣，相勉曰：‘此不可久立，宜速渡。’遂 南行。及岸数百步，有坏坊焉。

自入西门，随垣而北，其东大门屋，因造焉，又 无人而大开，遂入。及坏戟门，亦开，又入。逾屏回廊四合，有堂既扃。阶前有 四大樱桃树林，花发正茂。及月色满庭，似无人居，不知所告。因与新妇对卧阶 下。未几，有老人来诟逐，告以前情，遂去。又闻西廊步必履之声，有一少年郎 来诟，且呼老人令遂之。苦告之，少年郎低首而走。徐乃白衫素履，哭拜阶下曰： ‘某尚书之侄也。’乃恸哭曰：‘无处问耗，不知阿母与阿嫂至，乃自天降也。 此即旧宅，堂中所锁，无非旧物。’恸哭开户，宛如故居之地，居之九年前从化 （本句疑有脱误）。”其母大奇之。且人之荣悴，无非前定，素闻之矣，岂梦中 之信，又如此乎？乃心记之。

俄而楚金授钺广陵，神龙中以徐敬业有兴复之谋，连坐伏法，惟妻与妇□死， 配役掖庭十八年，则天因降诞日，大纵籍役者，得□例焉。午后受诏，及行，总 监绯阉走留食，候之。食毕，实将暮矣。其褰裳涉水而哭，及宅所在，无差梦焉。 噫！梦信徵也，则前所叙扶风公之见，又何以偕焉。 ○元无有 宝应中，有元无有，尝以仲春末独行维扬郊野。值日晚，风雨大至。时兵荒 后，人户逃窜，入路旁空庄。须臾霁止。斜月自出。无有憩北轩，忽闻西廊有人 行声，未几至堂中。有四人，衣冠皆异，相与谈谐，吟咏甚畅，乃云：“今夕如 秋，风月如此，吾党岂不为文，以纪平生之事？”其文即曰口号联句也。吟咏既 朗，无有听之甚悉。其一衣冠长人曰：

“齐纨鲁缟如霜雪，寥亮高声为子发。” 其二黑衣冠短陋人曰： “嘉宾良会清夜时，辉煌灯烛我能持。” 其三故弊黄衣冠人，亦短陋，诗曰： “清冷之泉俟朝汲，桑绠相牵常出入。” 其四黑衣冠，身亦短陋，诗曰： “爨薪贮水常煎熬，充他口腹我为劳。” 无有亦不以四人为异，四人亦不虞无有之在堂隍也，递相褒赏，羡其自负， 虽阮嗣宗《咏怀》亦不能加耳。四人迟明方归旧所，无有就寻之，堂中惟有故杵、 烛台、水桶、破铛，乃知四人即此物所为也。 ○郭代公 代国公郭元振，开元中下第，自晋之汾，夜行阴晦失道。久而绝远有灯火之 光，以为人居也，迳往投之。八九里有宅，门宇甚峻。既入门，廊下及堂下灯烛 辉煌，牢馔罗列，若嫁女之家，而悄无人。公系马西廊前，历阶而升，徘徊堂上， 不知其何处也。俄闻堂中东阁有女子哭声，呜咽不已。

公问曰：“堂中泣者，人 耶，鬼耶？何陈设如此，无人而独泣？”曰：“妾此乡之祠有乌将军者，能祸福 人，每岁求偶于乡人，乡人必择处女之美者而嫁焉。妾虽陋拙，父利乡人之五百 缗，潜以应选。今夕，乡人之女并为游宴者，到是，醉妾此室，共锁而去，以适 于将军者也。今父母弃之就死，而令惴惴哀惧。君诚人耶，能相救免，毕身为扫 除之妇，以奉指使。”公愤曰：“其来当何时？”曰：“二更。”公曰：“吾忝 为大丈夫也，必力救之。如不得，当杀身以徇汝，终不使汝枉死于淫鬼之手也。” 女泣少止，于是坐于西阶上，移其马于堂北，令一仆侍立于前，若为宾而待之。

未几，火光照耀，车马骈阗，二紫衣吏入而复出，曰：“相公在此。”逡巡， 二黄衣吏入而出，亦曰：“相公在此。”公私心独喜：“吾当为宰相，必胜此鬼 矣。”既而将军渐下，导吏复告之。将军曰：“入。”有戈剑弓矢翼引以入，即 东阶下，公使仆前曰：“郭秀才见。”遂行揖。将军曰：“秀才安得到此？”曰： “闻将军今夕嘉礼，愿为小相耳。”将军者喜而延坐，与对食，言笑极欢。公于 囊中有利刀，思取刺之，乃问曰：“将军曾食鹿腊乎？”曰：“此地难遇。

”公 曰：“某有少须珍者，得自御厨，愿削以献。”将军者大悦。公乃起，取鹿腊并 小刀，因削之，置一小器，令自取。将军喜，引手取之，不疑其他。公伺其无机， 乃投其脯，捉其腕而断之。将军失声而走，导从之吏，一时惊散。公执其手，脱 衣缠之，令仆夫出望之，寂无所见，乃启门谓泣者曰：“将军之腕已在于此矣。 寻其血踪，死亦不久。汝既获免，可出就食。”泣者乃出，年可十七八，而甚佳 丽，拜于公前，曰：“誓为仆妾。”公勉谕焉。天方曙，开视其手，则猪蹄也。
俄闻哭泣之声渐近，乃女之父母兄弟及乡中耆老，相与舁榇而来，将收其尸 以备殡殓。见公及女，乃生人也。咸惊以问之，公具告焉。乡老共怒残其神，曰： “乌将军，此乡镇神，乡人奉之久矣，岁配以女，才无他虞。此礼少迟，即风雨 雷雹为虐。奈何失路之客，而伤我明神，致暴于人，此乡何负？当杀公以祭乌将 军，不尔，亦缚送本县。”挥少年将令执公，公谕之曰：“尔徒老于年，未老于 事。

我天下之达理者，尔众听吾言。夫神，承天而为镇也，不若诸侯受命于天子 而疆理天下乎？”曰：“然。”公曰：“使诸侯渔色于中国，天子不怒乎？残虐 于人，天子不伐乎？诚使尔呼将军者，真神明也，神固无猪蹄，天岂使淫妖之兽 乎？且淫妖之兽，天地之罪畜也，吾执正以诛之，岂不可乎！尔曹无正人，使尔 少女年年横死于妖畜，积罪动天。安知天不使吾雪焉？从吾言，当为尔除之，永 无聘礼之患，如何？”乡人悟而喜曰：“愿从公命。”

乃令数百人，执弓矢刀枪锹之属，环而自随，寻血而行。才二十里，血入 大冢穴中。因围而属刂之，应手渐大如瓮口，公令束薪燃火投入照之。其中若大 室，见一大猪，无前左蹄，血卧其地，突烟走出，毙于围中。 乡人翻共相庆，会钱以酬公。公不受，曰：“吾为人除害，非鬻猎者。”得 免之女辞其父母亲族曰：“多幸为人，托质血属，闺闱未出，固无可杀之罪。今 者贪钱五十万，以嫁妖兽，忍锁而去，岂人所宜！若非郭公之仁勇，宁有今日？ 是妾死于父母而生于郭公也。请从郭公，不复以旧乡为念矣。”泣拜而从公，公 多歧援谕，止之不获，遂纳为侧室，生子数人。

公之贵也，皆任大官之位。事已前定，虽生远地，而至于鬼神终不能害，明 矣。 ○来君绰 隋炀帝征辽，十二军尽没，总管来护坐法受戮，炀帝尽欲诛其诸子。君绰忧 惧连诛，因与秀才罗巡、罗逖、李万进结为奔走之友，共亡命至海州。 夜黑迷路，路旁有灯火，因与共投之。扣门数下，有一苍头迎拜君绰，君绰 因问：“此是谁家？”答曰：“科斗郎君，姓威，即当府秀才也。”遂启门，又 自闭，敲中门，曰：“蜗儿，外有四五个客。”蜗儿即又一苍头也。遂开门，秉 烛引客就馆客位，床榻茵褥甚备。俄有二小童持烛自中门出，曰：“六郎子出来。” 君绰等降阶见主人。主人辞彩朗然，文辩纷错，自通姓名曰“威污蠖”。叙寒温 讫，揖客由阼阶，坐曰：“污蠖忝以本州乡赋，得与足下同声，清宵良会，殊是 所愿。”即命酒合坐。渐至酣畅，谈谑交至，众所不能对。

君绰颇不能平，欲以 理挫之，无计，因举觞曰：“君绰请起一令，以坐中姓名双声者，犯罚如律。” 君绰曰：“威污蠖。”实讥其姓。众皆抚手大笑，以为得言。及至污蠖，改令曰： “以坐中人姓为歌声，自二字至五字。”令曰：“罗李，罗来李，罗李罗来，罗 李罗李来。”众皆惭其辩捷。罗巡又问：“君风雅之士，足得自比云龙，何玉名 之自贬子耶？”污蠖曰：“仆久从宾贡，多为主司见屈。以仆后于群士，何异尺 蠖于污池乎？”巡又问：“公华宗，氏族何为不载？”污蠖曰：“我本田氏，出 于齐威王，亦犹桓丁之类，何足下之不学耶？”既而蜗儿举方丈盘至，珍羞水陆， 充溢其间。君绰及仆者无不饱饫。夜阑彻烛，连榻而寝。迟明叙别，恨恨俱不自 胜。

君绰等行数里，犹念污蠖，复来，见昨所会之处，了无人居，唯污池，池边 有大螾，长数尺。又有蜗螺丁子，皆大常者数倍，方知污蠖及二竖皆此物也。 遂共恶昨宵所食，各吐出青泥及污水数升。 卷二 ○崔环 安平崔环者，司戎郎宣之子。元和五年夏五月，遇疾于荥阳别业。忽见黄衫 吏二人，执帖来追，遂行数百步，入城。城中有街两畔，官林相对，绝无人家， 直北数里到门，题曰“判官院”。见二吏迤逦向北，亦有林木，袴靴秣头，佩刀 头，执弓矢者，散立者，各数百人。同到之人数千，或杻，或系，或缚，或囊 盛耳头，或连其项，或衣服俨然，或簪裙济济，各有惧色，或泣或叹。其黄衫人 一留伴环，一入告。

俄闻决人四下声，既而告者出曰：“判官传语：何故不抚幼 小，不务成家，广破庄园，但恣酒色！又虑尔小累无掌，且为宽恕，轻杖放归， 宜即洗心，勿复贰过。若踵前非，固无容舍。”乃敕伴者令送归。环曰：“判官 谓谁？”曰：“司戎郎也。”环泣曰：“弃背多年，号天莫及。幸蒙追到，慈颜 不遥，乞一拜见，死且无恨。”二吏曰：“明晦各殊，去留有隔，不合见也。” 环曰：“向者传语云已见责。此身不入，何以受刑？”吏曰：“入则不得归矣。 凡人有三魂，一魂在家，二魂受杖耳。不信，看郎胫合有杖痕。”遂褰衣自视， 其两胫各有杖痕四，痛苦不济，匍匐而行，举足甚艰。同到之人，叹羡之声，喧 于歧路。

南行百余步，街东有大林。二吏前曰：“某等日夜事判官，为日虽久，幽冥 小吏，例不免贫。各有许惠资财，竟无暇取，不因送郎阴路，无因得往求之。请 即暂止林下，某等偕去，俄顷即来。诸处皆是恶鬼曹司，不合往，乞郎不移足相 待。”言讫各去，久而不来。环闷，试诣街西行，一署门题曰“人矿院”，门亦 甚净。环素有胆，且恃其父为判官，身又蒙放，遂入其中。过屏障，见一大石， 周回数里。有一军将坐于石北厅上，据案而坐，铺人各绕石及石上，有数十大鬼， 形貌不同，以大铁椎椎人为矿石。东有杻械枷锁者数千人，悲啼恐惧，不可名 状。点名拽来，投来石上，遂椎之，既碎，唱其名。军将判之，一吏于案后读之 云：“傅某狱讫。”鬼亦捧云。其中有傅硙狱者，付火狱者，傅汤狱者。

环直逼 石前看之，军将指之云：“曹司法严，不合妄入，彼是何人，敢来闲看！”人吏 竞来传问，环恃不对。军将怒曰：“看既无端，问又不对，傍观岂如身试之审乎？” 敕一吏拽来锻之。环一魂尚立，见其石上别有一身，被拽扑卧石上，大锤锤之， 痛苦之极，实不可忍。须臾，骨肉皆碎，仅欲成泥。二吏者走来，槌胸曰：“郎 君，再三乞不闲行，何故来此？”遂告军将曰：“此是判官郎君，阳禄未终，追 来却放，暂来入者。无间地狱，入不须臾。遂道如斯。何计得令复旧？”军将者 亦惧曰：“初问不言，忿而处置，如何？”因问诸鬼曰：“何计得令复旧？”皆 曰：“唯濮阳霞一人耳。”曰；“远近？”曰：“去此万里。昨者北海王与化形 出游，为海人所愪。其王请出，今亦未回。”乃令一鬼召之。

有顷而到，乃一髯眇目翁也，应急而来，喘犹未定。军将指环曰：“何计？” 霞曰：“易耳。”遂解衣缠腰，取怀中药末，糁于矿上团扑，一翻一糁，扁槎其 矿为头顶及身手足，剜刻五脏，通为肠胃，雕为九窍，逡巡成形，以手承其项曰： “起！”遂起来，与立合为一，遂能行。大为二吏所贵。相与复南行。将去，濮 阳霞抚肩曰：“措大，人矿中搜得活，然而去不许一钱。”环许钱三十万。霞笑 曰：“老吏身忙，当使小鬼枭儿往取，见即分付。”

行及城门，见一吏南走，曰：“黄河欲分一枝，前者天令三丁取一，计功不 计，今请二丁取一。”二吏以私行有矿环之过，恐宣之怒环而召也，谓环曰： “彼见若问，但言欲观地狱之法，以为儆戒，故在此耳。”吏见果问，环答之如 言。遂别去复行。 须臾，至荥阳，二吏曰：“还生必矣。某将有所取，能一观乎？”环曰： “固所愿也。”共入县郭，到一人家中堂，一吏以怀中绳系床上女人头，尽力拽 之，一吏以豹皮囊徐收其气，气尽乃拽下，皆缚之。同送环家，入门，二吏大呼 曰：“崔环！”误筑门扇，遂寤。其家泣候之，已七日矣。后数日，有枭鸣于庭， 环曰：“濮阳翁之子来矣。”遂令家人刻纸钱焚之，乃去。疾平，潜寻所见妇人 家，乃县纠郭霈妻也。其时尚未有分河之议，后数日，河中节度使司徒薛公平议 奏分河一枝，冀减冲城之势。初奏三丁取一，既虑不足，复奏二丁役一，竟如环 阴司所见也。

○柳归舜 吴兴柳归舜，隋开皇二十年自江南抵巴陵，大风吹至君山下。因维舟登岸， 寻小径，不觉行四五里，兴酣，逾越溪涧，不由径路。忽道傍有一大石，表里洞 彻，圆而砥平，周匝六七亩。其外尽生翠竹，圆大如盎，高百余尺，叶曳白云， 森罗映天，清风徐吹，戛为丝竹音。石中央又生一树，高百尺，条干偃阴为五色。 翠叶如盘，花径尺余，色深碧，叶深红，异香成烟，箸物霏霏。 有鹦鹉数千，丹嘴翠衣，尾长二三尺，翱翔其间，相呼姓字，音旨清越。有 名“武游郎”者，有名“阿苏儿”者，有名“武仙郎”者，有名“自在先生”者， 有名“踏莲露”者，有名“凤花台”者，有名“戴蝉儿”者，有名“多花子”者。 或有唱歌者曰：“吾此曲是汉武钩弋夫人常所唱。”词曰： “戴蝉儿，分明传与君王语。

建章殿里未得归，朱箔金缸双凤舞。” 名阿苏儿者曰：“我忆阿娇深宫下泪，唱曰：‘昔请司马相如为作《长门赋》， 徒使费百金，君王终不顾。’”又有诵司马相如《大人赋》者曰：“吾初学赋时， 为赵昭仪抽七宝钗横鞭，余痛不彻。今日诵得，还是终身一艺。”名武游郎者言： “余昔见汉武帝乘郁金楫，泛积翠池，自吹紫玉笛，音韵朗畅，帝意欢适。李夫 人歌以随，歌曰：‘顾鄙贱、奉恩私。愿吾君，万岁期。’” 又名武仙郎者问归舜曰：“君何姓氏行第？”归舜曰：“姓柳，第十二。” 曰：“柳十二自何许来？”归舜曰：“吾将至巴陵，遭风泊舟，兴酣至此耳。” 武仙郎曰：“柳十二官人，偶因遭风，得臻异境，此所谓因病致妍耳。然下官禽 鸟，不能致力生人，为足下转达桂家三十娘子。”因遥呼曰：“阿春，此间有客。” 即有紫云数片，自西南飞来。去地丈余，云气渐散，遂见珠楼翠幕，重槛飞楹， 周匝石际。

一青衣自户出，年始十三四，身衣珠翠，颜甚姝美，谓归舜曰：“三 十娘子使阿春传语郎君：贫居僻远，劳此检校。不知朝来食否？请垂略坐，以具 蔬馔。”即有捧水精床出者。归舜再让而坐。阿春因呼凤花台鸟：“何不看客？ 三十娘子以黄郎不在，不敢接对郎君。汝若等闲，似前度受捶。”有一鹦鹉即飞 至曰：“吾乃凤花台也。近有一篇，君能听乎？”归舜曰：“平生所好，实契所 愿。”凤花台乃曰：“吾昨过蓬莱玉楼，因有一章。诗曰：

露接朝阳生，海波翻水晶。 玉楼瞰寥廓，天地相照明。 此时下栖止，投迹依旧楹。 顾余复何忝，日侍群仙行。 归舜曰：“丽则丽矣。足下师乃谁人？”凤花台曰：“仆在王丹左右一千余 岁，杜兰香教我真箓，东方朔授我秘诀。汉武帝求太中大夫，遂在石渠署见扬雄、 王褒等赋颂，始晓箴论。王莽之乱，方得还吴。后为朱然所得，转遗陆逊。复见 机、云制作，方学缀篇什。机、云被戮，便至于此。殊不知近日谁为宗匠？”归 舜曰：“薛道衡、江总也。”因诵数篇示之。凤花台曰：“近代非不靡丽，殊少 骨气。”俄而阿春捧赤玉盘，珍羞万品，目所不识，甘香裂鼻。

饮食讫，忽有二道士自空飞下，顾见归舜曰：“大难得！与鹦鹉相对。君非 柳十二乎？君船以风便，索君甚急，何不急回？”因投一尺绮曰：“以此掩眼， 即去矣。”归舜从之，忽如身飞，却坠巴陵。达舟所，舟人欲发。问之，失归舜 已三日矣。后却至此，泊舟寻访，不复再见也。 ○崔书生 开元天宝中，有崔书生者，于东周逻谷口居，好植花竹，乃于户外别莳名花， 春暮之时，英蕊芬郁，远闻百步。书生每晨必盥漱独看。忽见一女郎自西乘马东 行，青衣老少数人随后。女郎有殊色，所乘马骏。崔生未及细视，而女郎已过矣。 明日又过，崔生于花下先致酒茗樽杓，铺陈茵席，乃迎马首曰：“某以性好花木， 此园无非手植。今香茂似堪流盼。伏见女郎频自过此，计仆驭当疲，敢具箪醪， 希垂憩息。”女郎不顾而过。其后青衣曰：“但具酒馔，何忧不至。”女郎顾叱 曰：“何故轻与人言！”言讫遂去。

崔生明日又于山下别致醪酒，俟俟女郎至，崔生乃鞭马随之，到别墅之前， 又下马拜请。良久，一老青衣谓女郎曰：“车马甚疲，暂歇无伤。”因自控女郎 马至堂寝下，老青衣谓崔生曰：“君既未婚，予为媒妁可乎？”崔生大悦，再拜 跪，请不相忘。老青衣曰：“事即必定，后十五日大吉辰，君于此时，但具婚礼 所要，并于此备酒馔。小娘子阿姊在逻谷中，有微疾，故小娘子日往看省。某去， 便当咨启，至期则皆至此矣。”于是促行。崔生在后，即依言营备吉日所要。至 期，女郎及姊皆到。其姊亦仪质极丽。遂以女郎归于崔生。

崔生母在旧居，殊不知崔生纳室。崔生以不告而娶，但启聘媵。母见女郎， 女郎悉归之礼甚具。经月余日，忽有一人送食于女郎，甘香特异。后崔生觉母慈 颜衰瘁，因伏问几下，母曰：“吾有汝一子，冀得永寿。今汝所纳新妇，妖美无 双。吾于士塑图书之中，未尝识此，必恐是狐媚之辈，伤害于汝，遂致吾忧。” 崔生入室见女郎，女郎涕泪交下，曰：“本待箕帚，便望终天，不知尊夫人待以 狐媚辈，明晨即便请行，相爱今宵耳。”崔生掩泪不能言。

明日，女郎车骑至，女郎乘马，崔生从送之，入逻谷三十余里，山间有川， 川中异香珍果，不可胜纪。馆于屋室，侈于王者。青衣百许，迎拜女郎曰：“小 娘子，无行崔生，何必将来！”于是捧入，留崔生于门外。未几，一青衣传女郎 姊言曰：“崔生遗行，使太夫人疑阻，事宜便绝，不合相见。然小妹曾奉周旋， 亦当奉屈。”俄而召崔生入，责诮再三，辞辩清婉，崔生但拜伏受谴而已。遂坐 于中寝对食，食讫，命酒，召女乐洽饮，铿锵万变。乐阙，其姊谓女郎曰：“须 令崔郎却回，汝有何物赠送？”女郎遂出白玉合子遗崔生，崔生亦自留别。于是 各呜咽而出。行至逻谷，回望千岩万壑，无径路，自恸哭归家。常见玉合子，郁 郁不乐。
忽有胡僧扣门求食，崔生出见，胡僧曰：“君有至宝，乞相示也。”崔生曰： “某贫士，何有见请？”僧曰：“君岂不有异人奉赠，贫道望气知之。”崔生因 出合子示胡僧，僧起拜请曰：“请以百万市之。”遂将去。崔生问僧曰：“女郎 是谁？”曰：“君所纳妻，王母第三个女，玉卮娘子也。姊亦负美名在仙都，况 复人间。所惜君娶之不得久远。倘住一年，君举家必仙矣。”崔生叹怨迨卒。

○曹惠 武德初，有曹惠者，制授江州参军。官舍有佛堂，堂中有二木偶人，长尺余， 雕饰甚巧，丹青剥落。惠因持归与稚儿。后稚儿方食饼，木偶即引手请之。儿惊 报惠，惠笑曰：“取木偶来。”即言曰：“轻红、轻素自有名，何呼木偶！”于 是转盼驰走，悉无异人。 惠问曰：“汝何时来物，颇能作怪？”轻素曰：“某与轻红是宣城太守谢家 俑偶，当时天下工巧，总不及沈隐侯家老苍头孝忠也。轻素、轻红即孝忠所造也。 隐侯哀宣城无辜，葬日故有此赠。时轻素在圹中，方持汤与乐家娘子濯足，闻外 有持兵称敕声，娘子畏惧，跣足化为白蝼，少顷，二贼执炬至，尽掠财物，谢郎 时颔瑟瑟环，亦为贼敲颐脱之。贼人照见轻红等，曰：‘二明器不恶，可与小儿 为戏具。’遂持出，时天正二年也。自尔流落数家，陈末麦铁杖犹子咬头将至此， 以到今日。*?

被萦治试唬骸霸悰判恍鞮撬魍蹙丛蚺巠籧麑五嵩评旨夷餇子？”轻 素曰：“王氏乃生前之妻，乐家乃冥婚耳。王氏本屠酤种，性粗率多力，至冥中 犹与宣城琴瑟不睦，何宣城颜严，则磔石抵关以为威胁。宣城自密启于天帝，帝 许逐之。二女一男，悉随母归矣。遂再娶乐彦辅第八娘子，美资质，善书，好弹 琴，尤与殷东阳仲文、谢荆州晦夫人相得，日恣追寻。宣城尝云：‘我才方古词 人，唯不及东阿耳。其余文士，皆吾机中之肉，可以宰割矣。’见为南曹典铨郎， 与潘典门同列，乘肥衣轻，贵于生前百倍。然十日一朝晋、宋、梁，可以为劳， 近闻亦已停矣。”

惠又问曰：“汝二人灵异若此，吾欲舍汝，何如？”即皆喜曰：“以轻素等 变化，虽无不可，君意如不放，终不能逃。庐山山神欲索轻素作舞姬久矣，今此 奉辞，便当受彼荣富。然君能终恩，请命画工，便赐粉黛。”即令工人为图之， 使被锦绣。轻素喜笑曰：“此度非论舞姬，亦当彼夫人。无以奉酬，请以微言留 别。百代之中，但有他人会者，无不为忠臣居大位矣。言曰：‘鸡角入骨，紫鹤 吃黄角甲（疑此处有脱误，“黄角甲”，《广记》作“黄鼠申”，“申”字或属 下读），不害五通泉室，为六代吉昌。’”言讫而灭。 后有人祷庐山神，女巫云：“神君新纳一夫人，要翠花钗簪，汝宜求之，当 降大福。”祷者求而焚之，遂如愿焉。惠亦不能知其微言，访之时贤皆不识，或 云：中书令岑文本识其三句，亦不为人说云。

○滕庭俊 文明元年，毗陵掾滕庭俊患热病积年，每发身如火烧，热数日方定。召医， 医不能治。后之洛调选，行至荥阳西十四五里，天向暮，未达前所。遂投一道旁 庄家，主人暂出未至，庭俊心无聊赖，自叹吟曰：“为客多苦辛，日暮无主人。” 即有老父，须发甚秃，衣服亦弊，自堂西出而曰：“老父虽无所解，然性好文章， 适不知郎君来，正与和且耶联句次，闻郎君吟‘为客多苦辛，日暮无主人’，虽 曹丕‘客子常畏人’不能过也。老父与和且耶同作浑家门客，门客虽贫，亦有斗 酒接郎君清话耳。”庭俊甚异之，问：“老父居止何所？”老父曰：“仆忝浑家 扫门之客，姓麻，名束禾，第大，君何不呼为麻大。”庭俊即谢不敏，与之偕行， 绕堂西隅，遂见一门，门启，华堂复阁甚绮秀，馆中有樽酒盘杓。麻大揖庭俊同 坐。

良久，门中一客出，麻大曰：“和至矣。”庭俊即降阶相让，还坐，且耶谓 麻大曰：“适与君联句，诗头来未？”麻大自书题目曰：“同在浑平原门联句一 首。予已为四句矣。”麻大诗曰： 自与慎终邻，馨香遂满身。 无关好清净，又用去灰尘。 且耶良久乃曰：“仆是七言，韵又不同，如何？”麻大曰：“但自为一章， 亦不恶。”于是且耶即吟曰： 冬日每去依烟火，春至还归养子孙。 曾向苻王笔端坐，迩来求食浑家门。 庭俊犹未悟，见其馆华盛，因有淹留歇马之计，乃书四言云：

田文称好客，凡养几多人？ 如使冯驩在，今希厕下宾。 且耶、麻大笑曰：“何得相讥？向使君得在浑家，一日自当足矣。”于是餐 膳肴馔，引满数十巡。主人至，觅庭俊不见，使人叫唤之，庭俊应曰：“唯。” 而馆宇并麻、和二人一时不见，身在厕屋下，傍有大苍蝇、秃帚而已。庭俊先有 热疾，自此后顿愈，不复更发矣。 ○顾总 梁天监元年，顾总为县吏，数被鞭捶，尝郁郁愤怀，因逃墟墓之间，彷徨惆 怅，不知所适。忽有二黄衣见顾总曰：“刘君，颇忆畴昔周旋否？”总惊曰： “弊宗乃顾氏，先未曾面清颜，何有周旋之问？”二人曰：“仆二人，王粲、徐 幹也。足下生前是刘桢，为坤明侍中，以纳赂金谪为小吏，公今当不知矣。然公 言辞历历，犹有记室音旨。”因出袖中五轴书示总曰：“此君集也，当谛视之。” 总试省览，乃了然明悟，便觉藻思泉涌。

其集人多有本，惟卒后数篇记得。诗一章，题目曰《从驾游幽丽宫却忆平生 西园文会因寄修文府正郎蔡伯喈》，诗曰： 在汉绝纲纪，溟渎多腾湍。 煌煌魏世祖，拯溺静波澜。 天纪已垂定，邦人亦保完。 大开相公府，掇拾尽幽兰。 始从众君子，日侍贤主欢。 文皇在春宫，烝孝逾问安。 监抚多余闲，园圃恣游观。 末臣戴簪笔，翊圣从和鸾。 月出行殿凉，珍木清露溥。 天文信辉丽，铿锵振琅玕。 被命仰为和，顾征成所难。 弱质不自持，危脆朽萎残。 岂意十余年，陵寝梧楸寒。 今朝坤明国，再顾簪蝉冠。 侍游于离宫，高蹑浮云端。 却忆西园时，生死暂悲酸。

君昔汉公卿，未央冠群贤。 倘若念平生，览此同怆然。 其余七篇，传者失本。 王粲谓总曰：“吾本短小，无何取乐进女，女似其父，短小尤甚。自别君后， 改娶刘荆州女。寻生一子，荆州与名似翁奴，今年十八，长七尺三寸，所恨未得 参丈人也。当渠年十一，与余同览镜，余谓之曰：‘汝首魁梧于余。’渠立应余 曰：‘防风骨节专车，不如白起头小而锐。’余又谓曰：‘汝长大当为将。’又 应余曰：‘仲尼三尺童子，羞言霸道。况某承大人严训，敢措意于相斫道乎？’ 余知其了了过人矣。不知足下生来有郎娘否？”良久沉思，稍如相识，因曰： “二君子既是总友人，何计可脱小吏之厄？”徐幹曰：“君但执前集，诉于县宰， 则脱矣。”总又问：“坤明是何国？”幹曰：“魏开国邺地也。公昔为开国侍中， 何遽忘也？”公在坤明国家累悉无恙，贤小娘子娇羞娘，有一篇奉忆，昨者已诵 似丈人矣，诗曰：

忆爷抛女不归家，不作侍中为小吏。 就辛苦，弃荣华，愿爷相念早相见， 与儿买李市甘瓜。 诵讫，总不觉涕泪交下，为一章寄娇羞娘子： 忆儿貌，念儿心，望儿不见泪沾襟。 时殊世异难相见，弃谢此生当访寻。 既而王粲、徐幹与总殷勤叙别。 乃携《刘桢集》五卷，并具陈见王粲、徐幹之状，仍说前生是刘桢。县宰因 见桢卒后诗，大惊曰：“不可使刘公干为小吏。”即解遣，以宾礼待之。后不知 总所在，集亦寻失矣。时人勖子弟皆曰：“死刘桢犹庇得生顾总，可不进修哉！”

○周静帝居延部落主 周静帝初，居延部落主勃都骨低凌暴，奢逸好乐，居处甚盛。忽有人数十至 门，一人先投剌曰：“省名部落主成多受。”因趋入。骨低问曰：“何故省名部 落？”多受曰：“某等数人各殊，名字皆不别造。有姓马者，姓皮者，姓鹿者， 姓熊者，姓獐者，姓卫者，姓班者，然皆名受。唯某帅名多受耳。”骨低曰： “君等悉似伶官，有何所解？”多受曰：“晓弄碗珠。性不爱俗，言皆经义。” 骨低大喜曰：“目所未睹。”有一优即前曰：“某等肚饥，臈臈怡怡，皮漫 绕身三匝。主人食若不充，开口终当不舍。”骨低悦，更命加食。一人曰：“某 请弄大小相成，终始相生。”于是长人吞短人，肥人吞瘦人，相吞残两人。长者 又曰：“请作终始相生耳。”于是吐下一人，吐者又吐一人。递相吐出，人数复 足。骨低甚惊，因重赐赍遣之。
明日又至，戏弄如初。连翩半月，骨低颇烦，不能设食。诸伶皆怒曰：“主 人当以某等为幻术，请借郎君娘子试之。”于是持骨低儿女弟妹甥侄妻妾等吞之 于腹中。腹中皆啼呼请命，骨低惶怖，降阶顿首，哀乞亲属。伶者皆笑曰：“此 无伤，不足忧。”即吐出之，亲属完全如初。
